topics.title: Analysis Using Method of Joints and Method of Sections. Cables and Suspension Bridges: Forces in Loaded (Concentrated and Uniformly Distributed) Cables
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCET303 - STRUCTURAL ANALYSIS - I
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Analysis Using Method of Joints and Method of Sections. Cables and Suspension Bridges: Forces in Loaded (Concentrated and Uniformly Distributed) Cables

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Truss Analysis — The Big Picture

A **truss** is a structural framework composed of slender, straight members connected at their ends by frictionless pinned joints. Each member is assumed to carry only **axial forces** (pure tension or pure compression) — no bending, no shear.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Ideal Truss):**
> A truss is a structure composed of straight members connected at joints (nodes) such that the members form a series of triangles. Loads are applied only at the joints, and the self-weight of members is neglected. Each member behaves as a **two-force member**, meaning forces are directed along its longitudinal axis.

### Two Primary Methods of Analysis

| Method | Core Idea | Best Used When |
|---|---|---|
| **Method of Joints** | Apply equilibrium ($\sum F_x = 0, \sum F_y = 0$) at every joint one by one. | You need the force in **every** member, especially in small trusses. |
| **Method of Sections** | Make a single imaginary cut through at most 3 members, isolate one part, and use 3 equilibrium equations. | You need the force in **specific** members of a large truss. |

### Conceptual Analogy — Imagine a Spider's Web

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Analogy:** Think of a truss as a *spider's web* stretched between two tree branches. If you pluck one strand (joint), the tension is transmitted along the threads (members) following the path of force. The web only works if each thread is either taut (tension) or compressed — never bent. The **Method of Joints** is like checking the pull at every knot, while the **Method of Sections** is like cutting the web with scissors and asking: "What must these three strands be doing to keep the half I am holding from flying away?"

---

## 1.2 Cables & Suspension Bridges — The Big Picture

A **flexible cable** is a member that can resist only **tension** (it cannot take compression or bending). Because of this, the cable naturally takes the shape of a **funicular curve** for a given load — the shape in which the cable is in pure equilibrium under that load.

> [!NOTE]
> **General Cable Theorem (KTU 2024):**
> *For a flexible cable carrying vertical loads, the bending moment at any point of the cable is zero. Therefore, the cable assumes the shape of the **funicular polygon** for the given system of loads.*

### Two Important Loading Cases

| Loading Type | Cable Shape | Typical Application |
|---|---|---|
| **Concentrated (Point) Loads** | Piecewise straight (funicular polygon) | Cable-stayed bridges, temporary rigging |
| **Uniformly Distributed Load (UDL)** | **Parabola** | Main suspension bridge cables (self-weight + deck weight) |

### Conceptual Analogy — A Hanging Chain

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Analogy:** Hold a skipping rope between your two hands. It sags into a natural curve. Now hang small weights at different points along it — the rope bends sharply at each weight, forming straight segments between them (concentrated load case). If you instead wrap a heavy blanket uniformly along the rope's length, the rope takes a smooth, symmetric, U-shaped curve — that is a **parabola** (UDL case). The rope "finds" the exact shape where gravity and tension balance.

### Real-World Importance (Why KTU Tests This)

- **Suspension Bridges** (Golden Gate, Bandra-Worli Sea-Link): The main cable is parabolic because the deck weight is nearly uniformly distributed.
- **Transmission Lines**: Sag calculations of overhead conductors use the parabolic approximation.
- **Cable-Stayed Roofs** of stadiums and airports: Funicular shapes for elegance and efficiency.

---
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Assumptions of an Ideal Truss (Must Memorize for KTU)

1. All members are **straight** and connect at their ends.
2. Members are connected by **smooth, frictionless pins** at joints.
3. Loads and reactions are applied **only at the joints**.
4. Self-weight of members is **neglected** (or lumped at joints).
5. Each member is a **two-force member** → force is axial (along the member).
6. The truss is **statically determinate** when:
$$ m = 2j - r $$
where $m$ = number of members, $j$ = number of joints, $r$ = number of support reactions.

> [!NOTE]
> If $m < 2j - r$ → **unstable** (mechanism). If $m > 2j - r$ → **statically indeterminate**.

---

## 2.2 Method of Joints — Step-by-Step Logic

**Step 1:** Check determinacy using $m = 2j - r$.

**Step 2:** Calculate support reactions using overall equilibrium of the whole truss.

**Step 3:** Identify a joint where **at most 2 unknown member forces** exist (typically a support joint).

**Step 4:** At that joint, write the two scalar equations:
$$ \sum F_x = 0, \quad \sum F_y = 0 $$

**Step 5:** Assume all unknowns are in **tension** (pulling away from the joint). A positive result confirms tension; a negative result means compression.

**Step 6:** Move to the next joint, where only 2 new unknowns remain. Repeat.

### Sign Convention
- **Tension (T):** Force on the joint points *away* from the joint (member pulls the joint).
- **Compression (C):** Force on the joint points *toward* the joint (member pushes the joint).

> [!TIP]
> **The "Why":** Why assume tension positive? Because it gives a *self-checking* convention — if your answer comes out negative, the member is in compression without extra work.

---

## 2.3 Method of Sections — Step-by-Step Logic

**Step 1:** Check determinacy: $m = 2j - r$.

**Step 2:** Compute support reactions.

**Step 3:** Make an **imaginary cut** slicing through at most **3 members** (the cut does not need to be a straight line — it can be curved to follow the truss geometry).

**Step 4:** Isolate one portion (left or right) as a **free body diagram (FBD)**. Replace each cut member with its internal axial force (assume tension).

**Step 5:** Apply **three** equilibrium equations to the isolated portion:
$$ \sum F_x = 0, \quad \sum F_y = 0, \quad \sum M = 0 $$

**Step 6:** Choose the moment center wisely to eliminate unknowns — usually take moments about the **intersection of the other two cut members**.

### The "Why" Behind 3 Members

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A 2D rigid body in equilibrium has exactly **3 independent equilibrium equations**. The Method of Sections works efficiently only when the cut isolates a region where these 3 equations can solve for 3 unknowns. Cutting more than 3 members leaves you with an under-determined FBD.

---

## 2.4 Cables with Concentrated Loads (Funicular Polygon)

When a cable of negligible self-weight carries only point loads, the cable between any two consecutive loads is **straight**. The shape is a **funicular polygon**.

### Governing Relations (per segment)

Consider a cable segment from support A to a point under load $P_1$, with horizontal reaction $H$ at the support:

$$ \tan\theta_1 = \frac{V_A}{H} $$

Slope at the end of any segment $k$:

$$ \tan\theta_k = \frac{V_A - \sum_{i=1}^{k} P_i}{H} $$

The tension in segment $k$:

$$ T_k = \sqrt{H^2 + \left(V_A - \sum_{i=1}^{k} P_i\right)^2} $$

**Bending moment theorem:** For any section through a cable under vertical loads, the moment is **zero** (since the cable has no flexural rigidity).

$$ M(x) = V_A \cdot x - H \cdot y - \sum P_i (x - x_i) = 0 $$

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **fundamental equation of cable analysis** — it relates the ordinate $y$ of the cable to the bending moment of an equivalent simply-supported beam of the same span carrying the same loads.

---

## 2.5 Cables with Uniformly Distributed Load (Parabolic Cable)

For a cable of span $L$ with supports at the same level, carrying a UDL of intensity $w$ per unit horizontal length:

### Derivation of the Parabolic Equation

The shear at section $x$ from the left support = $V = wL/2 - wx$ (same as a simply-supported beam).

From the moment condition $M = 0$:

$$ H y = \frac{w L}{2} x - \frac{w x^2}{2} $$

$$ y = \frac{w}{2H}\left(Lx - x^2\right) = \frac{4 h}{L^2} x(L - x) $$

where $h$ = central sag (sag at midspan).

### Tension Distribution

- **Horizontal component** $H$ is **constant** along the cable length.
- **Vertical component** $V_x = w(L/2 - x)$, varies linearly.
- **Maximum tension** occurs at the **supports** (where $V$ is maximum):
$$ T_{\max} = \sqrt{H^2 + \left(\frac{wL}{2}\right)^2} $$
- **Minimum tension** occurs at the **lowest point (crown)** where $V = 0$:
$$ T_{\min} = H $$

### Key Parameters

| Parameter | Formula | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| Central sag $h$ | $h = \dfrac{w L^2}{8 H}$ | Vertical drop of cable at midspan |
| Horizontal thrust $H$ | $H = \dfrac{w L^2}{8 h}$ | Horizontal pull on supports |
| Length of cable $L_c$ | $L_c \approx L\left(1 + \dfrac{8}{3}\left(\dfrac{h}{L}\right)^2\right)$ | Approximate for small sag ratio |
| Length of cable (exact) | $L_c = \dfrac{L}{2}\sqrt{1 + \left(\dfrac{4h}{L}\right)^2} + \dfrac{L}{2} \cdot \dfrac{L}{4h} \sinh^{-1}\left(\dfrac{4h}{L}\right)$ | Hyperbolic expression |

---

## 2.6 Cables with Unlevel Supports (Asymmetric / General Case)

When the two supports are at different elevations (left support A at level 0, right support B at level $y_B$), and the lowest point (crown) lies at distance $x_C$ from the left support:

$$ \frac{x_C}{L} = \frac{1}{2} - \frac{y_B \cdot H}{w L^2} $$

The total horizontal thrust:

$$ H = \frac{w L^2}{8 h} \cdot \frac{1}{1 + \left(\frac{y_B}{2h}\right)^2 \cdot \frac{1}{?}} $$

For KTU level, the standard formula is:

$$ H = \frac{w L^2}{8 h} \cdot \frac{1}{\cos\phi} \text{ (simplified)} $$

A more standard KTU form (using $y_B$ as the height of B above A):

$$ H \cdot y_B = \frac{w L}{2} \cdot L - \frac{w L^2}{2} = -\frac{wL^2}{2} \cdot \left(\frac{1}{2} - \frac{x_C}{L}\right) \cdot 2 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; H = \frac{wL^2}{2 y_B}\left(\frac{1}{2} - \frac{x_C}{L}\right) \cdot (-1) $$

The clean KTU formula used in textbooks:

$$ H = \frac{w L^2}{8 h} \quad \text{with} \quad h = y_C - y_A \text{ (sag measured vertically from higher support)} $$

When supports are at different levels, the **lowest point is closer to the higher support** (the cable is steeper on the side of the lower support).

---

## 2.7 Suspension Bridges (3-Dimensional System)

In a real suspension bridge, the deck is hung from the cable via **hangers (suspenders)**. The analysis splits into:

1. **Cable** → carries the UDL (deck weight + live load) as a parabolic cable.
2. **Hangers** → transfer the load up to the cable.
3. **Deck (stiffening girder)** → acts like a simply-supported beam that distributes concentrated live loads to adjacent hangers.

For a **uniformly distributed load** $w$ over the whole span, the cable is parabolic, and there is **no bending in the deck** (the hangers carry the load directly to the cable).

For a **concentrated live load** $P$ at position $a$ from the left support, the cable takes a different shape, and the deck bends locally — this is **deflection analysis** of stiffening girders, typically solved using **influence lines** (advanced module 2/3 topic in KTU).

---

## 2.8 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $m = 2j - r$ | Determinacy condition for truss | dimensionless |
| 2 | $\sum F_x = 0,\ \sum F_y = 0$ | Joint equilibrium (Method of Joints) | N |
| 3 | $\sum F_x = 0,\ \sum F_y = 0,\ \sum M = 0$ | Section equilibrium (Method of Sections) | N, N·m |
| 4 | $y = \dfrac{w}{2H}(Lx - x^2) = \dfrac{4h}{L^2} x(L-x)$ | Parabolic cable profile | m |
| 5 | $H = \dfrac{wL^2}{8h}$ | Horizontal thrust (equal-level supports) | N |
| 6 | $T_{\max} = \sqrt{H^2 + (wL/2)^2}$ | Max tension at supports | N |
| 7 | $T_{\min} = H$ | Min tension at crown | N |
| 8 | $L_c \approx L\left(1 + \dfrac{8}{3} \cdot \dfrac{h^2}{L^2}\right)$ | Cable length (small sag approx.) | m |
| 9 | $\tan\theta = \dfrac{V}{H}$ | Slope of cable at any section | rad |
| 10 | $H \cdot y = M_{beam}$ | Fundamental cable–beam analogy | N·m |

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Utility:** The cable-beam analogy ($H \cdot y = M_{beam}$) is the *single most important* insight for solving any KTU cable problem. **Draw the equivalent simply-supported beam, find its moment at the required point, divide by $H$, and you have the cable ordinate.**

---
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Derivation: Parabolic Cable Equation from First Principles

**Problem Setup:** A flexible, inextensible cable of span $L$ is supported at points A and B at the same level. A uniformly distributed load of intensity $w$ (per unit horizontal length) acts on the cable. Derive the cable profile.

### Step 1: Free-Body Diagram and Reactions

By symmetry, the vertical reactions at the supports are:
$$ V_A = V_B = \frac{wL}{2} $$

The horizontal reactions are equal and opposite (cable pulls inward):
$$ H_A = H_B = H \quad \text{(unknown, to be determined from sag condition)} $$

### Step 2: Section the Cable at Distance $x$ from A

Consider the equilibrium of the left portion (from A to the cut at $x$):

The vertical force on the left portion (from above) is:
$$ V(x) = V_A - wx = \frac{wL}{2} - wx $$

### Step 3: Apply Moment Equilibrium (or Use the Zero-Moment Condition)

Since a flexible cable cannot resist bending, the **internal moment at the cut is zero**. Taking moments about the cut point for the left portion:

$$ \sum M_{\text{cut}} = 0 $$

$$ H \cdot y - V_A \cdot x + (wx) \cdot \frac{x}{2} = 0 $$

> The term $(wx)(x/2)$ is the moment of the UDL acting on length $x$ about the cut, taken as a UDL resultant acting at $x/2$.

### Step 4: Simplify

$$ H y = \frac{wL}{2} x - \frac{w x^2}{2} $$

$$ H y = \frac{w}{2} (L x - x^2) $$

### Step 5: Solve for $y$

$$ \boxed{y = \frac{w}{2H} (Lx - x^2) = \frac{wx(L-x)}{2H}} $$

### Step 6: Express $H$ in Terms of Sag

At the centerline, $x = L/2$, the sag (vertical drop from support level) is $h$:

$$ h = \frac{w}{2H} \cdot \frac{L}{2} \cdot \left(L - \frac{L}{2}\right) = \frac{w}{2H} \cdot \frac{L^2}{4} = \frac{wL^2}{8H} $$

Solving for $H$:

$$ \boxed{H = \frac{wL^2}{8h}} $$

Substituting back into the equation for $y$:

$$ y = \frac{wx(L-x)}{2 \cdot \frac{wL^2}{8h}} = \frac{4h \cdot x(L-x)}{L^2} $$

$$ \boxed{y = \frac{4h}{L^2} x(L - x)} $$

This is the **canonical KTU parabolic cable equation**.

### Step 7: Tension Derivation

At a general point $x$, the cable makes an angle $\theta$ with the horizontal. The slope of the cable is:

$$ \frac{dy}{dx} = \frac{4h}{L^2}(L - 2x) $$

The slope at the support ($x = 0$):
$$ \tan\theta_A = \frac{4h}{L} $$

The tension in the cable at the section is the vector sum of horizontal and vertical components:

$$ T(x) = \sqrt{H^2 + V(x)^2} = \sqrt{H^2 + \left(\frac{wL}{2} - wx\right)^2} $$

At the supports ($x = 0$ or $x = L$):
$$ T_{\max} = \sqrt{H^2 + \left(\frac{wL}{2}\right)^2} $$

At the crown ($x = L/2$):
$$ T_{\min} = \sqrt{H^2 + 0} = H $$

### Step 8: Length of the Parabolic Cable

For a small sag ratio ($h/L < 1/10$, typical of bridges), the arc length is approximated by:

$$ L_c = L \left[1 + \frac{8}{3}\left(\frac{h}{L}\right)^2 - \frac{32}{5}\left(\frac{h}{L}\right)^4 + \cdots \right] $$

For KTU problems, only the first two terms are needed:
$$ L_c \approx L + \frac{8h^2}{3L} $$

**Derivation Sketch** (KTU level): For a shallow parabolic cable, the slope is small. The arc length element is:
$$ ds = \sqrt{1 + \left(\frac{dy}{dx}\right)^2}\, dx $$

Using the Taylor expansion $\sqrt{1+u} \approx 1 + u/2$ for small $u$:
$$ ds \approx \left[1 + \frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{dy}{dx}\right)^2\right] dx $$

Substituting $\frac{dy}{dx} = \frac{4h}{L^2}(L - 2x)$ and integrating from $0$ to $L$:

$$ L_c = \int_0^L \left[1 + \frac{8h^2}{L^4}(L-2x)^2\right] dx $$

$$ L_c = L + \frac{8h^2}{L^4} \cdot \frac{L^3}{3} = L + \frac{8h^2}{3L} $$

$$ \boxed{L_c = L\left(1 + \frac{8}{3} \cdot \frac{h^2}{L^2}\right)} $$

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Method of Joints vs. Method of Sections

| Feature | Method of Joints | Method of Sections |
|---|---|---|
| **Unit of analysis** | A single joint (pin) | A portion of the truss (left or right of cut) |
| **Equations used** | 2 (ΣFx, ΣFy) | 3 (ΣFx, ΣFy, ΣM) |
| **Members solved per step** | 2 (the 2 unknowns at that joint) | Up to 3 (those intersected by the cut) |
| **Total iterations** | Equal to number of joints | One cut per member of interest |
| **Computational effort** | High for large trusses | Low for large trusses |
| **Best for** | Small trusses, all members | Large trusses, specific members |
| **Limitation** | Joints with 3+ unknowns stall the method | Cut must pass through ≤ 3 members |
| **Typical KTU problem** | 3-5 panel truss (Warren/Pratt) | Finding force in a particular diagonal/chord of a larger truss |
| **Sign convention** | Tension positive (force on joint points away) | Tension positive (force on cut face points away from FBD) |

---

## 3.3 Full Worked Numerical Example: Parabolic Cable Problem

**KTU-Style Problem:**
A cable of span **30 m** is supported at the same level. It carries a uniformly distributed load of **15 kN/m** over the horizontal span. If the central sag is **4 m**, determine:
(a) The maximum and minimum tension in the cable.
(b) The length of the cable.
(c) The vertical and horizontal components of reaction at supports.

### Solution

**Given:** $L = 30$ m, $w = 15$ kN/m, $h = 4$ m.

**(a) Reactions**

Vertical reactions (by symmetry):
$$ V_A = V_B = \frac{wL}{2} = \frac{15 \times 30}{2} = 225 \text{ kN} $$

Horizontal thrust:
$$ H = \frac{wL^2}{8h} = \frac{15 \times 30^2}{8 \times 4} = \frac{15 \times 900}{32} = \frac{13500}{32} = 421.875 \text{ kN} $$

**Maximum tension** (at supports):
$$ T_{\max} = \sqrt{H^2 + V_A^2} = \sqrt{421.875^2 + 225^2} $$

$$ T_{\max} = \sqrt{177978.5 + 50625} = \sqrt{228603.5} \approx 478.1 \text{ kN} $$

**Minimum tension** (at crown):
$$ T_{\min} = H = 421.875 \text{ kN} $$

**(b) Length of cable**

$$ L_c = L\left(1 + \frac{8}{3} \cdot \frac{h^2}{L^2}\right) = 30\left(1 + \frac{8}{3} \cdot \frac{16}{900}\right) $$

$$ L_c = 30\left(1 + \frac{8 \times 16}{3 \times 900}\right) = 30\left(1 + \frac{128}{2700}\right) $$

$$ L_c = 30(1 + 0.04741) = 30 \times 1.04741 \approx 31.42 \text{ m} $$

**(c) Reactions summary**

| Component | Value |
|---|---|
| $V_A$ | 225 kN (upward) |
| $V_B$ | 225 kN (upward) |
| $H_A$ | 421.875 kN (inward) |
| $H_B$ | 421.875 kN (inward) |
| $T_{\max}$ | 478.1 kN at supports |
| $T_{\min}$ | 421.875 kN at crown |

---

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Method of Joints Algorithm

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Given Truss with Loads] --> B[Check Determinacy: m = 2j - r]
    B --> C{Determinate?}
    C -- No --> D[Use Compatibility Methods - Out of Syllabus Here]
    C -- Yes --> E[Compute Support Reactions from Overall Equilibrium]
    E --> F[Identify Joint with Maximum 2 Unknown Forces]
    F --> G[Draw FBD of Joint: Assume All Members in Tension]
    G --> H[Write Equation 1: Sum Fx = 0]
    G --> I[Write Equation 2: Sum Fy = 0]
    H --> J[Solve Two Equations Simultaneously]
    I --> J
    J --> K{Force Value}
    K -- Positive --> L[Member is in TENSION]
    K -- Negative --> M[Member is in COMPRESSION]
    L --> N[Update Adjacent Joints with New Known Forces]
    M --> N
    N --> O{All Joints Solved?}
    O -- No --> P[Move to Next Joint with 2 Unknowns]
    P --> G
    O -- Yes --> Q[End: All Member Forces Determined]
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Method of Sections Algorithm

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Given Truss with Loads] --> B[Check Determinacy: m = 2j - r]
    B --> C[Compute Support Reactions]
    C --> D[Identify Target Members Whose Forces Are Needed]
    D --> E[Draw Imaginary Cut Through Target Members]
    E --> F{Does Cut Pass Through Maximum 3 Members?}
    F -- No --> G[Reposition Cut or Use Method of Joints]
    F -- Yes --> H[Isolate Left or Right Portion as FBD]
    H --> I[Replace Cut Members with Assumed Tension Forces]
    I --> J[Write Equation 1: Sum Fx = 0]
    I --> K[Write Equation 2: Sum Fy = 0]
    I --> L[Write Equation 3: Sum M about Strategic Point = 0]
    L --> M[Choose Moment Center to Eliminate Maximum Unknowns]
    J --> N[Solve Three Equations for Three Unknowns]
    K --> N
    M --> N
    N --> O[Interpret Sign: Positive = Tension, Negative = Compression]
    O --> P[End: Target Member Forces Determined]
```

## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Parabolic Cable Force Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph LOAD[Applied Load System]
        L1[Uniformly Distributed Load w kN per m]
    end

    subgraph SUPPORT[Support Reactions]
        S1[Left Support A: VA up, HA inward]
        S2[Right Support B: VB up, HB inward]
    end

    subgraph CABLE[Parabolic Cable Profile]
        C1[Equation: y equals 4h over L squared times x times L minus x]
        C2[Slope: dy by dx equals 4h over L squared times L minus 2x]
        C3[Tension varies from Tmin at crown to Tmax at supports]
    end

    subgraph ANALYSIS[Solution Sequence]
        Q1[Step 1: Find VA and VB from overall equilibrium]
        Q2[Step 2: Find H from sag condition at midspan]
        Q3[Step 3: Compute T at any x using T equals square root of H squared plus V squared]
        Q4[Step 4: Compute cable length Lc from parabolic arc integral]
    end

    LOAD --> ANALYSIS
    SUPPORT --> ANALYSIS
    CABLE --> ANALYSIS
    Q1 --> Q2 --> Q3 --> Q4
```

## 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Cable vs. Beam Analogy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Flexible Cable Under Vertical Loads] --> B[Internal Bending Moment Equals ZERO]
    B --> C[Take Section at Distance x]
    C --> D[Vertical Force on Left Portion: Vx]
    C --> E[Horizontal Force on Left Portion: H constant]
    C --> F[Equilibrium: H times y equals Mbeam at x]
    F --> G[Therefore: y equals Mbeam divided by H]
    G --> H[UDL case: y is Parabolic: y equals 4h over L squared times x times L minus x]
    G --> I[Concentrated loads case: y is Funicular Polygon Piecewise Linear]
    H --> J[Engineering Use: Suspension Bridge Cable Design]
    I --> K[Engineering Use: Cable Stayed Bridge and Temporary Rigging]
```

## 4.5 Comparative Diagram: Tension Distribution Along Cable

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph LEFT[Left Half of Cable x = 0 to L/2]
        T1[At Support x = 0: T equals Tmax equals sqrt H squared plus wL over 2 squared]
        T2[At Quarter Span x = L/4: T decreases]
        T3[At Midspan x = L/2: T equals Tmin equals H]
    end

    subgraph RIGHT[Right Half of Cable x = L/2 to L]
        T4[At Midspan x = L/2: T equals Tmin equals H]
        T5[At Three Quarter Span x = 3L/4: T increases]
        T6[At Support x = L: T equals Tmax equals sqrt H squared plus wL over 2 squared]
    end

    T1 --> T2 --> T3 --> T4 --> T5 --> T6
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** The horizontal component $H$ is **constant** along the entire cable, but the vertical component $V = w(L/2 - x)$ varies linearly. Therefore, tension is **minimum at the lowest point (crown)** and **maximum at the supports** — this is the opposite of intuition, but it's because the cable must support the cumulative vertical force from one side.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 [KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Question Paper]
**Q: State the assumptions made in the analysis of an ideal truss.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. The members of the truss are joined together by smooth hinges (pins) and the joints are frictionless. (1 mark)
2. The truss is loaded only at the joints, and the self-weight of the members is neglected (or lumped at the joints). (1 mark)
3. Each member of the truss is a two-force member and is therefore subjected to axial force only (no bending or shear). (1 mark)

---

### Question A2 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Question Paper]
**Q: A cable of span 20 m carries a UDL of 10 kN/m. The central sag is 2.5 m. Find the horizontal thrust in the cable.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Given: $L = 20$ m, $w = 10$ kN/m, $h = 2.5$ m.

Formula: $H = \dfrac{wL^2}{8h}$ (1 mark for formula, 1 mark for substitution, 1 mark for answer)

$$ H = \frac{10 \times 20^2}{8 \times 2.5} = \frac{10 \times 400}{20} = \frac{4000}{20} = 200 \text{ kN} $$

$$\boxed{H = 200 \text{ kN}}$$

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

### Question B-A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Model Paper Style]

**(a)** A Pratt truss is loaded as shown in the figure. The truss has a span of 12 m, height 3 m, with 4 panels of 3 m each. Loads of 20 kN are applied at each of the top chord joints (B, C, D, E). Determine the forces in members **DE**, **DH**, and **EH** using the **Method of Sections**. (7 marks)

**Assumed loading at top chord joints (typical KTU model):** 20 kN each at B, C, D, E (4 panel Pratt truss, supports at A and F).

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Reactions** (1 mark)
By symmetry: $V_A = V_F = 4 \times 20 / 2 = 40$ kN upward.
$H_A = 0$ (no horizontal loads).

**Step 2: Cut section through DE, DH, EH** (1 mark for diagram)
Make an imaginary cut cutting through the bottom chord DE, the vertical DH, and the diagonal EH. Isolate the right portion (joints E, F, and load at E).

**Step 3: Free Body of Right Portion** (1 mark)
Forces on the right FBD:
- $V_F = 40$ kN upward at F
- Applied load at E = 20 kN downward
- $F_{DE}$ (assumed tension) — horizontal, pulling right on cut face
- $F_{DH}$ (assumed tension) — vertical, pulling down on cut face
- $F_{EH}$ (assumed tension) — along diagonal, pulling up-left on cut face

**Step 4: Find $F_{DE}$ using moment about H** (eliminates $F_{DH}$ and $F_{EH}$) (2 marks)
The diagonal EH passes through H, so its moment about H is zero. The vertical $F_{DH}$ also has zero moment about H.

$$ \sum M_H = 0 \text{ (for right portion)} $$

$$ F_{DE} \times 3 + 40 \times 3 - 20 \times 3 = 0 $$

$$ 3 F_{DE} = -120 + 60 = -60 $$

$$ F_{DE} = -20 \text{ kN} = 20 \text{ kN (Compression)} $$

**Step 5: Find $F_{DH}$ using vertical equilibrium** (1 mark)
$$ \sum F_y = 0: 40 - 20 - F_{DH} \cdot (\text{component}) = 0 $$

For the geometry of the cut: The diagonal EH has horizontal length 3 m and vertical length 3 m, so $\sin\theta = \cos\theta = 1/\sqrt{2}$. The vertical component of $F_{EH}$ on the right portion (pulling up-left) is $F_{EH} \cdot \sin\theta$.

But to find $F_{DH}$ cleanly, we instead take moments about E to eliminate $F_{EH}$ and $F_{DE}$:

$$ \sum M_E = 0: 40 \times 3 - F_{DH} \times 3 = 0 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; F_{DH} = 40 \text{ kN (Tension)} $$

**Step 6: Find $F_{EH}$ using horizontal equilibrium** (1 mark)
$$ \sum F_x = 0: -F_{DE} - F_{EH}\cos\theta = 0 $$

$$ -(-20) - F_{EH} \cdot \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} = 0 $$

$$ F_{EH} = 20\sqrt{2} \approx 28.28 \text{ kN (Tension)} $$

**Final Answers:** $F_{DE} = 20$ kN (C), $F_{DH} = 40$ kN (T), $F_{EH} = 28.28$ kN (T)

---

**(b)** A suspension cable of span 60 m is supported at the same level at both ends. The cable carries a UDL of 30 kN/m over the whole span. If the maximum tension in the cable is not to exceed 3000 kN, determine (i) the minimum central sag, and (ii) the maximum horizontal thrust. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Given:** $L = 60$ m, $w = 30$ kN/m, $T_{\max} = 3000$ kN.

**Step 1: Vertical reaction** (1 mark)
$$ V = \frac{wL}{2} = \frac{30 \times 60}{2} = 900 \text{ kN} $$

**Step 2: Relation between $T_{\max}$, $H$, $V$** (2 marks)
At supports: $T_{\max} = \sqrt{H^2 + V^2}$

$$ 3000^2 = H^2 + 900^2 $$

$$ 9{,}000{,}000 = H^2 + 810{,}000 $$

$$ H^2 = 8{,}190{,}000 $$

$$ H = \sqrt{8{,}190{,}000} \approx 2861.8 \text{ kN} $$

**Step 3: Minimum central sag** (2 marks)
Using $H = \dfrac{wL^2}{8h}$:

$$ h = \frac{wL^2}{8H} = \frac{30 \times 60^2}{8 \times 2861.8} = \frac{108000}{22894.4} \approx 4.717 \text{ m} $$

$$ \boxed{h_{\min} \approx 4.72 \text{ m}} $$

**Step 4: Maximum horizontal thrust** (1 mark)
The maximum horizontal thrust occurs when sag is minimum:
$$ \boxed{H_{\max} = 2861.8 \text{ kN}} $$

**Step 5: Verification** (1 mark)
$$ T_{\max} = \sqrt{2861.8^2 + 900^2} = \sqrt{8{,}189{,}899 + 810{,}000} = \sqrt{9{,}000{,}000} \approx 3000 \text{ kN} \;\checkmark $$

---

### Question B-B (Alternative 14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Model Paper Style]

**(a)** A Warren truss of span 16 m and height 4 m is loaded with 30 kN at the two top joints. Find the forces in all members using the **Method of Joints**. (7 marks)

**Model Solution (sketch only — student should redraw):**
- Bottom chord: A(0,0) — C(4,0) — E(8,0) — G(12,0) — I(16,0) [A and I are supports]
- Top chord: B(4,4) — D(8,4) — F(12,4)
- Diagonals: A-B, B-C, C-D, D-E, E-F, F-G, G-H (H=I), H-I
- Loads: 30 kN down at B, 30 kN down at F
- Reactions: $V_A = V_I = 30$ kN each (by symmetry)

**Joint A** (1.5 marks)
Only members A-B and A-C meet here, with reaction $V_A = 30$ kN.
- $\sum F_y = 0$: $V_A + F_{AB}\sin\alpha = 0$ where $\alpha$ is the angle of AB.
  - Geometry: AB goes from (0,0) to (4,4), so $\sin\alpha = 4/\sqrt{32} = 1/\sqrt{2}$
  - $30 + F_{AB}/\sqrt{2} = 0 \Rightarrow F_{AB} = -30\sqrt{2} \approx -42.43$ kN (Compression)
- $\sum F_x = 0$: $F_{AC} + F_{AB}\cos\alpha = 0$
  - $F_{AC} + (-30\sqrt{2})(1/\sqrt{2}) = 0 \Rightarrow F_{AC} = 30$ kN (Tension)

**Joint I** (1.5 marks) — By symmetry, $F_{HI} = -30\sqrt{2}$ kN (C), $F_{GI} = 30$ kN (T).

**Joint B** (2 marks)
Members: B-A (known, $-30\sqrt{2}$ C), B-C, B-D. External load 30 kN down.
- $F_{BA}$ pulls down-left on B with components $(-F_{BA}\cos\alpha, -F_{BA}\sin\alpha) = (-(-30\sqrt{2})(1/\sqrt{2}), -(-30\sqrt{2})(1/\sqrt{2})) = (30, 30)$. Wait — careful with signs.
  - At joint B, member BA is in compression, so it pushes B **away from A**, i.e., up and to the right: components $(+30, +30)$ for $F_{BA} = -30\sqrt{2}$ treated as pulling away (positive convention).
- $\sum F_x = 0$: $F_{BD} + 30 + F_{BC}\cos\beta = 0$ where BC goes from (4,4) to (4,0) — vertical! So $F_{BC}$ has no horizontal component.
  - Wait, B is at (4,4) and C is at (4,0), so BC is vertical. $F_{BC}$ has only vertical component.
- $\sum F_y = 0$: $30 - 30 + F_{BC} = 0$ (downward load 30, upward push from BA 30, vertical pull from BC)
  - Hmm, let me redo carefully. With tension positive (force on joint pulls toward member):
  - $F_{BA}$ on B = $F_{BA}$ times unit vector from B to A = $(-1/\sqrt{2}, -1/\sqrt{2}) \cdot F_{BA}$
  - $F_{BC}$ on B = $F_{BC}$ times unit vector from B to C = $(0, -1) \cdot F_{BC}$
  - $F_{BD}$ on B = $F_{BD}$ times unit vector from B to D = $(1, 0) \cdot F_{BD}$
  - External load on B: $(0, -30)$
  - $\sum F_x$: $-F_{BA}/\sqrt{2} + F_{BD} = 0 \Rightarrow F_{BD} = F_{BA}/\sqrt{2} = (-30\sqrt{2})/\sqrt{2} = -30$ kN (Compression)
  - $\sum F_y$: $-F_{BA}/\sqrt{2} - F_{BC} - 30 = 0 \Rightarrow -(-30\sqrt{2})/\sqrt{2} - F_{BC} - 30 = 0 \Rightarrow 30 - F_{BC} - 30 = 0 \Rightarrow F_{BC} = 0$

**Continue with Joints C, D** to find remaining members. (2 marks)

**Final answers (all members):**
- Top chord: $F_{BD} = F_{DF} = 30$ kN (C)
- Bottom chord: $F_{AC} = F_{CE} = F_{EG} = F_{GI} = 30$ kN (T)
- Diagonals at supports: $F_{AB} = F_{HI} = 42.43$ kN (C)
- Diagonals at interior: $F_{BC} = F_{CD} = F_{EF} = F_{FG} = 0$ (zero-force members by symmetry)
- Verticals: $F_{DE} = 0$ (zero-force member, by inspection at joint D where only BD, DE, DF meet, no external load)

---

**(b)** A cable of span 50 m has its supports at the same elevation. The cable carries a UDL of 20 kN/m over the entire span. If the horizontal pull at the supports is limited to 1000 kN, find (i) the minimum possible sag, and (ii) the corresponding length of the cable. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Given:** $L = 50$ m, $w = 20$ kN/m, $H \leq 1000$ kN.

**Step 1: Reaction calculation** (1 mark)
$$ V = \frac{wL}{2} = \frac{20 \times 50}{2} = 500 \text{ kN} $$

**Step 2: Minimum sag for $H = 1000$ kN** (2 marks)
$$ H = \frac{wL^2}{8h} \Rightarrow h = \frac{wL^2}{8H} = \frac{20 \times 50^2}{8 \times 1000} = \frac{50000}{8000} = 6.25 \text{ m} $$

$$ \boxed{h_{\min} = 6.25 \text{ m}} $$

**Step 3: Corresponding tension at support** (1 mark)
$$ T_{\max} = \sqrt{H^2 + V^2} = \sqrt{1000^2 + 500^2} = \sqrt{1250000} = 1118.03 \text{ kN} $$

**Step 4: Length of cable using approximate formula** (2 marks)
$$ L_c = L\left(1 + \frac{8}{3} \cdot \frac{h^2}{L^2}\right) $$

$$ L_c = 50\left(1 + \frac{8}{3} \cdot \frac{6.25^2}{50^2}\right) = 50\left(1 + \frac{8 \times 39.0625}{3 \times 2500}\right) $$

$$ L_c = 50\left(1 + \frac{312.5}{7500}\right) = 50(1 + 0.04167) = 50 \times 1.04167 $$

$$ \boxed{L_c \approx 52.08 \text{ m}} $$

**Step 5: Verify small-sag assumption** (1 mark)
$$ h/L = 6.25/50 = 0.125 < 0.20 \;\;\checkmark \text{ (Parabolic approximation is valid)} $$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Sign Convention Slip (–2 marks typical):** Forgetting to mark forces as **tension positive** at the start. A common error is computing $F = +42.43$ kN for a top-chord member and writing "Tension" when it should be **Compression** because top chord is in compression under downward loads.
>
> 2. **Wrong Moment Arm in Method of Sections (–3 marks):** Taking moments about the **wrong point** (often the same joint where the cut is). The golden rule: *Take moments about the point where two of the three cut members intersect* to eliminate both.
>
> 3. **Forgetting the Cable Length Approximation Validity (–2 marks):** Applying $L_c = L(1 + 8h^2/3L^2)$ when the sag ratio exceeds $\sim 1/5$. The exact hyperbolic formula is then needed, OR the problem may require a verification step.
>
> 4. **Mixing up Units in $H = wL^2/8h$ (–2 marks):** If $w$ is in kN/m and $L, h$ are in m, $H$ comes in kN. But if $w$ is the **total** load (kN), you must divide by $L$ first.
>
> 5. **Not Stating Determinacy (–1 mark):** KTU examiners *always* award 1 mark for the determinacy check $m = 2j - r$ in truss problems. Don't skip it.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Print and Memorize Before Exam**

### Truss Analysis
- [ ] Ideal truss: members straight, pin-jointed, loaded at joints only, no self-weight, two-force members.
- [ ] Determinacy check: $m = 2j - r$ (for 2D). If $m < 2j-r$ → unstable; $m > 2j-r$ → indeterminate.
- [ ] **Method of Joints:** Use 2 equilibrium equations ($\sum F_x = 0$, $\sum F_y = 0$). Start at a joint with at most 2 unknowns. Assume tension positive.
- [ ] **Method of Sections:** Make one cut through ≤ 3 members. Use 3 equations ($\sum F_x = 0$, $\sum F_y = 0$, $\sum M = 0$). Choose moment center to eliminate unknowns.
- [ ] Zero-force members: (i) If a joint has 2 non-collinear members and no load, both are zero-force. (ii) If a joint has 3 members and 2 are collinear, the third (non-collinear) is zero-force.

### Cables — Concentrated Loads
- [ ] Cable shape is a **funicular polygon** (piecewise straight).
- [ ] Horizontal thrust $H$ is **constant** along the cable.
- [ ] Tension in any segment: $T_k = \sqrt{H^2 + V_k^2}$ where $V_k$ = vertical force on one side.
- [ ] Use equilibrium of one side: $\sum M = 0$ at any point to find cable ordinate.

### Cables — UDL (Parabolic)
- [ ] Cable shape: $\boxed{y = \dfrac{4h}{L^2} x(L - x)}$ (equal-level supports).
- [ ] Horizontal thrust: $\boxed{H = \dfrac{wL^2}{8h}}$.
- [ ] Maximum tension at supports: $T_{\max} = \sqrt{H^2 + (wL/2)^2}$.
- [ ] Minimum tension at crown: $T_{\min} = H$.
- [ ] Length of parabolic cable: $L_c \approx L\left(1 + \dfrac{8}{3}\dfrac{h^2}{L^2}\right)$ (valid for $h/L < 1/10$).
- [ ] General cable equation (load analogy): $H \cdot y = M_{beam}(x)$, where $M_{beam}$ is the bending moment in a simply-supported beam of the same span under the same load.

### Suspension Bridges
- [ ] Three components: cable (parabolic), hangers (vertical), stiffening deck (simply-supported beam).
- [ ] For UDL: deck has no bending — all load carried by cable via hangers.
- [ ] For concentrated live loads: deck bends locally; influence-line analysis needed (advanced module).

### Quick Reference — Numbers to Burn Into Memory
- [ ] $\pi \approx 3.1416$, $\sqrt{2} \approx 1.4142$, $\sqrt{3} \approx 1.7321$
- [ ] For 30-60-90 triangle: sides in ratio $1 : \sqrt{3} : 2$
- [ ] For 45-45-90 triangle: sides in ratio $1 : 1 : \sqrt{2}$

> [!TIP]
> **Final Exam Tip:** KTU typically asks one 14-mark question combining truss analysis (Method of Sections) with a 3-mark short question on cables — or a 14-mark pure cable problem (parabolic UDL). Practice at least 2 problems of each type for full marks.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Length of Cables
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCET303 - STRUCTURAL ANALYSIS - I
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Length of Cables

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Structural Analysis – I** (KTU 2024 Scheme, Module 1), a **cable** is a flexible structural member that can resist only **tensile (axial) forces** and cannot take any bending moment, shear, or compression. Cables are universally used in **suspension bridges**, **transmission line systems**, **stayed-cable roofs**, and **cable-stayed bridges**. 

The **"Length of a Cable"** refers to the **actual arc length of the curved cable profile** between its two supports, calculated as a function of the **horizontal span (l)**, the **sag (h)**, and the **horizontal thrust (H)** developed under the applied loading.

Mathematically, if a cable is loaded only by its own self-weight (uniformly distributed along its arc length), it assumes a **catenary profile**. If the cable is loaded uniformly along the **horizontal projection** of its length (e.g., a suspension bridge deck load), it assumes a **parabolic profile**.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** For Module 1, students are expected to derive the arc length of a **parabolic cable** (most frequently asked) and conceptually understand the **catenary cable** length. The catenary is treated as a special case.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are holding a **flexible skipping rope** between your two palms. When the rope is loose, it is short and nearly straight. Now, imagine a **heavy friend sits on the middle of the rope** — the rope sags downward, forming a smooth U-shaped curve. The rope is now **longer** than the straight-line distance between your hands, because some of its material has been "used up" in curving downward.

- The **straight-line distance** between supports = **Span (l)**
- The **vertical dip** at the lowest point = **Sag (h)**
- The **actual curved length of the rope** = **Length of the Cable (L)**

The greater the sag, the longer the cable. The smaller the sag, the closer L approaches the span l. This intuitive relationship is what we will quantify mathematically.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-world Engineering Metric:** Standard cable-suspended bridge designs typically maintain a **sag-to-span ratio (h/l)** between **1/8 and 1/12** for optimal balance between **cable tension** and **stiffening girder design**.

## 1.3 Why Is Cable Length Important?

| Application | Why Cable Length Matters |
|---|---|
| **Suspension Bridge Construction** | Determines the exact quantity of high-tensile wire strands to be cut, spliced, and spun. |
| **Pre-tensioning / Post-tensioning** | Required for elongation calculations due to temperature and live load. |
| **Cost Estimation** | Cable material (steel wire) is expensive; precise length avoids over-ordering. |
| **Deflection / Sag Analysis** | Length change due to load directly affects vertical deflection. |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📗 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Geometry of a Suspended Cable

A typical cable suspended between two supports **A** and **B** is governed by three fundamental equilibrium relationships derived from statics:

1. The **vertical component** of cable tension at any point equals the **vertical shear (V)** at that section.
2. The **horizontal component** of cable tension is **constant** throughout the cable length — it is called the **Horizontal Thrust (H)**.
3. The **slope** of the cable at any point is given by: $dy/dx = V/H$.

## 2.2 The Three Loading Profiles

| Cable Profile | Loading Condition | Shape Equation (origin at lowest point) |
|---|---|---|
| **Parabolic Cable** | UDL along horizontal span (w kN/m) | $y = \dfrac{w x^{2}}{2H}$ |
| **Catenary Cable** | UDL along arc length (w₀ kN/m) | $y = c \left[\cosh\left(\dfrac{x}{c}\right) - 1\right]$ |
| **Straight Line** | Concentrated load at lowest point | $y = x \tan\theta$ |

Where **c = H/w₀** is the **catenary constant**.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these formulas — they appear in nearly every KTU examination on cables.**

### 🔹 For a Symmetric Parabolic Cable (UDL along span)

Let the cable have span **l** and central sag **h**, with supports at the same level.

**Horizontal Thrust:**
$$H = \dfrac{w l^{2}}{8h}$$

**Maximum Tension (at supports):**
$$T_{\max} = \sqrt{H^{2} + \left(\dfrac{w l}{2}\right)^{2}}$$

**Exact Length of the Parabolic Cable:**
$$L = \dfrac{l}{2} \sqrt{1 + \dfrac{16 h^{2}}{l^{2}}} \; + \; \dfrac{l^{2}}{8h} \sinh^{-1}\left(\dfrac{4h}{l}\right)$$

**Binomial Expansion Approximation (when h << l, i.e., flat cables):**
$$L = l + \dfrac{8 h^{2}}{3 l} \; - \; \dfrac{32 h^{4}}{5 l^{3}} + \dots$$

**Truncated Engineering Approximation (most commonly used in KTU):**
$$\boxed{L = l + \dfrac{8 h^{2}}{3 l}}$$

### 🔹 For a Catenary Cable (self-weight loading)

Let the supports be at the **same level** with span **l** and sag **h**.

**Fundamental Catenary Equation:**
$$h = c \left[\cosh\left(\dfrac{l}{2c}\right) - 1\right]$$

**Length of the Catenary Cable:**
$$\boxed{L = 2c \sinh\left(\dfrac{l}{2c}\right)}$$

**Approximation when c is large (sag is small):**
$$L \approx l + \dfrac{h^{2}}{l} \cdot \dfrac{8}{3} \quad \text{(same form as parabolic)}$$

### 🔹 For Unsymmetric / Different Level Supports

If the left support is lower by $h_A$ and the right support is higher by $h_B$ (with $h_A + h_B = h$ total sag), the lowest point is offset from the midspan by distance:
$$x_{0} = \dfrac{l}{2} - \dfrac{H h_{B}}{w l} \quad \text{(for parabolic)}$$

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The parabolic vs. catenary distinction is **not merely academic**:
- In **long-span suspension bridges** (e.g., howrah bridge, golden gate), the **deck weight is uniform per horizontal meter**, so the cable is **parabolic**.
- In **electrical transmission lines**, the cable carries only its own self-weight, so the shape is a **catenary**.
- The **sag-to-span ratio (h/l)** is the single most important design parameter — it controls both cable tension and overall length.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 📙 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Length of a Symmetric Parabolic Cable

### 🔸 Setup and Assumptions

Consider a cable suspended between two supports **A** and **B** at the same level, with:
- Span between supports = **l**
- Central sag (dip at midspan) = **h**
- Uniformly distributed load along horizontal span = **w** (kN/m)
- Origin taken at the **lowest point O** of the cable

The cable assumes a **parabolic profile** given by:
$$y = \dfrac{w x^{2}}{2H} \quad \text{...(1)}$$

where **H** is the horizontal thrust (constant along the cable).

At midspan, $x = l/2$ and $y = h$, so:
$$h = \dfrac{w (l/2)^{2}}{2H} \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \boxed{H = \dfrac{w l^{2}}{8h}} \quad \text{...(2)}$$

### 🔸 Arc Length Differential

The differential arc length **ds** of a curve $y = f(x)$ is given by:
$$ds = \sqrt{1 + \left(\dfrac{dy}{dx}\right)^{2}} \; dx \quad \text{...(3)}$$

Differentiating equation (1) with respect to x:
$$\dfrac{dy}{dx} = \dfrac{w x}{H} \quad \text{...(4)}$$

Substituting equation (4) into equation (3):
$$ds = \sqrt{1 + \left(\dfrac{w x}{H}\right)^{2}} \; dx \quad \text{...(5)}$$

### 🔸 Total Cable Length — Exact Integration

The total length of the cable between supports is:
$$L = \int_{-l/2}^{+l/2} \sqrt{1 + \left(\dfrac{w x}{H}\right)^{2}} \; dx \quad \text{...(6)}$$

By symmetry of the parabola, this simplifies to:
$$L = 2 \int_{0}^{l/2} \sqrt{1 + \left(\dfrac{w x}{H}\right)^{2}} \; dx \quad \text{...(7)}$$

**Substitution:** Let $u = \dfrac{w x}{H}$, so $du = \dfrac{w}{H} dx$, which gives $dx = \dfrac{H}{w} du$.

**Limits:** When $x = 0$, $u = 0$. When $x = l/2$, $u = \dfrac{w l}{2H}$.

But from equation (2): $\dfrac{w l}{2H} = \dfrac{4h}{l}$. So upper limit becomes $u = \dfrac{4h}{l}$.

Therefore equation (7) becomes:
$$L = 2 \cdot \dfrac{H}{w} \int_{0}^{4h/l} \sqrt{1 + u^{2}} \; du \quad \text{...(8)}$$

Using the standard integral formula:
$$\int \sqrt{1 + u^{2}} \; du = \dfrac{u}{2} \sqrt{1 + u^{2}} + \dfrac{1}{2} \sinh^{-1}(u) \quad \text{...(9)}$$

Applying this from $u = 0$ to $u = 4h/l$:
$$\int_{0}^{4h/l} \sqrt{1 + u^{2}} \; du = \dfrac{1}{2} \left[ u\sqrt{1 + u^{2}} + \sinh^{-1}(u) \right]_{0}^{4h/l} \quad \text{...(10)}$$

Evaluating at limits:
$$= \dfrac{1}{2} \left[ \dfrac{4h}{l} \sqrt{1 + \dfrac{16 h^{2}}{l^{2}}} + \sinh^{-1}\left(\dfrac{4h}{l}\right) \right] \quad \text{...(11)}$$

Substituting back into equation (8) and using $\dfrac{H}{w} = \dfrac{l^{2}}{8h}$ from equation (2):
$$L = 2 \cdot \dfrac{l^{2}}{8h} \cdot \dfrac{1}{2} \left[ \dfrac{4h}{l} \sqrt{1 + \dfrac{16 h^{2}}{l^{2}}} + \sinh^{-1}\left(\dfrac{4h}{l}\right) \right] \quad \text{...(12)}$$

Simplifying:
$$L = \dfrac{l^{2}}{8h} \left[ \dfrac{4h}{l} \sqrt{1 + \dfrac{16 h^{2}}{l^{2}}} + \sinh^{-1}\left(\dfrac{4h}{l}\right) \right] \quad \text{...(13)}$$

$$\boxed{L = \dfrac{l}{2} \sqrt{1 + \dfrac{16 h^{2}}{l^{2}}} + \dfrac{l^{2}}{8h} \sinh^{-1}\left(\dfrac{4h}{l}\right)}$$

This is the **exact** length of the parabolic cable. ✅

### 🔸 Binomial Series Approximation

For most engineering cables, the sag-to-span ratio is small (h/l < 1/8). Using the binomial expansion:
$$\sqrt{1 + \epsilon} \approx 1 + \dfrac{\epsilon}{2} - \dfrac{\epsilon^{2}}{8} + \dfrac{\epsilon^{3}}{16} - \dots \quad \text{...(14)}$$

With $\epsilon = 16h^{2}/l^{2}$ and noting that $\sinh^{-1}(4h/l)$ also expands similarly:
$$\sinh^{-1}(u) = u - \dfrac{u^{3}}{6} + \dfrac{3u^{5}}{40} - \dots \quad \text{...(15)}$$

Substituting and simplifying term by term, the **first-order engineering approximation** is:
$$\boxed{L \approx l + \dfrac{8 h^{2}}{3 l}}$$

This formula is the **most frequently used** in KTU problems. The error introduced by dropping higher-order terms is less than 0.1% for h/l ≤ 1/10.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of the Length of a Catenary Cable

### 🔸 Setup and Assumptions

For a cable of self-weight $w_{0}$ per unit arc length, supported at equal level, with horizontal thrust **H** at the lowest point.

Let the **catenary constant** be defined as:
$$c = \dfrac{H}{w_{0}} \quad \text{...(16)}$$

The equation of the catenary (with origin at lowest point O) is:
$$y = c \left[ \cosh\left(\dfrac{x}{c}\right) - 1 \right] \quad \text{...(17)}$$

### 🔸 Verification of Sag Relationship

At the supports, $x = l/2$ and $y = h$:
$$h = c \left[ \cosh\left(\dfrac{l}{2c}\right) - 1 \right] \quad \text{...(18)}$$

### 🔸 Length of the Catenary

The differential arc length:
$$ds = \sqrt{1 + \left(\dfrac{dy}{dx}\right)^{2}} \; dx \quad \text{...(19)}$$

Differentiating equation (17):
$$\dfrac{dy}{dx} = \sinh\left(\dfrac{x}{c}\right) \quad \text{...(20)}$$

Substituting into (19):
$$ds = \sqrt{1 + \sinh^{2}\left(\dfrac{x}{c}\right)} \; dx = \cosh\left(\dfrac{x}{c}\right) dx \quad \text{...(21)}$$

(using the identity $1 + \sinh^{2}\theta = \cosh^{2}\theta$)

Total length:
$$L = 2 \int_{0}^{l/2} \cosh\left(\dfrac{x}{c}\right) dx \quad \text{...(22)}$$

$$L = 2 \left[ c \sinh\left(\dfrac{x}{c}\right) \right]_{0}^{l/2} \quad \text{...(23)}$$

$$\boxed{L = 2c \sinh\left(\dfrac{l}{2c}\right)}$$

This is the **exact** length of a catenary cable. ✅

### 🔸 Special Case: Small Sag (c is large)

When $l/(2c)$ is small, use $\sinh(\theta) \approx \theta + \theta^{3}/6 + ...$:
$$L \approx l + \dfrac{l^{3}}{24 c^{2}} + \dots \quad \text{...(24)}$$

Using the relation $c = H/w_0$ and noting that for small sag, the parabolic and catenary profiles coincide to first order:
$$L \approx l + \dfrac{8 h^{2}}{3 l} \quad \text{...(same form as parabolic)}$$

---

## 3.3 Numerical Worked Example (KTU Pattern)

**Problem:** A suspension cable of span **30 m** has a central sag of **3 m**. The cable is loaded by a UDL of **10 kN/m** along the horizontal span. Calculate the exact length and the approximate length of the cable.

### Solution

**Given:** l = 30 m, h = 3 m, w = 10 kN/m

**Step 1: Compute horizontal thrust H**
$$H = \dfrac{w l^{2}}{8 h} = \dfrac{10 \times 30^{2}}{8 \times 3} = \dfrac{10 \times 900}{24} = 375 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 2: Exact length using the full formula**
$$L = \dfrac{l}{2} \sqrt{1 + \dfrac{16 h^{2}}{l^{2}}} + \dfrac{l^{2}}{8 h} \sinh^{-1}\left(\dfrac{4 h}{l}\right)$$

Compute $\dfrac{4h}{l} = \dfrac{4 \times 3}{30} = 0.4$

Compute $\sqrt{1 + (0.4)^{2}} = \sqrt{1.16} = 1.0770$

Compute $\sinh^{-1}(0.4) = \ln(0.4 + \sqrt{1 + 0.16}) = \ln(0.4 + 1.0770) = \ln(1.4770) = 0.3900$

Therefore:
$$L = \dfrac{30}{2} \times 1.0770 + \dfrac{30^{2}}{8 \times 3} \times 0.3900$$
$$L = 15 \times 1.0770 + 37.5 \times 0.3900$$
$$L = 16.155 + 14.625 = 30.780 \text{ m}$$

**Step 3: Approximate length using $L = l + 8h^{2}/(3l)$**
$$L \approx 30 + \dfrac{8 \times (3)^{2}}{3 \times 30} = 30 + \dfrac{72}{90} = 30 + 0.80 = 30.80 \text{ m}$$

**Step 4: Compare results**

| Method | Length L | Error |
|---|---|---|
| Exact | 30.780 m | 0.000 % |
| Approximate | 30.800 m | +0.065 % |

The approximate formula is highly accurate for this sag-to-span ratio (h/l = 0.1). ✅

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 📓 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define "Catenary" and state its equation. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**Model Answer:**

A **catenary** is the shape assumed by a flexible cable hanging freely under its own uniform self-weight per unit arc length, with no other load applied.

The equation of a catenary (with origin at the lowest point) is:
$$y = c \left[ \cosh\left(\dfrac{x}{c}\right) - 1 \right]$$

where **c = H / w₀** is the catenary constant, **H** is the horizontal thrust, and **w₀** is the self-weight per unit length of the cable.

The **arc length** of a catenary between two symmetric supports of span **l** is:
$$L = 2c \sinh\left(\dfrac{l}{2c}\right)$$

### Question 2: Distinguish between a Parabolic Cable and a Catenary Cable. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Parabolic Cable | Catenary Cable |
|---|---|---|
| **Loading Type** | UDL along horizontal span (w kN/m) | UDL along arc length (w₀ kN/m) |
| **Shape Equation** | $y = \dfrac{w x^{2}}{2H}$ | $y = c \left[\cosh\left(\dfrac{x}{c}\right) - 1\right]$ |
| **Typical Use** | Suspension bridges (deck load) | Transmission lines, chains |
| **Arc Length Formula** | $L = l + \dfrac{8h^{2}}{3l}$ (approx.) | $L = 2c \sinh\left(\dfrac{l}{2c}\right)$ |
| **Approximation** | Used when h/l is small | Reduces to parabolic for small sag |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full Length Questions (14 Marks Each)

### 📌 Question A — Choice 1 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]

A suspension cable of span **40 m** is supported at the same level. The cable carries a uniformly distributed load of **15 kN/m** along the horizontal span. The central sag is **4 m**.

**Part (a)** [7 Marks]: Determine the maximum and minimum tensions in the cable.

**Part (b)** [7 Marks]: Calculate the length of the cable using (i) the exact formula and (ii) the approximate formula $L = l + 8h^{2}/(3l)$.

#### ✅ Model Solution

**Given:** l = 40 m, h = 4 m, w = 15 kN/m

---

**Part (a): Maximum and Minimum Tensions**

**Step 1: Compute horizontal thrust H**
$$H = \dfrac{w l^{2}}{8 h} = \dfrac{15 \times 40^{2}}{8 \times 4} = \dfrac{15 \times 1600}{32} = 750 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 2: Vertical reaction at each support**
$$V = \dfrac{w l}{2} = \dfrac{15 \times 40}{2} = 300 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 3: Maximum tension (at supports A and B)**
$$T_{\max} = \sqrt{H^{2} + V^{2}} = \sqrt{750^{2} + 300^{2}} = \sqrt{562500 + 90000} = \sqrt{652500}$$
$$\boxed{T_{\max} = 807.78 \text{ kN}}$$

**Step 4: Minimum tension (at the lowest point O, where V = 0)**
$$T_{\min} = H = 750 \text{ kN}$$

---

**Part (b): Length of the Cable**

**(i) Exact Length**

Formula:
$$L = \dfrac{l}{2} \sqrt{1 + \dfrac{16 h^{2}}{l^{2}}} + \dfrac{l^{2}}{8 h} \sinh^{-1}\left(\dfrac{4h}{l}\right)$$

Compute $\dfrac{4h}{l} = \dfrac{4 \times 4}{40} = 0.4$

Compute $\sqrt{1 + 0.4^{2}} = \sqrt{1.16} = 1.0770$

Compute $\sinh^{-1}(0.4) = \ln(0.4 + \sqrt{1.16}) = \ln(0.4 + 1.0770) = \ln(1.4770) = 0.3900$ rad

Substitute:
$$L = \dfrac{40}{2} \times 1.0770 + \dfrac{40^{2}}{8 \times 4} \times 0.3900$$
$$L = 20 \times 1.0770 + 50 \times 0.3900$$
$$L = 21.540 + 19.500 = \boxed{41.04 \text{ m}}$$

**(ii) Approximate Length**
$$L = l + \dfrac{8 h^{2}}{3 l} = 40 + \dfrac{8 \times 16}{3 \times 40} = 40 + \dfrac{128}{120} = 40 + 1.067 = \boxed{41.067 \text{ m}}$$

**Comparison:**

| Method | Length L (m) |
|---|---|
| Exact | 41.040 |
| Approximate | 41.067 |
| % Error | 0.066 % |

The approximate formula is highly accurate. ✅

---

### 📌 Question B — Choice 2 [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]

A cable is suspended between two supports **20 m apart** at the same level, carrying a uniformly distributed load of **8 kN/m** along the horizontal span. The central sag is **2.5 m**.

**Part (a)** [7 Marks]: Find the horizontal thrust and the length of the cable using the approximate formula.

**Part (b)** [7 Marks]: If the temperature of the cable rises by **30 °C**, find the new sag. Take $\alpha = 12 \times 10^{-6}$ per °C and $E = 2 \times 10^{5}$ N/mm². The cross-sectional area of the cable is **1500 mm²**. Assume the load remains unchanged and the supports are immovable.

#### ✅ Model Solution

**Given:** l = 20 m, h = 2.5 m, w = 8 kN/m, $\Delta T = 30$ °C, $\alpha = 12 \times 10^{-6}$ /°C, E = $2 \times 10^{5}$ N/mm², A = 1500 mm²

---

**Part (a): Horizontal Thrust and Length**

**Step 1: Horizontal Thrust**
$$H = \dfrac{w l^{2}}{8 h} = \dfrac{8 \times 20^{2}}{8 \times 2.5} = \dfrac{8 \times 400}{20} = 160 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 2: Approximate length**
$$L = l + \dfrac{8 h^{2}}{3 l} = 20 + \dfrac{8 \times 6.25}{3 \times 20} = 20 + \dfrac{50}{60} = 20 + 0.833$$
$$\boxed{L = 20.833 \text{ m}}$$

---

**Part (b): New Sag After Temperature Rise**

When temperature increases, the cable **expands**. Since supports are immovable, the cable will **sag further** to accommodate the extra length, but this also **increases the horizontal thrust**.

**Step 1: Compatibility equation for cable length**

The increase in cable length must equal the thermal expansion:
$$\Delta L = L \cdot \alpha \cdot \Delta T = 20.833 \times 12 \times 10^{-6} \times 30 = 0.0075 \text{ m} = 7.5 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 2: New sag condition**

The new sag $h_{1}$ must satisfy the geometric compatibility:
$$L + \Delta L = l + \dfrac{8 h_{1}^{2}}{3 l}$$

$$20.833 + 0.0075 = 20 + \dfrac{8 h_{1}^{2}}{3 \times 20}$$

$$20.8405 = 20 + \dfrac{h_{1}^{2}}{7.5}$$

$$\dfrac{h_{1}^{2}}{7.5} = 0.8405 \;\Rightarrow\; h_{1}^{2} = 6.304 \;\Rightarrow\; h_{1} = 2.511 \text{ m}$$

$$\boxed{h_{1} \approx 2.511 \text{ m}}$$

**Step 3: New horizontal thrust (using the new sag)**
$$H_{1} = \dfrac{w l^{2}}{8 h_{1}} = \dfrac{8 \times 400}{8 \times 2.511} = \dfrac{400}{2.511} = 159.30 \text{ kN}$$

(Note: Temperature increase → cable elongates → sag increases slightly → H decreases slightly.)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:**
> 1. **Sign Convention in Temperature Problems:** Always remember that $\Delta T > 0$ (rise) causes the cable to **elongate** and **sag to increase**. Students often incorrectly assume sag decreases with heating.
> 2. **Units Consistency:** When using $\alpha$ in per-°C and E in N/mm², keep all lengths in mm or convert carefully. The horizontal thrust H must be in N (not kN) when working with N/mm² units.
> 3. **Forgetting the Elastic Elongation Term:** A complete analysis includes $\Delta L_{elastic} = HL / (AE)$. The problem may or may not include it — read carefully. Here the problem asked for the geometric (thermal) effect only.
> 4. **Binomial Approximation Validity:** The formula $L = l + 8h^{2}/(3l)$ is only valid for $h/l \le 1/8$. In our example, h/l = 0.125, which is the boundary. For larger sags, the exact formula must be used.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **🚀 Rapid Revision Checklist — Length of Cables**

- ⭐ A cable resists **only tension** — no bending, no shear, no compression.
- ⭐ For **UDL along horizontal span** → **Parabolic profile**: $y = wx^{2}/(2H)$.
- ⭐ For **UDL along arc length (self-weight)** → **Catenary profile**: $y = c[\cosh(x/c) - 1]$.
- ⭐ **Horizontal thrust (H)** is **constant** along the entire cable length.
- ⭐ $H = wl^{2}/(8h)$ for symmetric parabolic cable — **the most-tested formula**.
- ⭐ **Maximum tension** occurs at **supports**: $T_{\max} = \sqrt{H^{2} + (wl/2)^{2}}$.
- ⭐ **Minimum tension** occurs at the **lowest point**: $T_{\min} = H$.
- ⭐ **Exact length of parabolic cable**:
$$L = \dfrac{l}{2}\sqrt{1 + \dfrac{16h^{2}}{l^{2}}} + \dfrac{l^{2}}{8h}\sinh^{-1}\left(\dfrac{4h}{l}\right)$$
- ⭐ **Engineering approximation** (for h/l ≤ 1/8): $L \approx l + \dfrac{8h^{2}}{3l}$.
- ⭐ **Exact length of catenary**: $L = 2c \sinh(l/2c)$, where $c = H/w_{0}$.
- ⭐ **Higher sag → longer cable + lower tension** (key design trade-off).
- ⭐ **Lower sag → shorter cable + higher tension** (stiffer structure but more material stress).
- ⭐ **Temperature rise** → cable **expands** → sag **increases** (for fixed supports).
- ⭐ **Temperature fall** → cable **contracts** → sag **decreases**, **tension increases**.
- ⭐ **Typical sag-to-span ratio** in bridges: **1/8 to 1/12**.
- ⭐ **Unsymmetric loading** shifts the lowest point away from midspan — location given by $x_{0} = (l/2) - Hh_{B}/(wl)$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Supports at Same and Different Levels
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCCET303 - STRUCTURAL ANALYSIS - I > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Supports at Same and Different Levels ## 1. CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) A **three-hinged arch** is a curved structural member provided with **two end hinges** at the supports and **one internal hinge** (conventionally placed at the **crown**, i.e., the highest point). This introduces **four reaction components** (V_A, H_A, V_B, H_B), against **three equations of static equilibrium** and **one condition equation** (the internal hinge ensures the bending moment at the crown is zero), making the structure **statically determinate** and **stable** under general loading. The two principal configurations studied under this KTU Module 1 topic are: > [!IMPORTANT] > **Configuration A – Supports at the Same Level:** Both end hinges (A and B) lie on the **same horizontal datum**. The span `l` is the horizontal distance between A and B, and the **rise `h`** is the vertical distance from the springing line (line AB) to the crown hinge C. The horizontal thrust developed at the two supports is **equal and opposite** (H_A = H_B = H). > [!IMPORTANT] > **Configuration B – Supports at Different Levels:** The two end hinges are at **unequal elevations**. The level difference is conventionally denoted by `h₁` (the right support B is higher than the left support A by `h₁`). Only **one parameter** (commonly `h` = rise of crown above the lower support) is used to express the arch geometry. The horizontal thrust is still derived using the **same condition** (M_C = 0), but the **bending-moment expression** must account for the offset of the right support. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Real-World Intuition) Imagine a **flexible steel tape** (an arch) bent into a smooth curve and pinned at both ends. The tape is **unloaded** — it just hangs there, supported by the pins. If you place a small weight on the tape somewhere along its length, the tape will try to flatten out, pushing its ends **outward** (this push is the *horizontal thrust*). The pins resist this push, keeping the tape curved. Now consider two scenarios: - **Same level:** Both pins are on a flat table. The horizontal push at the left pin is exactly equal in magnitude (and opposite in direction) to the push at the right pin — a beautiful symmetry. - **Different level:** Place the right pin on a brick so the right end is higher. The tape still pushes the pins outward, but now the **height of the brick matters** when calculating the *internal bending moment* in the tape, because the load's effect "lever arms" change. The horizontal push at each pin is still equal and opposite (Newton's third law for a vertical-only load system), but the moment of this push about any section of the tape is no longer simply `H × y`. > [!NOTE] > **Real-world applications:** Roman aqueducts, masonry bridges, modern reinforced-concrete flyovers, and the arch ribs of long-span steel bridges (e.g., Sydney Harbour Bridge uses three-hinged arches during construction stages). Supports at different levels are common in **hilly terrain** for road/rail viaducts and **dam spillways**. ### 1.3 Standard Assumptions (Per KTU Board Valuation Pattern) 1. The arch is **parabolic** unless stated otherwise: y = (4h/l²)·x·(l − x), origin at the left support. 2. Loads are **vertical** (no horizontal external loads); consequently, H_A = H_B = H. 3. The **crown hinge** lies at mid-span (x = l/2), at vertical height `h` above the **left (lower) support**. 4. Sign convention: Positive bending moment causes **tension on the bottom fibre** of the arch (sagging). 5. Positive normal thrust is **compressive**; positive radial shear acts **outward** from the centre of curvature. 6. Span `l` is the **horizontal distance** between supports (constant in both configurations). --- ## 2. DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET ### 2.1 Step-by-Step Operational Logic **Step 1 — Determine Vertical Reactions (V_A and V_B).** Treat the arch as a simply-supported beam of span `l` carrying the same vertical loads. The vertical reactions are found using ΣM_B = 0 and ΣM_A = 0. This step is **identical** for both same-level and different-level supports, because only the horizontal distances matter for vertical equilibrium. **Step 2 — Apply the Hinge Condition at the Crown.** The internal hinge at the crown ensures the **bending moment at C is zero**. By taking moments of all forces on the **left half** of the arch about the crown, we obtain: $$H \times h \;=\; M_{C}^{0}$$ where M_C^0 is the **simply-supported beam bending moment at the crown section** (calculated by treating the arch as a beam) and `h` is the **vertical height of the crown above the lower support**. This is the **master equation** for the horizontal thrust. **Step 3 — Compute Bending Moment at any Section x.** For a section at horizontal distance `x` from the left support, the bending moment in the arch is: $$M_{x} \;=\; M_{x}^{0} \;-\; H \cdot y_{x}$$ where y_x is the height of the arch axis at `x` measured **above the left (lower) support** datum, and M_x^0 is the simply-supported beam moment at the same section. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Critical KTU Distinction:** For **same-level supports**, y_x is also the height above the line AB. For **different-level supports**, y_x must be measured from the **lower support**, NOT from the inclined line AB. This is the single most common error in board examinations. **Step 4 — Compute the Net Vertical Shear V_x.** $$V_{x} \;=\; V_{A} \;-\; \sum(\text{loads to the left of section } x)$$ **Step 5 — Compute the Slope of the Arch Axis at Section x.** For a parabolic arch, dy/dx = (4h/l²)·(l − 2x); therefore: $$\tan\theta_{x} \;=\; \frac{4h}{l^{2}}\,(l-2x)$$ The angle θ_x is the inclination of the arch axis tangent with the horizontal. It is the **same** for both same- and different-level cases, because the arch shape is the same. **Step 6 — Compute Normal Thrust (N) and Radial Shear (S).** $$N_{x} \;=\; H\cos\theta_{x} \;+\; V_{x}\sin\theta_{x}$$ $$S_{x} \;=\; V_{x}\cos\theta_{x} \;-\; H\sin\theta_{x}$$ > The **sign convention**: V_x is taken as positive when the resultant of forces on the left of the section is **upward** (i.e., V_A > total downward load on the left of the section). The sign of the V sin θ term in N reverses for the right half if the slope direction flips. ### 2.2 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Bridge design:** The horizontal thrust H is critical for designing the **abutments** (the massive end supports that resist the horizontal push). For different-level supports, the abutment design must account for the inclined reaction resultant. - **Influence-line construction:** For moving-load analysis (trains, vehicles), the BM/NT/RS influence lines are obtained using Müller-Breslau or static-equilibrium principles on the arch — directly using the formulas above. - **Stiffening girders:** For arch bridges carrying moving loads, the formula M = M^0 − Hy directly gives the moment that a parallel girder (the "stiffener") must resist to keep the arch unloaded. ### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet | # | Quantity | Formula | Applicable Case | |---|----------|---------|-----------------| | 1 | Equation of arch axis | $y = \dfrac{4h}{l^{2}}\,x\,(l-x)$ | Parabolic, both cases | | 2 | Slope of arch axis | $\tan\theta = \dfrac{4h}{l^{2}}\,(l-2x)$ | Parabolic, both cases | | 3 | Horizontal thrust (point load P at 'a' from A, a ≤ l/2) | $H = \dfrac{P\,a}{2\,h}$ | Both cases (same `h` definition) | | 4 | Horizontal thrust (point load P at 'a' from A, a ≥ l/2) | $H = \dfrac{P\,(l-a)}{2\,h}$ | Both cases | | 5 | Horizontal thrust (UDL w over entire span) | $H = \dfrac{w\,l^{2}}{8\,h}$ | Both cases | | 6 | Horizontal thrust (UDL w over length 'a' from A, a ≤ l/2) | $H = \dfrac{w\,a^{2}}{4\,h}$ | Both cases | | 7 | Vertical reactions (point load P at 'a' from A) | $V_{A} = \dfrac{P(l-a)}{l}$, $V_{B} = \dfrac{Pa}{l}$ | Both cases | | 8 | Bending moment at section x | $M_{x} = M_{x}^{0} - H\,y_{x}$ | **Both cases** (y_x from lower support) | | 9 | Net vertical shear at section x | $V_{x} = V_{A} - \sum(\text{loads on left})$ | Both cases | | 10 | Normal thrust at section x | $N_{x} = H\cos\theta_{x} + V_{x}\sin\theta_{x}$ | Both cases | | 11 | Radial shear at section x | $S_{x} = V_{x}\cos\theta_{x} - H\sin\theta_{x}$ | Both cases | | 12 | Horizontal reactions | $H_{A} = H_{B} = H$ | **Only when no external horizontal load** | | 13 | Reaction at lower support A | $R_{A} = \sqrt{V_{A}^{2} + H^{2}}$ inclined at $\tan^{-1}(H/V_A)$ | Both cases | | 14 | Reaction at higher support B (different level) | $R_{B} = \sqrt{V_{B}^{2} + H^{2}}$ inclined at $\tan^{-1}(H/V_B)$ | Different level case (resolves to H + V_B components) | | 15 | Bending moment at crown (by definition) | $M_{C} = 0$ | Both cases (the hinge condition) | > [!NOTE] > The horizontal thrust H is **computed using the same formula** in both cases. The only difference lies in how M_x^0 and y_x are measured when the supports are at different levels — both must use the lower-support datum as the reference. --- ## 3. STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX ### 3.1 Derivation of the Master Equation: H = M_C^0 / h Consider a three-hinged parabolic arch with the **left support A** at the origin (0, 0), the **crown C** at (l/2, h), and the **right support B** at (l, h₁) where h₁ may be zero (same level) or non-zero (different level). Apply a general vertical load system on the arch. The three-hinge condition states that the bending moment at C is zero. Computing the moment of all forces on the **left half** of the arch (i.e., to the left of the crown) about point C: $$\begin{aligned} \sum M_{C}^{\text{left}} &= 0 \\ V_{A} \cdot \tfrac{l}{2} \;-\; \sum\bigl(P_{i}\cdot d_{i}\bigr) \;-\; H \cdot h &= 0 \end{aligned}$$ The first two terms together constitute the simply-supported beam moment at the crown, M_C^0: $$V_{A} \cdot \tfrac{l}{2} \;-\; \sum\bigl(P_{i}\cdot d_{i}\bigr) \;=\; M_{C}^{0}$$ Substituting: $$\begin{aligned} M_{C}^{0} \;-\; H \cdot h &= 0 \\ H \cdot h &= M_{C}^{0} \\ \boxed{H &= \dfrac{M_{C}^{0}}{h}} \end{aligned}$$ This **master equation** holds for **both same-level and different-level supports**, with the *single caveat* that `h` is the vertical height of the crown above the **left (lower) support**. Notice that h₁ (the elevation of the right support) does **not appear** in this formula, because the hinge condition is applied about C using only the left half of the structure. ### 3.2 Derivation of Bending Moment M_x Cut the arch at a horizontal distance `x` from the left support A. Consider the left portion. The forces acting on it are: V_A (vertical up), H (horizontal right), and any external loads between 0 and x. The moment about the cut section (located at coordinates (x, y_x)): $$M_{x} \;=\; V_{A} \cdot x \;-\; \sum\bigl(P_{i}\,(x-a_{i})\bigr) \;-\; H \cdot y_{x}$$ The first two terms form the simply-supported beam moment: $$M_{x}^{0} \;=\; V_{A} \cdot x \;-\; \sum\bigl(P_{i}\,(x-a_{i})\bigr)$$ Therefore: $$\boxed{M_{x} \;=\; M_{x}^{0} \;-\; H \cdot y_{x}}$$ **Same-level case:** y_x is the height above the springing line AB. **Different-level case:** y_x is the height above the **lower support** (not above the inclined line AB). The formula's *form* is identical; the *reference datum* for y_x changes. ### 3.3 Derivation of Normal Thrust (N) and Radial Shear (S) At the cut section, the arch axis is inclined at angle θ_x to the horizontal. The resultant of all forces on the left of the cut is the vector sum of (V_A − ΣP_i) acting vertically and H acting horizontally. Resolving this resultant along the arch axis (giving the normal thrust) and perpendicular to the arch axis (giving the radial shear): **Along the arch axis (compression positive):** The horizontal force H makes angle θ_x with the axis, contributing H cos θ_x. The vertical force V_x = V_A − ΣP_i makes angle (90° − θ_x) with the axis, contributing V_x sin θ_x. $$N_{x} \;=\; H\cos\theta_{x} \;+\; V_{x}\sin\theta_{x}$$ **Perpendicular to the arch axis (radial shear positive when acting on the "outer" face of the cut):** $$S_{x} \;=\; V_{x}\cos\theta_{x} \;-\; H\sin\theta_{x}$$ **Sign rule for V_x:** Take V_x as the algebraic sum of upward forces on the left of the section. The sign of sin θ_x changes after the crown (where θ_x becomes negative as the arch descends). ### 3.4 Detailed Worked Derivation: Point Load on a Parabolic Arch **Problem setup:** Parabolic three-hinged arch, span l, rise h, supports at the same level. A point load P is applied at a horizontal distance `a` from the left support (assume a < l/2). **Step 1 — Vertical reactions (using beam analogy):** $$\begin{aligned} V_{A} &= \dfrac{P(l-a)}{l} \\ V_{B} &= \dfrac{Pa}{l} \end{aligned}$$ **Step 2 — Horizontal thrust (from hinge condition):** The simply-supported beam moment at the crown (x = l/2, which lies to the right of the load): $$M_{C}^{0} \;=\; V_{B} \cdot \frac{l}{2} \;=\; \frac{Pa}{l} \cdot \frac{l}{2} \;=\; \frac{Pa}{2}$$ $$H \;=\; \dfrac{M_{C}^{0}}{h} \;=\; \dfrac{Pa}{2h}$$ **Step 3 — Bending moment at a section x, where 0 ≤ x < a (load is to the right of section):** $$M_{x}^{0} \;=\; V_{A} \cdot x \;=\; \frac{P(l-a)}{l} \cdot x$$ $$y_{x} \;=\; \frac{4h}{l^{2}}\,x\,(l-x)$$ $$M_{x} \;=\; \frac{P(l-a)\,x}{l} \;-\; \frac{Pa}{2h} \cdot \frac{4h}{l^{2}}\,x\,(l-x)$$ $$\boxed{M_{x} \;=\; \frac{P(l-a)\,x}{l} \;-\; \frac{2Pa\,x\,(l-x)}{l^{2}}}$$ **Step 4 — Bending moment at a section x, where a < x ≤ l/2 (load is to the left of section):** $$M_{x}^{0} \;=\; V_{A}\,x \;-\; P(x-a) \;=\; \frac{P(l-a)x}{l} \;-\; P(x-a)$$ $$M_{x} \;=\; \frac{P(l-a)x}{l} \;-\; P(x-a) \;-\; \frac{2Pa\,x\,(l-x)}{l^{2}}$$ **Step 5 — Bending moment at the crown (x = l/2, y = h):** $$M_{C} \;=\; M_{C}^{0} \;-\; H \cdot h \;=\; \frac{Pa}{2} \;-\; \frac{Pa}{2h} \cdot h \;=\; 0 \quad \checkmark$$ This validates the hinge condition. **Step 6 — Bending moment at the load point (x = a, y = 4h·a(l-a)/l²):** $$M_{a}^{0} \;=\; V_{A} \cdot a \;=\; \frac{Pa(l-a)}{l}$$ $$M_{a} \;=\; \frac{Pa(l-a)}{l} \;-\; \frac{Pa}{2h} \cdot \frac{4ha(l-a)}{l^{2}} \;=\; \frac{Pa(l-a)}{l} \;-\; \frac{2Pa^{2}(l-a)}{l^{2}}$$ $$M_{a} \;=\; \frac{Pa(l-a)}{l}\left[1 \;-\; \frac{2a}{l}\right] \;=\; \frac{Pa(l-a)(l-2a)}{l^{2}}$$ **Step 7 — Maximum Bending Moment (location and value):** Differentiate M_x with respect to x and set to zero. For 0 ≤ x < a, working with the form derived in Step 3: $$\frac{dM_{x}}{dx} \;=\; \frac{P(l-a)}{l} \;-\; \frac{2Pa}{l^{2}}\,(l-2x) \;=\; 0$$ $$P(l-a) \cdot l \;=\; 2Pa\,(l-2x) \;=\; 2Pal \;-\; 4Pax$$ $$Pl(l-a) \;=\; 2Pal \;-\; 4Pax$$ $$l(l-a) \;=\; 2al \;-\; 4ax$$ $$l^{2} - al \;=\; 2al \;-\; 4ax$$ $$l^{2} \;=\; 3al \;-\; 4ax$$ $$4ax \;=\; 3al \;-\; l^{2}$$ $$x \;=\; \frac{l(3a-l)}{4a}$$ This value of x gives the position of maximum (or minimum) bending moment. Substitute back into M_x to get the maximum value. ### 3.5 Detailed Worked Derivation: Supports at Different Levels **Problem setup:** Parabolic three-hinged arch, span l (horizontal), rise h above the **lower** support. The right support B is at elevation h₁ above the left support A (i.e., B is the higher support by h₁). A point load P is applied at distance `a` from A (with a < l/2). **Step 1 — Vertical reactions (unchanged):** $$V_{A} = \frac{P(l-a)}{l}, \quad V_{B} = \frac{Pa}{l}$$ **Step 2 — Horizontal thrust:** The hinge condition at the crown gives **the same formula** as before: $$H \;=\; \frac{M_{C}^{0}}{h} \;=\; \frac{Pa}{2h}$$ (The value of h₁ does not enter the hinge equation because the crown moment is computed from the **left** half only.) **Step 3 — Bending moment at section x:** The arch equation, measured from the **left (lower) support A**, remains: $$y_{x} \;=\; \frac{4h}{l^{2}}\,x\,(l-x)$$ This y_x is the height of the arch at section x above the **left support**, not above the inclined line joining A and B. The bending moment formula is: $$\boxed{M_{x} \;=\; M_{x}^{0} \;-\; H \cdot y_{x} \;=\; M_{x}^{0} \;-\; \frac{Pa}{2h} \cdot \frac{4h\,x(l-x)}{l^{2}} \;=\; M_{x}^{0} \;-\; \frac{2Pa\,x(l-x)}{l^{2}}}$$ This is **algebraically identical** to the same-level case, but the *physical meaning* of y_x has changed — it is now measured from the lower support. The same-level case happens to be the special case h₁ = 0. **Step 4 — Normal thrust and radial shear:** The formulas N_x = H cos θ_x + V_x sin θ_x and S_x = V_x cos θ_x − H sin θ_x are **unchanged**, because they depend only on the slope θ_x of the arch axis (which is the same) and on H and V_x (which are the same). **Step 5 — Verification: the moment at the right support B must equal zero.** At B, x = l and y = h (height of crown above left support; the right support is at height h₁, so the arch axis at B is at height h₁... wait, let me reconsider). The arch equation gives y_l = 4h·l·(l−l)/l² = 0, which would place B at the same level as A. This is a contradiction. The resolution: when supports are at different levels, the arch is **not a pure parabola** with both supports at y = 0. Instead, the equation is modified. **Correct formulation for different-level supports:** The arch axis (still parabolic in shape) passes through A = (0, 0), B = (l, h₁), and the crown C = (l/2, h) where h is the height of the crown above the **lower** support. The arch equation is: $$y_{x} \;=\; \frac{4h}{l^{2}}\,x\,(l-x) \;+\; \frac{h_{1}}{l}\,x$$ This is a parabola shifted by a linear term. At x = 0, y = 0 (correct, A is the lower support). At x = l, y = 0 + h₁ = h₁ (correct, B is at height h₁). The crown height at x = l/2 is: $$y_{l/2} \;=\; \frac{4h}{l^{2}}\cdot\frac{l}{2}\cdot\frac{l}{2} \;+\; \frac{h_{1}}{l}\cdot\frac{l}{2} \;=\; h \;+\; \frac{h_{1}}{2}$$ So the actual height of the crown above the **lower support** is `h + h₁/2`. In the standard KTU textbook notation, this combined height is sometimes called `h` and h₁ is the level difference. The student should be careful with notation. **Recomputed hinge condition:** The vertical distance from the lower support to the crown is now `h_crown = h + h₁/2`. The hinge condition gives: $$H \;=\; \frac{M_{C}^{0}}{h + h_{1}/2} \;=\; \frac{M_{C}^{0}}{(2h + h_{1})/2} \;=\; \frac{2\,M_{C}^{0}}{2h + h_{1}}$$ > [!NOTE] > **KTU Convention:** Most KTU board problems use the simpler notation where `h` already represents the **crown height above the springing line** (which is the inclined line joining A and B). In that convention, the hinge condition is H = M_C^0 / h **regardless of the level difference**. The student must read the problem statement carefully and identify which `h` is meant. ### 3.6 Derivation Summary Table | Quantity | Same-Level Supports | Different-Level Supports | |----------|--------------------|--------------------------| | Vertical reactions V_A, V_B | Identical (depends only on span `l` and loads) | Identical | | Horizontal thrust H | $H = M_C^0 / h$ | $H = M_C^0 / h$ (using **crown height above lower support**) | | Bending moment M_x | $M_x = M_x^0 - H y_x$ | $M_x = M_x^0 - H y_x$ (y_x from lower support) | | Arch shape y_x | $y_x = 4h x(l-x)/l^2$ | $y_x = 4h x(l-x)/l^2 + h_1 x/l$ (if h is the parabolic-rise component) | | Slope tan θ_x | $4h(l-2x)/l^2$ | Modified due to linear term: $4h(l-2x)/l^2 + h_1/l$ | | Normal thrust N_x | $H\cos\theta + V_x\sin\theta$ | Same formula | | Radial shear S_x | $V_x\cos\theta - H\sin\theta$ | Same formula | | Horizontal reactions | $H_A = H_B = H$ | $H_A = H_B = H$ (still equal under vertical loads) | --- ## 4.
