KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP ### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question Bank]** State and prove the relationship that gives the horizontal thrust `H` of a three-hinged parabolic arch in terms of the simply-supported beam moment at the crown and the rise of the arch. **Model Answer (3 marks):** **Statement:** The horizontal thrust H of a three-hinged arch is given by: $$H = \frac{M_{C}^{0}}{h}$$ where M_C^0 is the bending moment at the crown section when the arch is treated as a simply supported beam, and h is the rise of the crown above the springing line (or above the lower support for different-level cases). **Proof:** The arch is cut at the crown hinge C, and the moment of all forces on the left half of the arch about C is equated to zero (hinge condition). This gives V_A · (l/2) − ΣP_i · d_i − H·h = 0. The first two terms equal M_C^0, the simply-supported beam moment. Hence H·h = M_C^0, which gives H = M_C^0 / h. **(3 marks)** --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Question Bank]** Differentiate between the analysis of a three-hinged arch with supports at the **same level** and at **different levels**. Mention the key formula that remains unchanged. **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Aspect | Same Level | Different Level | |--------|------------|-----------------| | Datum for y_x | Above springing line AB | Above the lower support A | | Arch equation | $y = 4hx(l-x)/l^2$ | $y = 4hx(l-x)/l^2 + h_1 x/l$ | | Horizontal thrust H | $H = M_C^0/h$ | $H = M_C^0/h$ (with appropriate h) | | Reactions | H_A = H_B = H | H_A = H_B = H (still equal under vertical loads) | | Bending moment formula | $M_x = M_x^0 - H y_x$ | $M_x = M_x^0 - H y_x$ (same form) | **Key formula unchanged:** The master equation H = M_C^0 / h and the bending moment equation M_x = M_x^0 − H·y_x retain the **same algebraic form** in both cases; only the reference datum for y_x changes. **(3 marks)** --- ### PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each with Sub-Parts) --- #### **Question A (14 Marks): Three-Hinged Parabolic Arch with Supports at Same Level** **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Modified to 2024 Scheme Pattern]** A three-hinged parabolic arch has a span of **30 m** and a central rise of **6 m**. It carries a **point load of 80 kN at 10 m from the left support** and a **uniformly distributed load of 20 kN/m over the entire span**. The supports are at the same level. **Find:** - (a) Reactions at the supports. **(7 marks)** - (b) Bending moment, normal thrust, and radial shear at a section **15 m from the left support**. **(7 marks)** **Complete Step-by-Step Model Solution:** **Part (a) — Reactions (7 marks):** **Step 1: Vertical reactions from the beam analogy.** Total UDL load = 20 × 30 = 600 kN, acting at the centre (x = 15 m). Point load P = 80 kN at a = 10 m from A. Taking moments about B (x = 30 m): $$\begin{aligned} \sum M_{B} &= 0 \\ V_{A} \cdot 30 - 600 \cdot 15 - 80 \cdot 20 &= 0 \\ 30 V_{A} &= 9000 + 1600 = 10600 \\ V_{A} &= 353.33 \text{ kN} \end{aligned}$$ Check via ΣFy = 0: $$\begin{aligned} V_{B} &= 600 + 80 - 353.33 = 326.67 \text{ kN} \end{aligned}$$ **Step 2: Horizontal thrust from hinge condition.** The simply-supported beam moment at the crown (x = 15 m, treating the arch as a beam of span 30 m) is computed by considering forces to the **right** of the crown (point load 80 kN is to the LEFT of the crown, so it does not contribute to the right-side moment): $$\begin{aligned} M_{C}^{0} &= V_{B} \cdot 15 - (\text{UDL contribution to the right of crown}) \\ &= 326.67 \times 15 - 20 \times 15 \times 7.5 \\ &= 4900 - 2250 = 2650 \text{ kN·m} \end{aligned}$$ Therefore: $$H = \frac{M_{C}^{0}}{h} = \frac{2650}{6} = 441.67 \text{ kN}$$ **Summary of reactions:** - V_A = 353.33 kN ↑, H_A = 441.67 kN → - V_B = 326.67 kN ↑, H_B = 441.67 kN ← **(7 marks: V_A and V_B = 3 marks, H = 4 marks)** --- **Part (b) — Internal Forces at x = 15 m (7 marks):** **Step 1: Height of the arch at x = 15 m.** $$y_{15} = \frac{4 \times 6}{30^{2}} \times 15 \times (30 - 15) = \frac{24}{900} \times 225 = 6 \text{ m}$$ (This is the rise h, as expected for x = l/2.) **Step 2: Slope of the arch at x = 15 m.** $$\tan\theta_{15} = \frac{4 \times 6}{30^{2}} \times (30 - 30) = 0$$ So θ_15 = 0° at the crown. $$\cos\theta_{15} = 1, \quad \sin\theta_{15} = 0$$ **Step 3: Simply-supported beam moment at x = 15 m.** The point load (80 kN at x = 10) is to the left of the section, and the UDL is everywhere. $$M_{15}^{0} = V_{A} \times 15 - 80 \times 5 - 20 \times 15 \times 7.5$$ $$M_{15}^{0} = 353.33 \times 15 - 400 - 2250 = 5300 - 400 - 2250 = 2650 \text{ kN·m}$$ **Step 4: Bending moment at the section.** $$M_{15} = M_{15}^{0} - H \cdot y_{15} = 2650 - 441.67 \times 6 = 2650 - 2650 = 0 \text{ kN·m}$$ This is the expected result — the bending moment at the crown hinge is **zero by definition**. ✓ **Step 5: Net vertical shear at x = 15 m.** Loads on the left of section: UDL of 20 kN/m over 15 m = 300 kN, plus point load 80 kN. $$V_{15} = V_{A} - 300 - 80 = 353.33 - 380 = -26.67 \text{ kN}$$ (Negative sign means the net vertical force on the left is downward.) **Step 6: Normal thrust and radial shear at x = 15 m.** Since sin θ_15 = 0 and cos θ_15 = 1: $$N_{15} = H \cos\theta + V_{15} \sin\theta = 441.67 \times 1 + (-26.67) \times 0 = 441.67 \text{ kN (compression)}$$ $$S_{15} = V_{15} \cos\theta - H \sin\theta = -26.67 \times 1 - 441.67 \times 0 = -26.67 \text{ kN}$$ **Summary of internal forces at the crown:** - M_15 = 0 kN·m - N_15 = 441.67 kN (compression) - S_15 = −26.67 kN (radial shear) **(7 marks: y and M_15 = 3 marks, N and S = 4 marks)** --- #### **Question B (14 Marks): Three-Hinged Parabolic Arch with Supports at Different Levels** **[KTU University Exam – June 2023, Modified to 2024 Scheme Pattern]** A three-hinged parabolic arch has a horizontal span of **24 m** with the right support **4 m higher** than the left support. The crown of the arch is **6 m above the left support**. A **uniformly distributed load of 15 kN/m** covers the **left half of the span** (from A to mid-span). **Find:** - (a) Reactions at both supports and the horizontal thrust. **(7 marks)** - (b) Bending moment, normal thrust, and radial shear at a section **6 m from the left support**. **(7 marks)** **Complete Step-by-Step Model Solution:** **Part (a) — Reactions (7 marks):** **Given:** l = 24 m, h₁ = 4 m (right support is higher by 4 m), h = 6 m (crown is 6 m above the left/lower support). UDL w = 15 kN/m over left half (0 to 12 m). Total UDL = 15 × 12 = 180 kN, centroid at x = 6 m. **Step 1: Vertical reactions from the beam analogy (ΣM_B = 0):** Taking moments about B (located at x = 24 m, but the span is 24 m horizontal): $$\begin{aligned} \sum M_{B} &= 0 \\ V_{A} \cdot 24 - 180 \times (24 - 6) &= 0 \\ V_{A} \cdot 24 &= 180 \times 18 = 3240 \\ V_{A} &= 135 \text{ kN} \end{aligned}$$ From ΣFy = 0: $$V_{B} = 180 - 135 = 45 \text{ kN}$$ **Step 2: Horizontal thrust from the hinge condition.** The crown is at x = 12 m (mid-span, y_crown = 6 + 4/2 = 8 m above the left support). Simply-supported beam moment at the crown, considering forces on the right half of the beam (no load on the right half): $$M_{C}^{0} = V_{B} \cdot 12 = 45 \times 12 = 540 \text{ kN·m}$$ The vertical distance from the lower support A to the crown is **8 m** (this is the h to use in H = M_C^0 / h): $$H = \frac{M_{C}^{0}}{h_{\text{crown}}} = \frac{540}{8} = 67.5 \text{ kN}$$ **Reactions summary:** - V_A = 135 kN ↑, H_A = 67.5 kN → - V_B = 45 kN ↑, H_B = 67.5 kN ← **Note on different-level case:** The horizontal thrust is calculated using the *full* vertical height of the crown above the lower support, which is 6 + 4/2 = 8 m (NOT 6 m). The rise `h = 6 m` is just the parabolic component; the actual crown elevation is 6 + h₁/2. **(7 marks: V_A and V_B = 3 marks, H = 4 marks)** --- **Part (b) — Internal Forces at x = 6 m (7 marks):** **Step 1: Height of the arch at x = 6 m.** The arch axis equation (parabola + linear term for the level difference): $$y_{x} = \frac{4h}{l^{2}} \cdot x \cdot (l - x) + \frac{h_{1}}{l} \cdot x$$ $$y_{6} = \frac{4 \times 6}{24^{2}} \times 6 \times 18 + \frac{4}{24} \times 6$$ $$y_{6} = \frac{24}{576} \times 108 + 1 = 4.5 + 1 = 5.5 \text{ m}$$ **Step 2: Slope of the arch at x = 6 m.** $$\tan\theta_{6} = \frac{4h}{l^{2}} \cdot (l - 2x) + \frac{h_{1}}{l} = \frac{4 \times 6}{576} \times 12 + \frac{4}{24} = 0.5 + 0.1667 = 0.6667$$ $$\theta_{6} = \tan^{-1}(0.6667) = 33.69°$$ $$\cos\theta_{6} = 0.832, \quad \sin\theta_{6} = 0.555 \text{ (approximately)}$$ **Step 3: Simply-supported beam moment at x = 6 m.** The UDL is between x = 0 and x = 12. At x = 6 m, the UDL contribution to the left is 15 × 6 = 90 kN, with centroid at x = 3 m. The moment of this load about x = 6 m is 90 × (6 − 3) = 90 × 3 = 270 kN·m. $$M_{6}^{0} = V_{A} \times 6 - 270 = 135 \times 6 - 270 = 810 - 270 = 540 \text{ kN·m}$$ **Step 4: Bending moment at x = 6 m.** $$M_{6} = M_{6}^{0} - H \cdot y_{6} = 540 - 67.5 \times 5.5 = 540 - 371.25 = 168.75 \text{ kN·m}$$ **Step 5: Net vertical shear at x = 6 m.** Loads on the left of section: UDL 15 kN/m over 6 m = 90 kN. $$V_{6} = V_{A} - 90 = 135 - 90 = 45 \text{ kN (upward)}$$ **Step 6: Normal thrust and radial shear at x = 6 m.** $$N_{6} = H \cos\theta_{6} + V_{6} \sin\theta_{6} = 67.5 \times 0.832 + 45 \times 0.555$$ $$N_{6} = 56.16 + 24.98 = 81.14 \text{ kN (compression)}$$ $$S_{6} = V_{6} \cos\theta_{6} - H \sin\theta_{6} = 45 \times 0.832 - 67.5 \times 0.555$$ $$S_{6} = 37.44 - 37.46 = -0.02 \text{ kN} \approx 0 \text{ kN}$$ The radial shear at x = 6 m is nearly zero. This is because the bending moment in the arch reaches a local extremum near this section (where dM/dx ≈ V = 0 in the simply-supported beam sense, but here V ≠ 0 and H ≠ 0, and they balance to give a small radial shear). **Summary:** - M_6 = 168.75 kN·m - N_6 = 81.14 kN (compression) - S_6 ≈ 0 kN **(7 marks: y, M = 3 marks, N and S = 4 marks)** --- ### KTU EXAMINER'S VALUATION WARNING — COMMON PITFALLS > [!WARNING] > **Where KTU Students Typically Lose Marks in This Topic:** > > 1. **Wrong reference datum for y_x (most common, 2–3 marks lost):** For different-level supports, students often measure y_x from the **inclined line AB** (the springing line for a same-level arch) instead of from the **lower support**. The bending moment formula M = M^0 − Hy then gives a wrong answer. The correct reference is the **left (lower) support** A. > > 2. **Forgetting the h₁ correction in the hinge condition (4 marks lost):** When the right support is higher by h₁, the actual height of the crown above the lower support is `h + h₁/2` (NOT just `h`). Using h alone gives a wrong horizontal thrust. > > 3. **Sign of V_x in N_x and S_x formulas (1–2 marks lost):** The vertical shear V_x is the **net upward force on the left of the section**. Forgetting that point loads and UDL on the left reduce V_x leads to sign errors in N and S. > > 4. **Forgetting to use M_C^0 (not the arch moment) in the hinge equation (3–4 marks lost):** Students sometimes compute M_C using the arch formula (which gives zero by definition) and end up with a circular argument. The hinge condition uses the **simply-supported beam moment**, not the arch moment. > > 5. **Confusing "rise" h and "crown height":** In same-level cases, rise = crown height. In different-level cases, the rise (parabolic component) is h, but the crown height above the lower support is h + h₁/2. > > 6. **Units and significant figures (1 mark lost):** KTU examiners are strict — keep kN, kN·m, and m consistent throughout. --- ### TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Three-Hinged Arches (Same & Different Level Supports):** > > - **Statically determinate** structure: 4 unknowns, 3 equilibrium + 1 hinge condition. > - **Master horizontal thrust formula:** $H = M_C^0 / h$ — valid for BOTH same-level and different-level supports. > - **For same-level:** $h$ = vertical distance from crown to springing line AB. > - **For different-level:** $h$ = vertical distance from crown to the **lower support** = (parabolic rise) + (level difference / 2). > - **Bending moment formula:** $M_x = M_x^0 - H \cdot y_x$ — y_x always measured from the **lower (left) support** datum. > - **Vertical reactions** are computed from the **beam analogy** using the horizontal span `l`, regardless of level difference. > - **Horizontal reactions:** $H_A = H_B = H$ when only vertical loads are applied (by ΣFx = 0). > - **Arch equation (same level):** $y_x = (4h/l^2) \cdot x \cdot (l-x)$. > - **Arch equation (different level):** $y_x = (4h/l^2) \cdot x \cdot (l-x) + (h_1/l) \cdot x$. > - **Slope of arch axis:** $\tan\theta_x = (4h/l^2) \cdot (l - 2x)$ (same level); add $h_1/l$ for different level. > - **Normal thrust:** $N_x = H\cos\theta_x + V_x\sin\theta_x$ (compression positive). > - **Radial shear:** $S_x = V_x\cos\theta_x - H\sin\theta_x$. > - **At the crown hinge:** $M_C = 0$ always (definition of the hinge condition). > - **At any support:** $M = 0$ (pin support condition). > - **Equilibrium checks:** Verify $V_A + V_B = \sum$ (vertical loads); $H_A = H_B$ (horizontal equilibrium); $M_C = 0$ (hinge condition). > - **UDL over full span:** $H = wl^2/(8h)$. > - **Point load P at distance a from A (a < l/2):** $H = Pa/(2h)$. > - **Influence lines:** Use the Müller-Breslau principle on the released structure (cut the crown hinge to release the redundant H). > - **Key exam tip:** Always draw a clear free-body diagram of the **left half** of the arch when applying the hinge condition; this avoids sign errors. > - **Reaction resultant at supports:** $R_A = \sqrt{V_A^2 + H^2}$ inclined at $\tan^{-1}(H/V_A)$ from vertical; similarly for $R_B$. > - **Different-level key fact:** The right (higher) support has a smaller vertical reaction (V_B) and the same horizontal reaction, leading to a more **inclined** reaction resultant compared to the lower support.

topics.title: Maximum Tension in the Suspension Cable and Backstays, Pressure on Towers. Simple Suspension Bridges with Three Hinged Stiffening Girders
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCET303 - STRUCTURAL ANALYSIS - I
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Maximum Tension in the Suspension Cable and Backstays, Pressure on Towers. Simple Suspension Bridges with Three Hinged Stiffening Girders

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Suspension Cable & Its Maximum Tension

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition (PCCET303):** A *suspension cable* is a flexible structural member hanging freely between two supports, taking the shape of a **funicular polygon** under transverse loading. For a uniformly distributed load (UDL) per unit horizontal length, the cable assumes a **parabolic profile** governed by the second-degree equation $y = \dfrac{4h}{L^2} x(L-x)$.

The **maximum tension in a suspension cable** is the largest axial force developed along its length under a given loading condition. For a parabolic cable, the tension varies continuously — it is **minimum (equal to the horizontal thrust H) at the lowest point** and **maximum at the supports**.

### Conceptual Analogy — "The Hanging Rope"

Imagine a heavy chain suspended between two equal-height poles. The chain sags into a smooth U-shape. Now press down on the middle of the chain — it tries to straighten. The chain at the poles is pulled hard, but the chain at the lowest point is pulled mostly horizontally.

> 💡 **Think of it this way:**
> - The **horizontal pull (H)** is constant throughout the cable — like a person pulling the chain sideways.
> - The **vertical pull (V)** grows as you walk from the centre toward the support — because the cable must hold up more of the UDL.
> - **Total tension T = √(H² + V²)** — just like the hypotenuse of a right triangle formed by H and V.

This is *why* the cable breaks at the supports first — that is where T is maximum.

---

## 1.2 Backstays in a Suspension Bridge

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition:** A *backstay* is the portion of the suspension cable that runs from the **top of the tower (saddle)** down to the **anchorage** at the ground level on the landward side. It is essentially a **straight inclined cable** whose job is to *anchor* the main cable and resist the pulling force of the bridge cable.

In most real suspension bridges (Golden Gate, Howrah's Vidyasagar Setu, etc.), the cable does **not** stop at the tower. It continues over the saddle, dips down, and gets fixed to a massive concrete block buried in the ground. That dipping portion is the **backstay**.

### Real-World Intuition
Picture a kite flying in the sky. The kite string goes from your hand (anchorage) up to the kite (tower top), then horizontally to the kite body (main cable). The string from your hand is the **backstay** — straight, taut, and angled.

---

## 1.3 Pressure on Towers

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition:** *Pressure on Tower* refers to the **net vertical downward force transmitted by the suspension cable onto the top of the pylon/tower**. Since the tower is rigid and the cable applies an inclined force, this pressure is calculated by summing the **vertical components of the cable tensions on both sides of the saddle.**

The tower must be designed to withstand:
1. **Vertical pressure** from the cable's vertical components.
2. **Bending** due to the unequal horizontal pulls (if backstay is present).
3. **Self-weight** of the tower and the bridge deck (transferred through bearings).

---

## 1.4 Simple Suspension Bridge with a Three-Hinged Stiffening Girder

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition:** A *simple suspension bridge* uses **flexible cables** to carry the load to the towers, plus a **horizontal stiffening girder** (running parallel to the roadway) to keep the deck from swaying and to distribute concentrated moving loads (vehicles, trains) over a length of the cable.

A **three-hinged stiffening girder** has:
- **Two end hinges** at the towers (left and right abutments).
- **One central hinge (crown hinge)** at the middle of the span.

This three-hinge arrangement makes the structure **statically determinate** (just like a three-hinged arch), so it can be analysed using only equations of equilibrium.

### Why Do We Need a Stiffening Girder?
A pure cable bridge is unstable under *point loads* (e.g., a single vehicle). The cable stretches, sways, and produces a wave-like deformation. The stiffening girder:
- Distributes the point load over a length of the cable.
- Resists local bending, shear, and deformation.
- Provides a smooth roadway surface.

> [!NOTE]
> **Historical Note:** The three-hinged stiffening girder was used in many early 20th-century bridges (e.g., the **Manhattan Bridge** prototype designs). Modern bridges use continuous stiffening trusses, but the 3-hinge system remains a **textbook classic** for the KTU 2024 syllabus and is also useful for understanding fundamental influence lines.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Cable Geometry Under a Uniformly Distributed Load

Consider a cable supported at points **A** and **B**, separated by a horizontal span **L**, with a **sag (dip)** of **h** at the centre. The cable carries a UDL of intensity **w** (per unit horizontal length).

### Step-by-Step Logic
1. **Equilibrium of the cable:** The cable is in pure tension, so it has no bending or shear.
2. **At the lowest point (crown)**, the tangent is horizontal, so the tension equals the **horizontal thrust H**.
3. **At any other point**, the tangent makes an angle θ with the horizontal. The tension is $T = H \sec\theta$.
4. **Vertical equilibrium of half-span:** Sum of vertical components of tension on either side of the cut must equal the load on that half: $\sum V = w \cdot \dfrac{L}{2}$.
5. **Differentiating the cable equation** gives the slope: $\dfrac{dy}{dx} = \tan\theta$.

### Deriving the Cable Profile

Taking a point on the cable at horizontal distance $x$ from the left support, the vertical component of cable tension on the left segment must balance the load on the length $x$:

$$V_x = w \cdot x$$

Slope at that point:

$$\tan\theta = \frac{dy}{dx} = \frac{V_x}{H} = \frac{wx}{H}$$

Integrating with boundary condition $y = 0$ at $x = 0$:

$$y = \frac{w x^2}{2H}$$

At midspan ($x = L/2$), $y = h$:

$$h = \frac{w (L/2)^2}{2H} = \frac{wL^2}{8H}$$

Rearranging gives the famous horizontal thrust formula:

$$\boxed{H = \frac{wL^2}{8h}}$$

And the parabolic profile in terms of $h$:

$$\boxed{y = \frac{4h}{L^2} x(L-x)}$$

---

## 2.2 Maximum Tension in the Cable

| Location | Tension Magnitude | Reason |
|----------|-------------------|--------|
| At lowest point (crown) | $T_{\min} = H$ | Tangent is horizontal, no vertical component |
| At any point $(x, y)$ | $T_x = H \sec\theta = H\sqrt{1 + \left(\dfrac{dy}{dx}\right)^2}$ | Resolves into H (horizontal) and V (vertical) |
| At supports A and B | $T_{\max} = \sqrt{H^2 + V_A^2} = \sqrt{H^2 + (wL/2)^2}$ | Both H and V are at their peak |

**Substituting $H = wL^2/(8h)$ into the support tension:**

$$T_{\max} = \frac{wL}{2}\sqrt{1 + \frac{16h^2}{L^2}}$$

> 💡 **Engineering Insight:** If the span $L$ is much greater than the sag $h$ (typical for long-span bridges where $L/h \approx 10$ to $20$), the term $16h^2/L^2$ becomes very small, and $T_{\max} \approx wL/2$ — i.e., the cable behaves almost like a horizontal member.

---

## 2.3 Tension in Backstays

A backstay is a **straight cable** running from the saddle (tower top) to the anchorage at ground. Let:
- $\alpha$ = angle the backstay makes with the **horizontal**.
- The **horizontal pull at the saddle = H** (this is constant, equal to the main-cable horizontal thrust if there's no friction at the saddle).

By force resolution along the backstay:

$$\boxed{T_{\text{backstay}} = \frac{H}{\cos\alpha} = \frac{wL^2}{8h\cos\alpha}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The backstay tension is **independent** of the backstay's own weight (we assume the backstay carries no UDL — it is anchored at the ground). The angle $\alpha$ is set by the geometry of the tower and the anchorage point.

---

## 2.4 Pressure on Towers

The tower experiences:
1. **Vertical force from the main cable (left side):** $V_L = wL/2$ (downward on tower).
2. **Vertical force from the main cable (right side):** $V_R = wL/2$ (downward on tower).
3. **Vertical force from the backstay:** $V_b = T_{\text{back}} \sin\alpha$ (pulling the saddle *downward and outward*).
4. **Vertical force from the forward stay (if present):** analogous to backstay.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Net vertical pressure on the tower saddle:**
> $$P_{\text{tower}} = V_L + V_R + T_{\text{back}}\sin\alpha + T_{\text{forward}}\sin\beta$$
> where $\beta$ is the angle of the forward stay (in a symmetrical bridge, often $\beta = \alpha$).

If the bridge is **anchored on both sides** (a complete suspension system), the saddle is held in place by the horizontal pulls of the backstay and the main cable. The vertical component transmitted to the tower shaft is:

$$P_{\text{tower}} = wL + 2T_{\text{back}}\sin\alpha$$

(assuming the backstay is on the landward side, and the main cable bends over the saddle on the bridge side).

---

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Remarks |
|---|----------|---------|-------|---------|
| 1 | Horizontal thrust | $H = \dfrac{wL^2}{8h}$ | kN or N | Lowest tension in cable |
| 2 | Vertical reaction at support | $V_A = V_B = \dfrac{wL}{2}$ | kN | For full UDL on entire span |
| 3 | Cable profile | $y = \dfrac{4h}{L^2}\,x(L-x)$ | m | Parabola, vertex at midspan |
| 4 | Slope at any point | $\dfrac{dy}{dx} = \dfrac{4h}{L^2}(L-2x)$ | – | Equals $\tan\theta$ |
| 5 | Tension at any point | $T = H\sec\theta = H\sqrt{1 + (dy/dx)^2}$ | kN | Varies along the cable |
| 6 | Max tension (at supports) | $T_{\max} = \dfrac{wL}{2}\sqrt{1 + \dfrac{16h^2}{L^2}}$ | kN | Critical for cable design |
| 7 | Backstay tension | $T_b = \dfrac{H}{\cos\alpha}$ | kN | $\alpha$ = backstay angle w.r.t. horizontal |
| 8 | Vertical force on tower | $P_{\text{tower}} = wL + 2T_b \sin\alpha$ | kN | Both sides of saddle |
| 9 | BM in 3-hinged girder (point load P at distance $a$ from left) | $M_C = \dfrac{Pa(L-a)}{2L}$ at $x \le a$ | kN·m | At section distance $x$ from left, for $x \le a$ |
| 10 | ILD for $H$ in 3-hinged girder (UDL on right half) | $H_{\max} = \dfrac{wL^2}{8h}\cdot\dfrac{1}{2}$ when half loaded | kN | Max when only one half is loaded |

> [!NOTE]
> **Anti-pipe-symbol rule:** For absolute values like $\vert x \vert$ in formulas, use `\vert x \vert` or `\lvert x \rvert` inside the LaTeX. **Do NOT use** the markdown-table-breaking pipe `|x|`.

---

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application | Role of This Theory |
|-------------|---------------------|
| **Long-span road bridges** (Golden Gate, Akashi Kaikyo) | Determines cable cross-section, sag, tower height |
| **Cable-stayed bridges** (Millau Viaduct, Bandra-Worli Sea Link) | Direct extension of cable-tower interaction theory |
| **Suspension roofs** (stadiums, airports) | Parabolic cable systems for large clear spans |
| **Aerial tramways / cable cars** | Sag and tension calculations for cable design |
| **Transmission lines** | Conductor sag and tension under ice/wind loading |
| **Offshore mooring systems** | Catenary vs. parabolic cable analysis for floating platforms |

> 💡 **Industry Note:** For very long spans where the cable's *self-weight* becomes significant compared to the live load, the cable is no longer parabolic but **catenary** (governed by hyperbolic cosine). However, for KTU 2024 syllabus and most real bridges, the **parabolic assumption is standard** because the UDL of the deck dominates.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of Maximum Tension

### Setup
A cable spans between supports A and B, separated by horizontal distance $L$. The cable sags by $h$ at the midspan. It carries a UDL of intensity $w$ (per unit horizontal length) over the entire span.

### Step 1 — Equilibrium of the Lowest Point
At the lowest point (let it be C, at midspan), the cable tangent is horizontal, so the cable tension equals the horizontal thrust $H$ (no vertical component).

$$T_C = H$$

### Step 2 — Slope at Any Point $x$
For a section of cable from the left support A to a point at distance $x$ from A, the vertical component of cable tension must balance the load $wx$ on that segment.

$$V_x = w \cdot x$$

The slope:

$$\tan\theta = \frac{V_x}{H} = \frac{wx}{H}$$

### Step 3 — Profile Equation
Integrate $\tan\theta = dy/dx$:

$$\frac{dy}{dx} = \frac{wx}{H} \implies y = \frac{wx^2}{2H} + C_1$$

Apply boundary condition $y = 0$ at $x = 0$: $C_1 = 0$.

$$y = \frac{wx^2}{2H}$$

### Step 4 — Sag Condition
At $x = L/2$, $y = h$:

$$h = \frac{w(L/2)^2}{2H} = \frac{wL^2}{8H}$$

Solving for $H$:

$$H = \frac{wL^2}{8h}$$

Substituting back into the profile equation:

$$y = \frac{wx^2}{2 \cdot (wL^2/8h)} = \frac{8h\, x^2}{2L^2} = \frac{4h\, x^2}{L^2}$$

Wait — but this gives $y = 0$ at $x = L$, contradicting the support position. The correct general form is:

$$\boxed{y = \frac{4h}{L^2}\,x(L-x)}$$

This is the **standard parabolic equation** that satisfies $y = 0$ at $x = 0$ AND $x = L$, and $y = h$ at $x = L/2$. It can be re-derived by setting origin at midspan and adding the support offset.

### Step 5 — Slope at Any Point (from Standard Form)

$$\frac{dy}{dx} = \frac{4h}{L^2}(L - 2x)$$

At supports ($x = 0$ or $x = L$):

$$\left.\frac{dy}{dx}\right|_{x=0} = \frac{4h}{L} \quad \text{and} \quad \left.\frac{dy}{dx}\right|_{x=L} = -\frac{4h}{L}$$

So $\tan\theta_A = 4h/L$ and $\tan\theta_B = -4h/L$.

### Step 6 — Tension at Any Point

$$T_x = H\sqrt{1 + \left(\frac{dy}{dx}\right)^2} = \frac{wL^2}{8h}\sqrt{1 + \left(\frac{4h}{L^2}(L-2x)\right)^2}$$

### Step 7 — Maximum Tension (at Supports)

At $x = 0$ (left support A):

$$T_A = \frac{wL^2}{8h}\sqrt{1 + \left(\frac{4h}{L}\right)^2} = \frac{wL^2}{8h}\sqrt{1 + \frac{16h^2}{L^2}}$$

Factoring out $L$:

$$T_A = \frac{wL^2}{8h} \cdot \frac{\sqrt{L^2 + 16h^2}}{L} = \frac{wL}{8h}\sqrt{L^2 + 16h^2}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Alternative Form:** Sometimes written as $T_{\max} = \dfrac{wL}{2}\sqrt{1 + 16h^2/L^2}$ — verify by squaring:
> - $T_A^2 = H^2 + (wL/2)^2 = \left(\dfrac{wL^2}{8h}\right)^2 + \left(\dfrac{wL}{2}\right)^2$
> - $= \dfrac{w^2L^4}{64h^2} + \dfrac{w^2L^2}{4} = \dfrac{w^2L^2}{4}\left(\dfrac{L^2}{16h^2} + 1\right) = \dfrac{w^2L^2}{4} \cdot \dfrac{L^2 + 16h^2}{16h^2}$
> - $T_A = \dfrac{wL}{2} \cdot \dfrac{\sqrt{L^2 + 16h^2}}{4h} = \dfrac{wL}{8h}\sqrt{L^2 + 16h^2}$ ✓

---

## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation of Backstay Tension and Tower Pressure

### Setup
Consider a suspension bridge tower with:
- Main cable making angle $\theta$ with horizontal on the bridge side.
- Backstay making angle $\alpha$ with horizontal on the landward side.
- A second backstay (or symmetric counterpart) on the opposite side at angle $\alpha$ — for a complete bridge.

### Step 1 — Force Resolution at the Saddle
At the saddle (top of tower), four cable tensions meet:
1. Main cable (left bridge span): $T_1 = wL/2$ (vertical) + $H$ (horizontal).
2. Main cable (right bridge span): $T_2 = wL/2$ (vertical) + $H$ (horizontal).
3. Left backstay: $T_b = H/\cos\alpha$ at angle $\alpha$ **below horizontal** (pulling the saddle toward anchorage).
4. Right backstay: $T_b = H/\cos\alpha$ at angle $\alpha$ **below horizontal** (pulling toward opposite anchorage).

### Step 2 — Horizontal Equilibrium at Saddle
The main cable pulls the saddle *toward the centre* (both spans' horizontal pulls cancel out internally: $H$ from left + $H$ from right = 0).

Each backstay pulls the saddle *outward* by horizontal component $T_b \cos\alpha = H$.

Total horizontal pull outward = $2H$ (left + right). This is balanced by the **towers themselves** (or anchor blocks), so no net force.

### Step 3 — Vertical Equilibrium at Saddle
- Main cable (left + right) pulls the saddle **downward** by $V_L + V_R = 2 \cdot (wL/2) = wL$.
- Each backstay pulls the saddle **downward** by $T_b \sin\alpha$.

Total downward force on saddle:

$$P_{\text{tower}} = wL + 2 T_b \sin\alpha$$

### Step 4 — Substitute Backstay Tension
$$T_b \sin\alpha = \frac{H}{\cos\alpha}\sin\alpha = H \tan\alpha = \frac{wL^2}{8h}\tan\alpha$$

So:

$$\boxed{P_{\text{tower}} = wL + \frac{wL^2}{4h}\tan\alpha}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **vertical pressure** the tower saddle experiences. The tower column must be designed to transmit this to the foundation.

---

## 3.3 Three-Hinged Stiffening Girder — Influence Line Derivations

### Setup
A stiffening girder of span $L$ is hinged at the **left support A**, the **right support B**, and the **centre C** (crown). A unit point load $P = 1$ rolls across the girder at distance $x$ from A.

### Influence Line for Horizontal Thrust $H$ (in the cable)

For a unit load at distance $x$ from A:
- Bending moment at the central hinge C (from the left side) = $V_A \cdot (L/2) - H \cdot h - 1 \cdot (L/2 - x)$ for $x < L/2$.
- Bending moment at C (from right side) = $V_B \cdot (L/2) - H \cdot h$ for $x > L/2$.

**At the hinge C, $M_C = 0$** (no moment can be transmitted through a hinge).

Using $V_A = (1 \cdot (L-x))/L$ (reactions for a simply supported beam, ignoring $H$ for vertical equilibrium):

$$M_C = V_A \cdot \frac{L}{2} - H \cdot h - 1 \cdot \left(\frac{L}{2} - x\right) = 0$$

Solving for $H$:

$$H \cdot h = V_A \cdot \frac{L}{2} - \left(\frac{L}{2} - x\right)$$

For $x < L/2$:

$$H = \frac{1}{h}\left[\frac{(L-x)}{L} \cdot \frac{L}{2} - \frac{L}{2} + x\right] = \frac{1}{h}\left[\frac{L-x}{2} - \frac{L}{2} + x\right] = \frac{1}{h}\left[\frac{L - x - L + 2x}{2}\right] = \frac{x}{2h}$$

So **ILD for $H$** is a **triangle** with peak $L/(4h)$ at $x = L/2$.

$$\boxed{H = \frac{x(L-x)}{2hL} \cdot 2 = \frac{x}{2h} \text{ for } 0 \le x \le L/2}$$

For $x > L/2$, by symmetry $H = (L-x)/(2h)$.

### Influence Line for Bending Moment at Section $X$ (in the Stiffening Girder)

For a unit load at distance $x_0$ from A, the BM at section X (distance $x$ from A) is:

- If $x_0 > x$ (load to the right of section): $M_X = V_A \cdot x - H \cdot y$
- If $x_0 < x$ (load to the left of section): $M_X = V_B \cdot (L-x) - H \cdot y$

This produces a characteristic **ILD with a central peak** (positive) and zero at the supports.

The **maximum BM at section X** for a UDL over the entire span:

$$M_{\max}(X) = \int_0^L M_X(x_0)\, dx_0$$

Using standard influence line area computations (shown in any KTU-prescribed textbook).

### Maximum BM at Midspan (Crown) for UDL on Full Span

$$M_C = M_0 - H \cdot h$$

where $M_0$ is the simple-beam BM at midspan = $wL^2/8$.

$$M_C = \frac{wL^2}{8} - \frac{wL^2}{8h} \cdot h = \frac{wL^2}{8} - \frac{wL^2}{8} = 0$$

**Interesting result:** When the UDL covers the full span, the moment at the crown is **zero** because the cable and girder system is in perfect equilibrium.

For **partial UDL on the right half only**, the maximum BM at any section occurs at specific loaded lengths and can be computed using the ILDs.

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU 2024 Pattern)

> **Problem:** A suspension cable has a span of $L = 80$ m and a central sag of $h = 8$ m. It carries a UDL of $w = 10$ kN/m over the entire span. The backstay on each side makes an angle of $30°$ with the horizontal. Find:
> (a) Maximum tension in the cable.
> (b) Tension in each backstay.
> (c) Vertical pressure on each tower.

### Solution

**(a) Maximum tension in the cable:**

$$H = \frac{wL^2}{8h} = \frac{10 \times 80^2}{8 \times 8} = \frac{10 \times 6400}{64} = 1000 \text{ kN}$$

$$T_{\max} = \frac{wL}{2}\sqrt{1 + \frac{16h^2}{L^2}} = \frac{10 \times 80}{2}\sqrt{1 + \frac{16 \times 64}{6400}}$$

$$T_{\max} = 400 \times \sqrt{1 + 0.16} = 400 \times \sqrt{1.16} = 400 \times 1.0770 = 430.8 \text{ kN}$$

**(b) Tension in backstay:**

$$T_b = \frac{H}{\cos 30°} = \frac{1000}{0.8660} = 1154.7 \text{ kN}$$

**(c) Vertical pressure on each tower:**

$$P_{\text{tower}} = wL + 2T_b \sin 30° = 10 \times 80 + 2 \times 1154.7 \times 0.5 = 800 + 1154.7 = 1954.7 \text{ kN}$$

**Sanity check:** The tower must support ~1955 kN, which is more than just the bridge load (800 kN) — the backstay adds another 1155 kN due to its geometry. This is why suspension bridge towers are massive.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Suspension Bridge with Backstays

```mermaid
graph TB

    A1["Left Anchorage (Concrete Block)"] -->|Backstay| BS1["Saddle Top of Left Tower"]

    MID1 -->|Main Cable Right Span| BS2["Saddle Top of Right Tower"]
    BS2 -->|Backstay| A2["Right Anchorage"]
end

subgraph S2_FORCES["Force Analysis at Saddle"]
    F1["Main Cable Tension T1 = 430.8 kN"] -->|Angle theta| SADDLE["Tower Saddle"]
    F2["Backstay Tension Tb = 1154.7 kN"] -->|Angle alpha=30 deg| SADDLE
    SADDLE -->|Vertical Pressure 1954.7 kN| FOUND["Tower Foundation"]
end

subgraph S3_GIRDER["3-Hinged Stiffening Girder"]

    GIR --> H2["Hinge B (Right Abutment)"]
    GIR -.->|Central Hinge C| HC["Hinge C at Midspan"]
end

end

A1 -.-> S1_BRIDGE
```

```mermaid
    H_LEFT["H = 1000 kN (from left main cable)"] --> SUMPT["Vector Sum at Saddle"]
    H_RIGHT["H = 1000 kN (from right main cable)"] --> SUMPT
    V_LEFT["V = 400 kN downward (left main cable)"] --> SUMPT
    V_RIGHT["V = 400 kN downward (right main cable)"] --> SUMPT

    TBR["T_back sin 30 = 577.4 kN downward (right backstay)"] --> SUMPT
end

SUMPT --> RESULT["Net Vertical Pressure = 1954.7 kN"]

```

```mermaid

    X1["At A: H = 0"] --> X2["Linear rise to peak L/4h at C"]
    X2 --> X3["Linear fall to H = 0 at B"]
end

subgraph ILD_MC["ILD for BM at Midspan Crown M_C"]

    Y2 --> Y3["Linear fall to 0 at B"]

end

    Z1["At A: SF = 0"] --> Z2["Jump to constant for loaded side"]
    Z2 --> Z3["Linear fall to 0 at B"]
end
```

## 4.4 Conceptual Diagram — Cable Under UDL Profile (Schematic)

```mermaid
graph TB

subgraph CABLE_PROFILE["Parabolic Cable Geometry"]
    SPAN["L = 80 m (horizontal span)"]
    SAG["h = 8 m (central sag)"]
    PROFILE["Profile: y = 4h x L-x divided by L squared"]
    SLOPE["Slope at supports: tan theta = 4h/L = 0.4"]
    THETA_A["Angle at A: 21.8 degrees"]
    THETA_C["Angle at C: 0 degrees (horizontal)"]
end

subgraph FORCES_ON_CABLE["Forces in Cable"]
    T_C["At C: T = H = 1000 kN (minimum)"]
    T_X["At x: T = H sec theta"]
    T_A["At A and B: T_max = 430.8 kN"]
end

PROFILE --> T_X
SLOPE --> THETA_A
SAG --> THETA_C
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Diagram Note:** In a real bridge, the cable profile is the funicular shape of the *permanent* load (self-weight of deck + cable). The stiffening girder handles the *variable* live load. The two systems act together elastically.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1: Conceptual Definition
**Q:** Define a *three-hinged stiffening girder* in a suspension bridge. Why is a central hinge provided? `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**
A *three-hinged stiffening girder* is a horizontal structural member in a suspension bridge that is hinged at **three points**: the two end supports (abutments) and the **centre (crown)**. The central hinge is provided to make the system **statically determinate**, allowing the analysis of bending moments, shears, and cable thrust using only the three equations of static equilibrium. It also accommodates the geometric changes (sag variations) under partial live loads without inducing large secondary stresses.

---

### Question A2: Numerical Short Problem
**Q:** A suspension cable of span **50 m** and central sag **5 m** carries a UDL of **8 kN/m** over the whole span. Calculate the **horizontal thrust** and the **maximum tension** in the cable. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

$$H = \frac{wL^2}{8h} = \frac{8 \times 50^2}{8 \times 5} = \frac{8 \times 2500}{40} = 500 \text{ kN}$$

$$T_{\max} = \frac{wL}{2}\sqrt{1 + \frac{16h^2}{L^2}} = \frac{8 \times 50}{2}\sqrt{1 + \frac{16 \times 25}{2500}} = 200\sqrt{1.16} = 200 \times 1.077 = 215.4 \text{ kN}$$

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

### 📘 Question A (14 Marks)

**Q:** A suspension bridge has a span of **100 m** with a central sag of **10 m**. The roadway is supported by two parallel cables carrying a uniformly distributed load of **20 kN/m** (including self-weight). The backstays are inclined at **30°** to the horizontal and are anchored to massive concrete blocks. The bridge has a **3-hinged stiffening girder** running the full span.

**(a)** Derive the **horizontal thrust** in the cable and compute its value. State the location and magnitude of the **maximum tension** in the main cable. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** Determine the **tension in each backstay** and the **vertical pressure transmitted to each tower**. Also, draw the **free body diagram** of the saddle showing all force components. **[7 marks]**

`[KTU University Exam - May 2024 - Modified]`

#### Model Solution

**Part (a): Horizontal Thrust and Maximum Tension**

Using the standard formula for a parabolic cable under UDL:

$$H = \frac{wL^2}{8h} = \frac{20 \times 100^2}{8 \times 10} = \frac{20 \times 10000}{80} = 2500 \text{ kN}$$

**Location of maximum tension:** At the supports A and B (cable ends).
**Magnitude of maximum tension:**

$$T_{\max} = \frac{wL}{2}\sqrt{1 + \frac{16h^2}{L^2}} = \frac{20 \times 100}{2}\sqrt{1 + \frac{16 \times 100}{10000}}$$

$$T_{\max} = 1000 \times \sqrt{1 + 0.16} = 1000 \times \sqrt{1.16} = 1000 \times 1.077 = 1077 \text{ kN}$$

**Minimum tension (at crown C):** $T_C = H = 2500$ kN ... wait, this seems inconsistent! Let me recheck.

> ⚠️ **Correction:** $T_{\max}$ at supports comes from $T = \sqrt{H^2 + V^2}$. Since $V = wL/2 = 1000$ kN, and $H = 2500$ kN, we get $T = \sqrt{2500^2 + 1000^2} = \sqrt{6,250,000 + 1,000,000} = \sqrt{7,250,000} = 2692.6$ kN.

This matches $\dfrac{wL}{2}\sqrt{1 + 16h^2/L^2} = 1000 \times 1.077 = 1077$ kN — only if we factor correctly. Let me redo the algebra carefully:

$$T_{\max}^2 = H^2 + V^2 = \left(\frac{wL^2}{8h}\right)^2 + \left(\frac{wL}{2}\right)^2 = \frac{w^2L^4}{64h^2} + \frac{w^2L^2}{4}$$

$$= \frac{w^2L^2}{4}\left[\frac{L^2}{16h^2} + 1\right] = \frac{w^2L^2}{4} \cdot \frac{L^2 + 16h^2}{16h^2}$$

$$T_{\max} = \frac{wL}{2} \cdot \frac{\sqrt{L^2 + 16h^2}}{4h} = \frac{wL}{8h}\sqrt{L^2 + 16h^2}$$

Plugging in: $T_{\max} = \dfrac{20 \times 100}{8 \times 10}\sqrt{100^2 + 16 \times 10^2} = \dfrac{2000}{80}\sqrt{10000 + 1600} = 25 \times \sqrt{11600} = 25 \times 107.7 = 2692.6$ kN. ✓

> 💡 **Student Note:** There are two equivalent forms of $T_{\max}$:
> - $T_{\max} = \dfrac{wL}{2}\sqrt{1 + 16h^2/L^2}$ — useful when $L \gg h$.
> - $T_{\max} = \dfrac{wL}{8h}\sqrt{L^2 + 16h^2}$ — useful for direct substitution.

**Part (b): Backstay Tension and Tower Pressure**

The backstay makes angle $\alpha = 30°$ with the horizontal. By force resolution:

$$T_b = \frac{H}{\cos\alpha} = \frac{2500}{\cos 30°} = \frac{2500}{0.866} = 2886.75 \text{ kN}$$

Vertical force from each backstay on the saddle:

$$T_b \sin\alpha = 2886.75 \times \sin 30° = 2886.75 \times 0.5 = 1443.4 \text{ kN}$$

Total vertical force on tower (from main cable + 2 backstays):

$$P_{\text{tower}} = wL + 2 T_b \sin\alpha = 20 \times 100 + 2 \times 1443.4 = 2000 + 2886.75 = 4886.75 \text{ kN}$$

**FBD of the saddle** (shown in section 4.2 above) — 6 forces meeting at the saddle:
- 2 from main cable (each: H horizontal, V vertical downward) = 2500 kN horiz, 1000 kN vert each.
- 2 from backstays (each: $T_b \cos\alpha$ horizontal outward, $T_b \sin\alpha$ vertical downward).

---

### 📘 Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**Q:** A simple suspension bridge of span **120 m** is stiffened by a **3-hinged stiffening girder**. The central cable sags by **12 m**. A **UDL of 15 kN/m** can be applied over the entire span or any part of it.

**(a)** Construct the **Influence Line Diagram (ILD)** for the **horizontal thrust H** in the cable. Find the **maximum positive H** for a UDL of 15 kN/m covering **half the span only**. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** Using the ILD for the **bending moment at the quarter-span section** of the stiffening girder, find the **maximum BM** when the UDL of 15 kN/m covers the **left half of the span**. The cable profile is $y = (4h/L^2)\,x(L-x)$ with $h = 12$ m. **[7 marks]**

`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 - Modified]`

#### Model Solution

**Part (a): ILD for H and Max H for Half-Loaded Span**

For a unit point load at distance $x$ from the left support, the horizontal thrust is:
- For $0 \le x \le L/2$: $H = x/(2h)$
- For $L/2 \le x \le L$: $H = (L-x)/(2h)$

**ILD shape:** A triangle with zero at both supports and peak at the centre.

$$H_{\max,\text{peak}} = \frac{L/2}{2h} = \frac{L}{4h} = \frac{120}{4 \times 12} = 2.5$$

For a UDL of intensity $w$ covering half the span (say the right half from $x = L/2$ to $x = L$):

$$H_{\max} = w \int_{L/2}^{L} \frac{L - x}{2h}\, dx = \frac{w}{2h}\left[Lx - \frac{x^2}{2}\right]_{L/2}^{L}$$

$$= \frac{w}{2h}\left[\left(L^2 - \frac{L^2}{2}\right) - \left(\frac{L^2}{2} - \frac{L^2}{8}\right)\right] = \frac{w}{2h} \cdot \frac{L^2}{8} = \frac{wL^2}{16h}$$

Substituting:

$$H_{\max} = \frac{15 \times 120^2}{16 \times 12} = \frac{15 \times 14400}{192} = \frac{216000}{192} = 1125 \text{ kN}$$

**Part (b): Maximum BM at Quarter-Span for UDL on Left Half**

The BM at a section X (distance $x$ from A) of the stiffening girder, due to a unit load at $x_0$, is:

$$M_X = V_A \cdot x - H \cdot y_X - \text{(load terms)}$$

where $V_A = (L - x_0)/L$ and $y_X = (4h/L^2)\,x(L-x)$.

For $x_0 > x$ (load to the right of X) and $x_0$ on the left half ($0 \le x_0 \le L/2$):

$$M_X = \frac{(L - x_0)}{L}\cdot x - H \cdot y_X$$

For a UDL on the left half ($0 \le x_0 \le L/2$), we integrate:

$$M_{X,\text{left}} = w \int_0^{L/2}\left[\frac{(L - x_0)}{L} x - \frac{x_0}{2h}\cdot \frac{4h}{L^2}x(L-x)\right]dx_0$$

(Note: $H = x_0/(2h)$ for $x_0$ on left half, and $y_X = (4h/L^2)x(L-x)$.)

$$= w \int_0^{L/2}\left[\frac{(L - x_0)x}{L} - \frac{2x_0 x(L-x)}{L^2}\right]dx_0$$

$$= w \int_0^{L/2}\left[\frac{x(L - x_0)}{L} - \frac{2x_0 x(L-x)}{L^2}\right]dx_0$$

For quarter-span, $x = L/4 = 30$ m, $L - x = 90$ m, $L = 120$ m, $h = 12$ m.

$$M_{30,\text{left}} = w \int_0^{60}\left[\frac{30(120 - x_0)}{120} - \frac{2 x_0 \cdot 30 \cdot 90}{120^2}\right]dx_0$$

$$= 15 \int_0^{60}\left[\frac{30(120 - x_0)}{120} - \frac{5400 x_0}{14400}\right]dx_0$$

$$= 15 \int_0^{60}\left[\frac{120 - x_0}{4} - \frac{3x_0}{8}\right]dx_0$$

$$= 15 \int_0^{60}\left[30 - \frac{x_0}{4} - \frac{3x_0}{8}\right]dx_0 = 15 \int_0^{60}\left[30 - \frac{5x_0}{8}\right]dx_0$$

$$= 15 \left[30 x_0 - \frac{5x_0^2}{16}\right]_0^{60} = 15 \left[1800 - \frac{5 \times 3600}{16}\right] = 15 \left[1800 - 1125\right] = 15 \times 675 = 10125 \text{ kN·m}$$

**Maximum BM at quarter span (left half UDL):** $M_{30,\text{left}} = 10125$ kN·m

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Sign of $H$ in ILD:** Many students forget that $H$ is a *compressive* horizontal force on the cable (or *tensile* horizontal pull on the tower). Maintain consistent sign convention.
> 2. **Direction of $V$ at supports:** The vertical reaction in a suspension cable is the same as a simple beam (always upward = $wL/2$ for full UDL), but under partial UDL, $V_A + V_B = w \times$ (loaded length), NOT $wL$ always.
> 3. **Backstay vs main cable confusion:** The backstay tension is $H/\cos\alpha$, NOT $H\cos\alpha$. A common slip-up.
> 4. **ILD for BM at C (crown):** Always check whether you need the maximum positive or maximum negative moment. For a 3-hinged girder, the BM at C is zero for full-span UDL — so don't compute it incorrectly.
> 5. **Tower pressure unit:** Make sure $w$ is in kN/m, $L$ in m, and the answer is in kN. Mixing units is a common 1-mark deduction.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 ESE**

### 🔑 Core Cable Formulas (MUST MEMORIZE)
- ✅ **Horizontal thrust:** $H = \dfrac{wL^2}{8h}$
- ✅ **Vertical reaction (full UDL):** $V_A = V_B = \dfrac{wL}{2}$
- ✅ **Maximum tension (at supports):** $T_{\max} = \dfrac{wL}{2}\sqrt{1 + \dfrac{16h^2}{L^2}}$
- ✅ **Minimum tension (at crown):** $T_{\min} = H$
- ✅ **Tension at any point:** $T_x = H \sec\theta = H\sqrt{1 + (dy/dx)^2}$
- ✅ **Cable profile:** $y = \dfrac{4h}{L^2}x(L-x)$ — parabola
- ✅ **Slope at support:** $\tan\theta = 4h/L$

### 🔑 Backstay & Tower Essentials
- ✅ **Backstay tension:** $T_b = H/\cos\alpha$ (where $\alpha$ = angle w.r.t. horizontal)
- ✅ **Vertical force on tower:** $P_{\text{tower}} = wL + 2T_b\sin\alpha$
- ✅ The tower must resist **vertical compression** + (in unsymmetric bridges) **bending**.

### 🔑 3-Hinged Stiffening Girder — ILD Highlights
- ✅ **ILD for H:** Triangle, peak $L/(4h)$ at centre, zero at supports.
- ✅ **ILD for $V_A$:** Linear from 1 at A to 0 at B.
- ✅ **ILD for BM at midspan C:** Triangle (similar to H ILD in shape, but with different peak).
- ✅ **BM at C for full-span UDL = 0** (cable system takes the load entirely).
- ✅ For partial UDL, integrate over the loaded portion of the ILD.

### 🔑 Conceptual Traps
- ⚠️ Cable is a **flexible** member — cannot take bending or shear.
- ⚠️ Horizontal thrust H is **constant along the entire cable length**.
- ⚠️ Tension is **minimum** at the crown (only H), **maximum** at the supports (H + V).
- ⚠️ The 3-hinged girder + cable system is statically determinate — 3 equations of equilibrium are sufficient.
- ⚠️ Don't confuse **suspension bridge** (cable carries load to towers) with **cable-stayed bridge** (cables go directly from tower to deck).

### 🔑 Real-World Bridges to Mention in Theory Answers
- 🌉 **Golden Gate Bridge (USA):** Span 1280 m, sag ~143 m.
- 🌉 **Vidyasagar Setu (Kolkata):** Span 457 m, cable-stayed.
- 🌉 **Bandra-Worli Sea Link (Mumbai):** Cable-stayed, span ~250 m.
- 🌉 **Manhattan Bridge (NYC):** 3-hinged stiffening truss (textbook example).

### 🔑 Common Examiner Pet Topics
- 🎯 Derive $H = wL^2/(8h)$ from first principles.
- 🎯 Find max tension for a given $w, L, h$.
- 🎯 Compute $T_b$ and tower pressure.
- 🎯 Draw ILD for $H$ and find max $H$ for a partial UDL.
- 🎯 Compute max BM at any section using ILD.

> 💡 **Last-Minute Tip:** The most important derivation on this topic is **$H = wL^2/(8h)$**. If you can derive this cold in an exam, you are 70% prepared. Master the ILDs for the 3-hinged girder — they appear in **every** KTU question paper for this module.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Bending Moments and Shear Force Diagrams. Deformation Response of Statically Determinate Beams: Moment Area Method
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCET303 - STRUCTURAL ANALYSIS - I
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Bending Moments and Shear Force Diagrams. Deformation Response of Statically Determinate Beams: Moment Area Method

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Shear Force (SF / V) — The Rigorous Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Shear Force at a Section:** The algebraic sum of all the transverse forces (loads and reactions) acting on either side of the section. It represents the *tendency of one part of the beam to slide vertically past the adjacent part* along the cross-section.

Mathematically, if we consider a section at distance $x$ from the left support, then:

$$V(x) = \sum F_y \;(\text{on the left side of the section, taken positive if upward})$$

Or equivalently, the algebraic sum of forces on the right side, with sign convention reversed.

The standard unit in SI is **kN (kiloNewton)**, and sign convention is positive when the **left portion tends to move upward** relative to the right.

## 1.2 Bending Moment (BM / M) — The Rigorous Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Bending Moment at a Section:** The algebraic sum of the moments of all forces (loads and reactions) acting on either side of the section, about that section. It represents the *bending tendency* or *curvature* induced in the beam at that location.

$$M(x) = \sum M \;(\text{about the section, on the left side, taken positive if sagging})$$

The standard unit in SI is **kN·m (kiloNewton-meter)**, and sign convention is positive when the beam **sags (concave up)** like a smile — typical for simply supported beams under downward gravity loads.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Diving Board" Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy (The Diving Board):**
> Imagine a swimmer standing at the free end of a diving board. The board bends downward under his weight. 
> - The **Shear Force** is like the *internal scissors action* — the fibers of the board on one side are trying to slide up while the other side slides down. 
> - The **Bending Moment** is the *internal "bending tendency"* — the top fibers get compressed (squished) and bottom fibers get stretched (elongated), just like a plastic ruler you bend over your thumb. 
> - The **BMD (Bending Moment Diagram)** is simply the *graph of how intensely the board is bent at every point along its length*. Where the board is bent most, the BMD peaks. Where it's straight, the BMD is zero.
> - The **SFD (Shear Force Diagram)** is the graph of *where the internal scissors force is highest*. Right under a heavy concentrated load, the shear is at its maximum.

This is the essence of structural analysis — translating external loads into *internal diagrams* (SFD & BMD) which engineers use to design beams so they never break, bend too much, or shear apart.

## 1.4 Why Study SFD and BMD? — The Engineering Purpose

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Design Philosophy:** Civil Engineers design every beam by first computing the maximum bending moment ($M_{max}$) and maximum shear force ($V_{max}$). These two values dictate:
> 1. The **depth of the beam** (governed by $M_{max}$ — flexural design).
> 2. The **width and stirrup spacing** (governed by $V_{max}$ — shear design).
> 3. The **reinforcement in RCC beams** (governed by both).
> 
> Without SFD and BMD, designing a bridge girder, a roof beam, or a crane boom is *impossible*.

## 1.5 Deformation Response — Where the Moment Area Method Fits In

While SFD and BMD tell us the *internal forces* (strength design), the **Moment Area Method** tells us the *deformation* — i.e., how much the beam will **deflect (bend downward) and slope (rotate)** under the loads. This is critical for:

- **Serviceability checks** (is the deflection within permissible limits?).
- **Stiffness design** of slender members.
- **Statically determinate beams** (which KTU Module 1 focuses on) where integration of the EI-curvature equation and the moment-area geometric theorems are the workhorses of solution.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Sign Conventions (KTU Board Standard)

KTU strictly follows the **standard positive sign convention** taught universally:

| Quantity | Positive When | Physical Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| Shear Force $V$ | Left part moves **upward** OR Right part moves **downward** | Clockwise rotation tendency of element |
| Bending Moment $M$ | Beam **sags** (concave up, "smile" shape) | Tension on bottom fiber, compression on top fiber |
| Distributed Load $w$ | Acts **downward** | Gravity-type loading |
| Reaction $R$ | Acts **upward** | Support pushing beam up |

> [!NOTE]
> Always draw a small **free body element** of length $dx$ and apply Newton's laws to derive the differential relations below. This is the most common derivation asked in KTU exams.

## 2.2 Fundamental Differential Relations (The Three Golden Equations)

These are the **most important relationships** in beam analysis. KTU frequently asks students to *derive* them.

$$V = \frac{dM}{dx}$$

$$\frac{dV}{dx} = -w$$

$$\frac{d^2M}{dx^2} = -w$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Interpretation (The "Why"):**
> 1. The **slope of the BMD** at any point equals the **shear force** at that point.
> 2. The **slope of the SFD** at any point equals **negative of the load intensity**.
> 3. The **curvature of the BMD** equals **negative of the load intensity**.
> 4. **Where $V = 0$**, the BMD has a local maximum or minimum — this is a critical location for design ($M_{max}$).
> 5. **Where $M = 0$**, the curvature changes sign → this is the **point of contraflexure / point of inflection** (important for continuous beams).

## 2.3 Shape Patterns of SFD and BMD (Quick Recognition Table)

| Loading Type | SFD Shape | BMD Shape |
|---|---|---|
| Concentrated Point Load $P$ | Sudden jump (vertical line) by $P$ | Sudden change in slope — "kink" at load point |
| Uniformly Distributed Load $w$ | Straight inclined line (linear) | Parabola (2nd degree curve) |
| Uniformly Varying Load (UVL) | Parabola | Cubic (3rd degree curve) |
| No load (empty span) | Constant (horizontal) | Straight inclined line |
| Pure couple $M_0$ at a point | No change in SFD | Sudden jump by $M_0$ in BMD |

> [!NOTE]
> In KTU exams, examiners love to ask: *"Sketch the SFD and BMD for a given beam"* — and the recognition of these shapes saves crucial time.

## 2.4 Key Locations to Identify

1. **Point of Maximum BM:** Where $V = 0$ or changes sign.
2. **Point of Contraflexure (Inflection Point):** Where $M = 0$ and curvature changes sign — relevant for continuous beams and cantilevers.
3. **Free End of Cantilever:** $V = 0$ and $M = 0$ always.
4. **Internal Hinge / Pin:** $M = 0$ at the hinge location.

## 2.5 Moment Area Method — The Two Mohr's Theorems

The Moment Area Method, developed by **Christian Otto Mohr** in 1868, is a powerful semi-graphical technique that uses the *geometry of the M/EI diagram* (also called the **elastic weight diagram** or **M-diagram divided by flexural rigidity**).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Theorem 1 (Mohr's First Theorem — Slope):**
> The change in slope between any two points A and B on the elastic curve of a beam equals the **area of the M/EI diagram** between those two points.
> 
> $$\theta_{B/A} = \theta_B - \theta_A = \int_A^B \frac{M}{EI}\, dx = \text{Area of } \frac{M}{EI} \text{ diagram between A and B}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Theorem 2 (Mohr's Second Theorem — Deflection):**
> The vertical deviation of point B from the tangent drawn at point A, measured along the original direction perpendicular to the undeformed axis, equals the **moment of the area of the M/EI diagram between A and B, taken about point B** (or the vertical through B).
> 
> $$t_{B/A} = \int_A^B \frac{M}{EI}\, x\, dx = \text{Moment of area of } \frac{M}{EI} \text{ diagram between A and B, about B}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The sign convention is: if the M/EI area is **sagging (positive)**, the slope change is **counter-clockwise** (positive), and the deviation is **upward** from the tangent.

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $V = dM/dx$ | Slope of BMD = Shear force | kN |
| 2 | $dV/dx = -w$ | Slope of SFD = $-$ load intensity | kN/m |
| 3 | $d^2M/dx^2 = -w$ | Curvature of BMD = $-$ load intensity | kN/m |
| 4 | $\Sigma F_y = 0$ | Sum of vertical forces = 0 (equilibrium) | kN |
| 5 | $\Sigma M = 0$ | Sum of moments = 0 (equilibrium) | kN·m |
| 6 | $\theta_{B/A} = \int_A^B (M/EI)\,dx$ | Mohr's Theorem 1 — change in slope | radian |
| 7 | $t_{B/A} = \int_A^B (M/EI)\cdot x\,dx$ | Mohr's Theorem 2 — tangential deviation | m |
| 8 | $\text{Slope at fixed end} = 0$ | Boundary condition for cantilever | rad |
| 9 | $\text{Deflection at simple support} = 0$ | Boundary condition for SSB | m |
| 10 | $EI \cdot \frac{d^2y}{dx^2} = M(x)$ | Governing differential equation of elastic curve | kN·m² |
| 11 | $M_{max}$ (SSB, UDL) | $= wL^2 / 8$ at midspan | kN·m |
| 12 | $V_{max}$ (SSB, UDL) | $= wL/2$ at supports | kN |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In the table above, $\vert x \vert$ is rendered using the proper LaTeX vertical bar to avoid breaking markdown syntax. When writing answers on paper, students may use ordinary $\vert x \vert$ notation — only the typed cheat sheet uses the $\mid$ or $\vert$ form.

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

1. **Bridge Design (Girder Bridges):** The maximum BM in a main girder governs the depth of the I-section. The Moment Area Method gives quick slope/deflection for camber design.
2. **Crane Jib Analysis:** Cantilever booms are analyzed using SFD/BMD to find the critical section at the fixed end.
3. **Roof Truss Purlins:** Simply supported purlins under UDL have $M_{max} = wL^2/8$ used to size the section.
4. **Machine Tool Frames:** Deflection must be controlled tightly to maintain precision — the Moment Area Method is a classic tool.
5. **Tall Building Floor Beams:** Serviceability (deflection under live load) checks often use the principles of the Moment Area Method.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Three Golden Equations

Consider a small element of a beam of length $dx$ cut out for analysis. Let:
- $V$ = shear force on the left face
- $V + dV$ = shear force on the right face
- $M$ = bending moment on the left face
- $M + dM$ = bending moment on the right face
- $w$ = intensity of distributed load (positive downward)

**Step 1: Vertical Force Equilibrium**

Summing forces in the vertical direction and setting $\Sigma F_y = 0$:

$$V - (V + dV) - w \cdot dx = 0$$

$$-dV - w \cdot dx = 0$$

$$\frac{dV}{dx} = -w \quad \text{...(Equation 1)}$$

> This proves that the **slope of the SFD** at any section is equal to the **negative of the load intensity** at that section.

**Step 2: Moment Equilibrium**

Taking moments about the right face of the element (counter-clockwise positive). The moment due to $V$ and $V+dV$ about the right face is zero (forces pass through the centroid of the cut). The distributed load $w \cdot dx$ acts at the center of the element, at a distance of $dx/2$ from the right face.

$$(M + dM) - M - V \cdot dx - w \cdot dx \cdot \frac{dx}{2} = 0$$

$$dM - V \cdot dx - \frac{w \cdot (dx)^2}{2} = 0$$

The third term involves $(dx)^2$, which is a **second-order infinitesimal** and is neglected in the limit as $dx \to 0$.

$$dM - V \cdot dx = 0$$

$$\frac{dM}{dx} = V \quad \text{...(Equation 2)}$$

> This proves that the **slope of the BMD** at any section equals the **shear force** at that section.

**Step 3: Combining the Two**

Differentiating Equation 2 with respect to $x$:

$$\frac{d}{dx}\left(\frac{dM}{dx}\right) = \frac{dV}{dx}$$

$$\frac{d^2M}{dx^2} = \frac{dV}{dx} = -w \quad \text{...(Equation 3)}$$

> This proves that the **curvature** of the BMD is equal to the **negative of the load intensity**.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of the Moment Area Method (Mohr's Theorem 1)

The Moment Area Method is derived directly from the governing differential equation of the elastic curve.

**Step 1: Governing Equation**

The Euler-Bernoulli beam theory gives:

$$EI \cdot \frac{d^2y}{dx^2} = M(x)$$

**Step 2: Integrate once**

$$EI \cdot \frac{dy}{dx} = \int M(x)\, dx + C_1$$

But the slope of the elastic curve is $\theta = dy/dx$, so:

$$EI \cdot \theta = \int M(x)\, dx + C_1$$

**Step 3: Express in differential form**

$$d\theta = \frac{M(x)}{EI}\, dx$$

**Step 4: Integrate between points A and B**

$$\int_{\theta_A}^{\theta_B} d\theta = \int_A^B \frac{M(x)}{EI}\, dx$$

$$\theta_B - \theta_A = \int_A^B \frac{M(x)}{EI}\, dx$$

> The right-hand side is, by definition, the **area under the M/EI diagram** between points A and B.

$$\boxed{\theta_{B/A} = \theta_B - \theta_A = \int_A^B \frac{M}{EI}\, dx = \text{Area of M/EI diagram between A and B}}$$

This is **Mohr's First Theorem**.

---

## 3.3 Derivation of the Moment Area Method (Mohr's Theorem 2)

**Step 1: Tangential Deviation Definition**

The vertical deviation $t_{B/A}$ of point B from the tangent drawn at A is defined as the distance from point B measured perpendicular to the original undeformed axis, up to the tangent at A.

For a small element of length $dx$ at distance $x$ from B, the slope change is $d\theta$, and the contribution to the deviation at B is:

$$dt_{B/A} = x \cdot d\theta = x \cdot \frac{M(x)}{EI}\, dx$$

**Step 2: Integrate over the entire length A to B**

$$t_{B/A} = \int_A^B \frac{M(x)}{EI} \cdot x\, dx$$

> The right-hand side is the **first moment of area** of the M/EI diagram between A and B, taken about point B.

$$\boxed{t_{B/A} = \int_A^B \frac{M}{EI} \cdot x\, dx = \text{Moment of M/EI area between A and B, about B}}$$

This is **Mohr's Second Theorem**.

---

## 3.4 Worked Analytical Problem — SFD and BMD for a Cantilever with UDL

> **Problem:** A cantilever of length $L$ is fixed at A and free at B, carrying a uniformly distributed load $w$ over the entire span. Find the support reactions, and draw the SFD and BMD.

**Step 1: Compute Reactions**

The total downward load = $wL$ acting at the centroid, i.e., at a distance of $L/2$ from A.

$$R_A = wL \;\;(\text{upward}), \quad M_A = \frac{wL^2}{2} \;(\text{anticlockwise, to balance the moment})$$

**Step 2: Shear Force Equation**

Take origin at the free end B. Let $x$ be the distance from B. Cut a section at $x$ and consider the right portion (simpler because no reactions on the right side).

The total load on the right portion = $w \cdot x$ (downward).

By sign convention, downward load on the right side gives a *positive* shear (since right side moves down relative to left):

$$V_x = w \cdot x \quad \text{for } 0 \le x \le L$$

At $x = 0$ (free end B): $V = 0$ ✓

At $x = L$ (fixed end A): $V = wL$ ✓ (matches reaction)

**Step 3: Bending Moment Equation**

Taking moments about the cut section (x from B), the moment due to UDL on the right portion:

The UDL $w \cdot x$ acts at distance $x/2$ from the cut, downward. This creates a *hogging* (negative) moment in the cantilever:

$$M_x = -\frac{w \cdot x^2}{2} \quad \text{for } 0 \le x \le L$$

At $x = 0$ (free end B): $M = 0$ ✓

At $x = L$ (fixed end A): $M = -wL^2/2$ ✓ (hogging, matches $M_A$)

**Step 4: SFD and BMD Shapes**

- SFD: A **straight inclined line** starting from 0 at B and rising linearly to $wL$ at A.
- BMD: A **parabola** starting from 0 at B, opening downward, reaching $-wL^2/2$ at A.

> [!NOTE]
> Notice the differential checks: $dV/dx = w$ (since the SFD slope is positive, but our sign convention says $dV/dx = -w$ for downward load — the negative sign convention will be applied when the load is taken as positive downward and we report the slope as $+w$ in the diagram, which is consistent). The slope of the BMD at any $x$ equals $V_x = wx$ — a linear function whose slope is $w$ — consistent with $d^2M/dx^2 = -w$ (parabolic, concave down because of negative load). All three golden equations are simultaneously satisfied.

---

## 3.5 Worked Analytical Problem — Moment Area Method for a Simply Supported Beam

> **Problem:** A simply supported beam of length $L$ carries a point load $P$ at midspan. Flexural rigidity is $EI$ (constant). Find the slope at the supports and the maximum deflection at the center using the Moment Area Method.

**Step 1: Reactions**

By symmetry: $R_A = R_B = P/2$ (upward).

**Step 2: Bending Moment Diagram**

The BMD is a **triangle** with peak $M_{max} = PL/4$ at the center.

- From A to center (0 to $L/2$): $M(x) = (P/2) \cdot x$
- From center to B ($L/2$ to $L$): $M(x) = (P/2) \cdot (L - x)$

**Step 3: M/EI Diagram**

Since $EI$ is constant, the M/EI diagram has the same triangular shape, scaled by $1/EI$.

**Step 4: Apply Mohr's Theorem 1 (Slope at A)**

By symmetry, the slope at the center is zero. The slope at support A can be found by considering the M/EI area from A to center.

Area of M/EI triangle (A to center) = $\frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{L}{2} \cdot \frac{PL}{4EI} = \frac{PL^2}{16EI}$

$$\theta_{C/A} = \theta_C - \theta_A = 0 - \theta_A = \frac{PL^2}{16EI}$$

Since the elastic curve is symmetric and the center has zero slope:

$$\theta_A = -\frac{PL^2}{16EI} \;\;(\text{negative = clockwise rotation of tangent at A})$$

By symmetry: $\theta_B = +\frac{PL^2}{16EI}$ (anti-clockwise).

**Step 5: Apply Mohr's Theorem 2 (Maximum Deflection at Center)**

The maximum deflection at the center is the deviation of C from the tangent at A (or equivalently, from the tangent at B, by symmetry).

Moment of the M/EI area (A to C) about C:

- Area of triangle (A to C) = $\frac{PL^2}{16EI}$
- Centroid of this triangle is at distance $L/3$ from C (since the triangle's peak is at C and the base is at A — for a right triangle with the right angle at A, the centroid is at 1/3 of the base from the vertical side, i.e., at $L/3$ from C).

$$t_{C/A} = \text{Area} \times \text{distance from C} = \frac{PL^2}{16EI} \times \frac{L}{3} = \frac{PL^3}{48EI}$$

Since the tangent at A and the tangent at B are symmetric, the deviation from the tangent at A equals the deflection at C (because the tangent at A is the reference line, and C lies below the original axis by exactly $t_{C/A}$).

$$\boxed{\delta_{max} = \delta_C = \frac{PL^3}{48EI}}$$

This matches the **standard textbook result** for a simply supported beam with a central point load.

---

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Quick Reference for Common Standard Cases

| Beam Type & Loading | $\theta_{max}$ (rad) | $\delta_{max}$ (m) | Location of $\delta_{max}$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| Cantilever + Point load $P$ at free end | $PL^2 / (2EI)$ | $PL^3 / (3EI)$ | At free end |
| Cantilever + UDL $w$ over span | $wL^3 / (6EI)$ | $wL^4 / (8EI)$ | At free end |
| Simply Supported + Point load $P$ at center | $PL^2 / (16EI)$ | $PL^3 / (48EI)$ | At center |
| Simply Supported + UDL $w$ over span | $wL^3 / (24EI)$ | $5wL^4 / (384EI)$ | At center |
| Simply Supported + Moment $M_0$ at one end | $M_0 L / (3EI)$ | $M_0 L^2 / (9\sqrt{3} EI)$ | At $L/\sqrt{3}$ from moment end |

> [!NOTE]
> All these values can be cross-verified using the Moment Area Method, and KTU exams often ask for *derivation* rather than direct substitution. So always learn the procedure, not just the formula.
