```mermaid
    A4b["Moment at C is zero by hinge"]
    A4c["Gives H = M_beam_at_C / h"]
    A4 --> A4a
    A4 --> A4b
    A4 --> A4c

    A5["Support B - Right Hinge"]:::support
    A5a["Vertical reaction V_B (upward)"]
    A5b["Horizontal reaction H (towards left, into arch)"]
    A5c["Resultant R_B at angle theta_B from vertical"]
    A5 --> A5a
    A5 --> A5b
    A5 --> A5c

    classDef title fill:#fff4cc,stroke:#c89b00,color:#000
    classDef support fill:#d6e9ff,stroke:#0050b3,color:#000
    classDef section fill:#ffe7d6,stroke:#cc5500,color:#000
    classDef internal fill:#d9f7be,stroke:#389e0d,color:#000
    classDef crown fill:#ffd6e7,stroke:#c41d7f,color:#000
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block — Circular Arch Geometry and Force Resolution

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    G1["Span L and Rise h given"]:::input
    G2["Compute radius r = (L^2 + 4h^2) / 8h"]:::calc
    G3["Compute central half-angle alpha from sin_alpha = L / (2r)"]:::calc
    G4["Equation of arc: y = (h - r) + sqrt(r^2 - (x - L/2)^2)"]:::calc
    G5["Slope tan_phi = (L/2 - x) / sqrt(r^2 - (x - L/2)^2)"]:::calc

    G1 --> G2
    G2 --> G3
    G2 --> G4
    G4 --> G5

    G5 --> R1["Resolve V_beam and H along arch axis"]:::res
    R1 --> R2["Normal thrust N along tangent"]:::res
    R1 --> R3["Radial shear V perpendicular to tangent"]:::res
    R1 --> R4["Bending moment M from internal equilibrium"]:::res

    classDef input fill:#fff4cc,stroke:#c89b00,color:#000
    classDef calc fill:#d6e9ff,stroke:#0050b3,color:#000
    classDef res fill:#d9f7be,stroke:#389e0d,color:#000
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Block — Algorithm for Solving a Three-Hinged Arch Problem

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1["Start: Span L, Rise h, Loading known"]:::st
    S2["Draw equivalent simply supported beam"]:::st
    S3["Find V_A and V_B using static equilibrium"]:::st
    S4["Compute M_beam at crown x = L/2"]:::st
    S5["Compute H = M_beam_at_crown / h"]:::key
    S6["Choose section D at distance x from A"]:::st
    S7["Compute y and tan_phi for chosen arch shape"]:::st
    S8["Compute M_beam and V_beam at section D"]:::st
    S9["Compute M, V, N using formulae 9, 10, 11"]:::key
    S10["Report H, R_A, R_B and internal forces at D"]:::st

    S1 --> S2 --> S3 --> S4 --> S5
    S5 --> S6 --> S7 --> S8 --> S9 --> S10

    classDef st fill:#e6f7ff,stroke:#1890ff,color:#000
    classDef key fill:#fff7e6,stroke:#d46b08,color:#000
```

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Q1, 3 Marks]**
> **Q.** *Define a three-hinged arch. Why is the horizontal thrust an additional unknown and how is it determined?*
>
> **Model Answer:**
> A *three-hinged arch* is a curved rib with two hinges at the springings (supports) A and B and a third hinge at the **crown C**. The number of reaction components is $3 + 2 = 5$ (each support has a vertical and a horizontal reaction, plus the crown hinge adds no reactions but one extra equation). Equilibrium of the whole arch gives 3 equations, and equilibrium of either half (say A to C) provides 2 more — total of **5 equations for 5 unknowns**, so it is **statically determinate**.
>
> The horizontal thrust $H$ is *additional* compared with a simply supported beam. It is determined by applying the **zero-moment condition at the crown hinge**:
>
> $$H = \dfrac{M_{C}^{\text{beam}}}{h}$$
>
> where $M_{C}^{\text{beam}}$ is the simple-beam bending moment at mid-span and $h$ is the central rise.

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Q2, 3 Marks]**
> **Q.** *For a three-hinged parabolic arch of span $L$ and rise $h$ carrying a UDL $w$ over the entire span, show that the bending moment is zero at every section.*
>
> **Model Answer:**
> The horizontal thrust from the funicular calculation is $H = wL^{2}/(8h)$.
> The bending moment in the equivalent simple beam at any section $x$ is:
>
> $$M^{\text{beam}}(x) = \dfrac{wL}{2}\cdot x - \dfrac{wx^{2}}{2} = \dfrac{wx}{2}(L-x)$$
>
> The arch equation gives $y = \dfrac{4h}{L^{2}}x(L-x)$, so:
>
> $$H\cdot y = \dfrac{wL^{2}}{8h}\cdot\dfrac{4h}{L^{2}}x(L-x) = \dfrac{wx}{2}(L-x)$$
>
> Hence, $M^{\text{arch}}(x) = M^{\text{beam}}(x) - H\cdot y = 0$ for all $x$ — the parabola is the **funicular shape for a UDL**.

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> - **Forgetting the $H\cdot y$ term** when computing BM at an intermediate section — most common, costs **3–4 marks** per question.
> - **Using the *parabolic* equation for a *circular* arch** (or vice-versa) — check the problem statement twice.
> - **Confusing the central half-angle with the support tangent angle** in a circular arch. They are equal *only* by construction; the support tangent is perpendicular to the radius OA.
> - **Wrong sign on $\sin\phi$ for the right half of the arch** — by KTU convention $\phi$ is positive on the left half (rising), negative on the right half (falling).
> - **Skipping the resultant reaction calculation** $R_{A} = \sqrt{V_{A}^{2}+H^{2}}$ when the question explicitly asks for "reactions at supports" — the *inclined* reactions are required, not just $V_{A}, V_{B}, H$.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — Three-Hinged Parabolic Arch (UDL on Half Span) + Three-Hinged Circular Arch (Point Load)**

> **[KTU University Exam — Module 4, 14 Marks]**

**(a)** A three-hinged **parabolic** arch has a span of $30\;\text{m}$ and a central rise of $6\;\text{m}$. It carries a uniformly distributed load of $30\;\text{kN/m}$ over the **left half** of the span. Determine:
1. The horizontal thrust and the vertical reactions.
2. The bending moment, normal thrust and radial shear at a section $10\;\text{m}$ from the left support A. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** A three-hinged **circular** arch of span $20\;\text{m}$ and rise $5\;\text{m}$ carries a concentrated load of $100\;\text{kN}$ at a distance of $5\;\text{m}$ from the left support. Calculate:
1. The horizontal thrust and the resultant reactions at the supports.
2. The values of normal thrust and radial shear at the section under the load. **[7 Marks]**

#### **Model Solution to Q.A(a)**

**Step 1 — Equivalent beam analysis (UDL on left half).**

Total load $= 30 \times 15 = 450\;\text{kN}$, acting at the centroid $7.5\;\text{m}$ from A.

$$
V_{A} = \dfrac{30 \times 15 \times (30 - 7.5)}{30} = \dfrac{450 \times 22.5}{30} = 337.5\;\text{kN}
$$

$$
V_{B} = \dfrac{450 \times 7.5}{30} = 112.5\;\text{kN}
$$

**Step 2 — Horizontal thrust from crown-moment condition.**

The simple-beam BM at $x = L/2 = 15\;\text{m}$ (the BM expression switches because $x = 15$ is the *edge* of the UDL):

$$
M_{C}^{\text{beam}} = V_{A} \times 15 - w \times 15 \times \dfrac{15}{2} = 337.5 \times 15 - 30 \times 15 \times 7.5
$$

$$
M_{C}^{\text{beam}} = 5062.5 - 3375 = 1687.5\;\text{kN}\cdot\text{m}
$$

$$
H = \dfrac{1687.5}{6} = 281.25\;\text{kN}
$$

**Step 3 — Internal forces at $x = 10\;\text{m}$.**

Arch ordinate: $y = \dfrac{4 \times 6}{30^{2}} \times 10 \times 20 = \dfrac{24}{900} \times 200 = 5.333\;\text{m}$

Slope: $\tan\phi = \dfrac{4 \times 6}{30^{2}}(30 - 2 \times 10) = \dfrac{24}{900} \times 10 = 0.2667$
$\Rightarrow \phi = 14.93^{\circ};\;\sin\phi = 0.2576;\;\cos\phi = 0.9662$

Simple beam values at $x = 10$:
$M^{\text{beam}} = 337.5 \times 10 - 30 \times 10 \times 5 = 3375 - 1500 = 1875\;\text{kN}\cdot\text{m}$
$V^{\text{beam}} = 337.5 - 30 \times 10 = 37.5\;\text{kN}$

$$
M = M^{\text{beam}} - H \cdot y = 1875 - 281.25 \times 5.333 = 1875 - 1500 = 375\;\text{kN}\cdot\text{m}
$$

$$
N = V^{\text{beam}}\sin\phi + H\cos\phi = 37.5 \times 0.2576 + 281.25 \times 0.9662 = 9.66 + 271.74 = 281.4\;\text{kN}
$$

$$
V = V^{\text{beam}}\cos\phi - H\sin\phi = 37.5 \times 0.9662 - 281.25 \times 0.2576 = 36.23 - 72.45 = -36.22\;\text{kN}
$$

#### **Model Solution to Q.A(b)**

**Step 1 — Reactions and horizontal thrust.**

For the equivalent beam:
$V_{A} = \dfrac{100 \times (20 - 5)}{20} = 75\;\text{kN}, \quad V_{B} = \dfrac{100 \times 5}{20} = 25\;\text{kN}$

Crown BM at $x = 10\;\text{m}$ (load is on the left, so $a = 5 \le L/2 = 10$):

$$
M_{C}^{\text{beam}} = \dfrac{W \cdot a}{2} = \dfrac{100 \times 5}{2} = 250\;\text{kN}\cdot\text{m}
$$

$$
H = \dfrac{250}{5} = 50\;\text{kN}
$$

Resultant reactions:
$R_{A} = \sqrt{75^{2} + 50^{2}} = \sqrt{5625 + 2500} = \sqrt{8125} = 90.14\;\text{kN}$
$R_{B} = \sqrt{25^{2} + 50^{2}} = \sqrt{625 + 2500} = \sqrt{3125} = 55.90\;\text{kN}$

**Step 2 — Geometry of the circular arch.**

$$
r = \dfrac{20^{2} + 4 \times 5^{2}}{8 \times 5} = \dfrac{400 + 100}{40} = 12.5\;\text{m}
$$

**Step 3 — Internal forces at $x = a = 5\;\text{m}$.**

Ordinate:
$$
y = (h - r) + \sqrt{r^{2} - (x - L/2)^{2}} = (5 - 12.5) + \sqrt{12.5^{2} - (5 - 10)^{2}}
$$
$$
y = -7.5 + \sqrt{156.25 - 25} = -7.5 + \sqrt{131.25} = -7.5 + 11.456 = 3.956\;\text{m}
$$

Slope:
$$
\tan\phi = \dfrac{L/2 - x}{\sqrt{r^{2} - (x - L/2)^{2}}} = \dfrac{5}{11.456} = 0.4365
$$
$\phi = 23.57^{\circ};\;\sin\phi = 0.4;\;\cos\phi = 0.9165$

Simple beam at $x = 5$ (left of load): $V^{\text{beam}} = V_{A} = 75\;\text{kN}$, $M^{\text{beam}} = 75 \times 5 = 375\;\text{kN}\cdot\text{m}$

$$
M = M^{\text{beam}} - H \cdot y = 375 - 50 \times 3.956 = 375 - 197.8 = 177.2\;\text{kN}\cdot\text{m}
$$

$$
N = 75 \times 0.4 + 50 \times 0.9165 = 30 + 45.83 = 75.83\;\text{kN}
$$

$$
V = 75 \times 0.9165 - 50 \times 0.4 = 68.74 - 20 = 48.74\;\text{kN}
$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

> **[KTU University Exam — Module 4, 14 Marks]**

**(a)** A three-hinged **parabolic** arch has a span of $24\;\text{m}$ and a rise of $4\;\text{m}$. A concentrated load of $80\;\text{kN}$ acts at $8\;\text{m}$ from the left support. Determine:
1. The horizontal thrust and the bending moment at the section under the load.
2. The normal thrust and radial shear at the crown. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** A three-hinged **circular** arch of span $16\;\text{m}$ and rise $4\;\text{m}$ carries a UDL of $20\;\text{kN/m}$ over the **entire** span. Determine:
1. The vertical reactions, horizontal thrust and resultant support reactions.
2. The bending moment, normal thrust and radial shear at $4\;\text{m}$ from the left support. **[7 Marks]**

#### **Model Solution to Q.B(a)**

**Step 1 — Reactions.** For the equivalent beam:
$V_{A} = \dfrac{80 \times 16}{24} = 53.33\;\text{kN}, \quad V_{B} = \dfrac{80 \times 8}{24} = 26.67\;\text{kN}$

**Step 2 — Horizontal thrust.** $a = 8 \le L/2 = 12$, so:

$$
H = \dfrac{80 \times 8}{2 \times 4} = 80\;\text{kN}
$$

**Step 3 — BM at the section under the load ($x = 8\;\text{m}$).**

$y = \dfrac{4 \times 4}{24^{2}} \times 8 \times 16 = \dfrac{16}{576} \times 128 = 3.556\;\text{m}$
$M^{\text{beam}} = 53.33 \times 8 = 426.67\;\text{kN}\cdot\text{m}$

$$
M = 426.67 - 80 \times 3.556 = 426.67 - 284.44 = 142.22\;\text{kN}\cdot\text{m}
$$

**Step 4 — At the crown ($x = 12\;\text{m}$).**

$\tan\phi = \dfrac{4 \times 4}{24^{2}}(24 - 24) = 0 \Rightarrow \phi = 0$
$y = h = 4\;\text{m}$

Beam values: $V^{\text{beam}} = 53.33 - 0 = 53.33\;\text{kN}$ (the load at $x=8$ is to the left, so it has been "passed"). Wait — re-check: at $x = 12$ we are to the right of the load, so $V^{\text{beam}} = 53.33 - 80 = -26.67\;\text{kN}$.

$M^{\text{beam}} = 53.33 \times 12 - 80 \times 4 = 640 - 320 = 320\;\text{kN}\cdot\text{m}$

But by hinge, $M_{\text{crown}} = M^{\text{beam}} - H \cdot h = 320 - 80 \times 4 = 0$ ✓ (sanity check passes).

$$
N = V^{\text{beam}}\sin 0 + H \cos 0 = 0 + 80 \times 1 = 80\;\text{kN}
$$

$$
V = V^{\text{beam}}\cos 0 - H\sin 0 = -26.67 \times 1 - 0 = -26.67\;\text{kN}
$$

#### **Model Solution to Q.B(b)**

**Step 1 — Geometry.**
$$
r = \dfrac{16^{2} + 4 \times 4^{2}}{8 \times 4} = \dfrac{256 + 64}{32} = 10\;\text{m}
$$

**Step 2 — Reactions and H.**
$V_{A} = V_{B} = wL/2 = 20 \times 16/2 = 160\;\text{kN}$
$H = wL^{2}/(8h) = 20 \times 16^{2}/(8 \times 4) = 5120/32 = 160\;\text{kN}$
$R_{A} = R_{B} = \sqrt{160^{2} + 160^{2}} = 160\sqrt{2} = 226.27\;\text{kN}$

**Step 3 — Internal forces at $x = 4\;\text{m}$.**

Ordinate:
$$
y = (4 - 10) + \sqrt{10^{2} - (4 - 8)^{2}} = -6 + \sqrt{100 - 16} = -6 + \sqrt{84} = -6 + 9.165 = 3.165\;\text{m}
$$

Slope:
$\tan\phi = \dfrac{8 - 4}{\sqrt{84}} = \dfrac{4}{9.165} = 0.4365$
$\phi = 23.57^{\circ};\;\sin\phi = 4/10 = 0.4;\;\cos\phi = \sqrt{84}/10 = 0.9165$

Beam values: $V^{\text{beam}} = 160 - 20 \times 4 = 80\;\text{kN}$, $M^{\text{beam}} = 160 \times 4 - 20 \times 4 \times 2 = 640 - 160 = 480\;\text{kN}\cdot\text{m}$

$$
M = 480 - 160 \times 3.165 = 480 - 506.4 = -26.4\;\text{kN}\cdot\text{m}
$$

$$
N = 80 \times 0.4 + 160 \times 0.9165 = 32 + 146.64 = 178.64\;\text{kN}
$$

$$
V = 80 \times 0.9165 - 160 \times 0.4 = 73.32 - 64 = 9.32\;\text{kN}
$$

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Pin This!)**

- [x] A **three-hinged arch** is statically determinate: 5 reactions, 5 equations (3 for whole arch + 2 for half).
- [x] The **master formula** $H = M_{C}^{\text{beam}} / h$ works for *both* parabolic and circular arches — the difference between the two shapes shows up only at sections **other than** the crown.
- [x] Parabolic shape $y = \dfrac{4h}{L^{2}}x(L-x)$ is the **funicular shape for UDL** — gives $M = 0$, $V = 0$ everywhere under full-span UDL.
- [x] Circular shape radius: $r = \dfrac{L^{2} + 4h^{2}}{8h}$ — *not* the funicular shape for UDL.
- [x] Point-load H formula: $H = \dfrac{W}{2h}\cdot\min(a,\,L-a)$ — pick the **shorter** segment to the load.
- [x] Full-span UDL H formula: $H = \dfrac{wL^{2}}{8h}$ — same for both arch types.
- [x] Always compute $M^{\text{beam}}$ first (ignoring the arch curvature), then **subtract $H\cdot y$** to get $M^{\text{arch}}$.
- [x] Slope of parabolic arch tangent: $\tan\phi = \dfrac{4h}{L^{2}}(L-2x)$. At crown $\phi = 0$; at supports $\tan\phi = \pm 4h/L$.
- [x] Slope of circular arch tangent: $\tan\phi = \dfrac{(L/2 - x)}{\sqrt{r^{2} - (x - L/2)^{2}}}$. At supports, $\tan\alpha = L/[2(r-h)]$.
- [x] Resolve $V^{\text{beam}}$ and $H$ along/perp to arch axis: $N = V^{\text{beam}}\sin\phi + H\cos\phi$ (compression positive); $V = V^{\text{beam}}\cos\phi - H\sin\phi$.
- [x] Resultant reaction: $R = \sqrt{V^{2} + H^{2}}$, inclined at $\tan^{-1}(H/V)$ from the vertical.
- [x] The arch is *efficient* only when the loading closely matches the funicular shape — the parabolic arch with UDL is the textbook example.
- [x] **Common KTU pitfalls:** forgetting $H\cdot y$ in BM; mixing up the two arch equations; sign errors on $\sin\phi$ in the right half of the span; omitting the resultant-reaction step.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Bending Moment
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCET303 - STRUCTURAL ANALYSIS - I
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Bending Moment

# Module 4 | Bending Moment

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Bending Moment (BM)** at any section of a structural member (beam) is defined as the **algebraic sum of the moments of all the forces** (loads and reactions) acting on either side of that section, about a point lying on the section itself (typically the centroidal axis of the cross-section).

Mathematically, for a section at a distance $x$ from a reference origin, considering the forces to the **left** of the section:

$$M_x = \sum (\text{Forces to the left}) \times (\text{their perpendicular distance from the section})$$

Or equivalently, considering forces to the **right** of the section:

$$M_x = \sum (\text{Forces to the right}) \times (\text{their perpendicular distance from the section})$$

The **SI unit** of bending moment is the **Newton-metre (N·m)** or more commonly for civil/structural engineering, the **kilonewton-metre (kN·m)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (PCCET303 - Module 4):**
> Bending moment is the cornerstone concept that connects applied loads, support reactions, and the internal resistance of a beam. Mastery of BM sign convention, point of contraflexure, and the BM-Shear-Load differential relationship ($\frac{dM}{dx}=V$, $\frac{dV}{dx}=-w$) is **mandatory** for solving statically determinate beam problems.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are holding a **wooden metre scale** (ruler) horizontally with both hands and a friend places a heavy book in the middle. You will feel the ruler **bending** downward, and the fibres on the **top surface stretch** (tension) while the fibres on the **bottom surface compress**. This internal "bending tendency" at any cross-section is quantified as the **Bending Moment**.

**Real-world analogy — The Diving Board:**

A diving board fixed at one end (cantilever) — when a diver stands at the free end, the board bends downward. The **internal bending moment** at any cross-section of the board equals the weight of the diver multiplied by the distance from that cross-section to the diver. The further the cross-section is from the load, the larger the bending moment.

**Why does this matter?**
- It tells the structural engineer **how much a beam will bend**.
- It dictates the **design of cross-section dimensions** (depth, width, reinforcement).
- A high BM means the beam needs a **deeper section** or **stronger material**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Simple Rule of Thumb (Intuition Builder):**
> - **Sagging BM (Positive):** Beam smiles 😊 → bottom fibres in tension, top in compression.
> - **Hogging BM (Negative):** Beam frowns ☹ → top fibres in tension, bottom in compression.
> - The **magnitude** of BM = "how hard the beam is being bent" at that section.

---

### 1.3 Standard Sign Convention (KTU Board-Exam Standard)

| Moment Type | Sign | Physical Effect on Beam | Fibre Behaviour |
|:------------|:----:|:-----------------------|:----------------|
| **Sagging (Positive)** | + | Concave upward (smiles) | **Bottom** in tension, **top** in compression |
| **Hogging (Negative)** | − | Concave downward (frowns) | **Top** in tension, **bottom** in compression |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Tip:** When you write $M$ values in your answer scripts, **always** indicate the sign explicitly. Examiners will deduct marks for ambiguous sign usage. A common phrasing is: *"BM at section XX is $+20$ kN·m (sagging)"* or *"$-15$ kN·m (hogging)"*.

---

### 1.4 Difference Between Bending Moment and Shear Force

| Parameter | Shear Force (SF / V) | Bending Moment (BM / M) |
|:----------|:--------------------|:------------------------|
| **Definition** | Algebraic sum of transverse forces on either side of the section | Algebraic sum of moments of forces about the section |
| **Physical meaning** | Tendency to **slide** one part of beam past the other | Tendency to **bend** (rotate) the cross-section |
| **Unit** | kN (or N) | kN·m (or N·m) |
| **Diagram** | Shear Force Diagram (SFD) | Bending Moment Diagram (BMD) |
| **Sign convention** | Left-up/Right-down = Positive (typical) | Sagging = Positive |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Differential Relationships — The Holy Trinity of Beam Analysis

The three fundamental relationships that govern beam behaviour are derived from elementary equilibrium and calculus. These are the **most heavily tested** relations in KTU examinations.

#### Relationship 1: Load Intensity and Shear Force

Consider a small element of beam of length $dx$ carrying a uniformly distributed load $w$ (kN/m, taken positive downward). The shear forces on the left and right faces are $V$ and $V + dV$ respectively. Vertical equilibrium gives:

$$\sum F_y = 0 \implies V - (V + dV) - w \cdot dx = 0$$

Therefore:

$$\boxed{\dfrac{dV}{dx} = -w}$$

> **Physical interpretation:** The **slope of the SFD** at any section equals the **negative of the load intensity** at that section.

#### Relationship 2: Shear Force and Bending Moment

Taking moments about the right face of the element (counter-clockwise positive), and neglecting the moment of the infinitesimal load $w \cdot dx$ about the section (as it is a higher-order infinitesimal):

$$\sum M = 0 \implies M + V \cdot dx - (M + dM) = 0$$

Therefore:

$$\boxed{\dfrac{dM}{dx} = V}$$

> **Physical interpretation:** The **slope of the BMD** at any section equals the **shear force** at that section.

#### Relationship 3: Load and Bending Moment (Second-Order)

Differentiating the second relation and substituting the first:

$$\dfrac{d^2M}{dx^2} = \dfrac{dV}{dx} = -w$$

$$\boxed{\dfrac{d^2M}{dx^2} = -w}$$

> **Physical interpretation:** The **curvature** of the BMD is related to the **negative of load intensity**. This dictates the shape (parabolic, linear, constant) of the BMD.

---

### 2.2 The Six "Golden Rules" of BMD/SFD Construction

These rules are used by KTU board examiners as instant marking shortcuts. Memorise them.

| Rule | Statement | Implication for Diagrams |
|:-----|:----------|:------------------------|
| **Rule 1** | $\frac{dV}{dx} = -w$ | SFD slope = $-w$; if $w$ is constant → SFD is **linear**; if $w$ is zero → SFD is **horizontal/constant** |
| **Rule 2** | $\frac{dM}{dx} = V$ | BMD slope at any point = SFD value at that point |
| **Rule 3** | $\frac{d^2M}{dx^2} = -w$ | BMD is parabolic (degree 2 in $x$) for UDL; cubic for UVL; linear for point loads |
| **Rule 4** | **SF is zero** where **BM is maximum or minimum** (local extremum) | Used to locate dangerous sections |
| **Rule 5** At a **concentrated load** $P$ | SFD has a **vertical jump of magnitude $P$** | BMD has a **kink (sharp change in slope)** |
| **Rule 6** At a **concentrated moment** $M_0$ | BMD has a **vertical jump of magnitude $M_0$** | SFD is **unaffected** (continuous) |

---

### 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet — Standard Loading Cases (Simply Supported Beam of span $L$)

> **CRITICAL FORMATTING NOTE:** All formula entries below are written using $\vert$ only for absolute value separation to keep markdown tables valid.

| Loading Type | Reaction at each support | Maximum BM ($M_{max}$) | Location of $M_{max}$ | Nature of BMD |
|:-------------|:-------------------------|:-----------------------|:----------------------|:--------------|
| **Point Load $P$ at centre** | $\frac{P}{2}, \frac{P}{2}$ | $\frac{PL}{4}$ | At midspan ($x = L/2$) | Triangular (linear) |
| **Point Load $P$ at distance $a$ from left** | $R_A = \frac{Pb}{L}$, $R_B = \frac{Pa}{L}$ | $\frac{Pab}{L}$ | At the load point | Two triangles (kinked) |
| **Uniformly Distributed Load (UDL) $w$ over full span** | $\frac{wL}{2}, \frac{wL}{2}$ | $\frac{wL^2}{8}$ | At midspan | Parabolic |
| **Uniformly Varying Load (UVL) zero at one end, $w_0$ at other** | $\frac{w_0 L}{6}, \frac{w_0 L}{3}$ | $\frac{w_0 L^2}{9\sqrt{3}} \approx 0.1282 \, w_0 L^2$ | At $x = \frac{L}{\sqrt{3}}$ | Cubic (parabola of degree 3) |
| **Two equal point loads $P$ at $L/3$ from each support** | $P, P$ | $\frac{PL}{3}$ | Constant between the two loads | Trapezoidal (flat plateau) |
| **Central point moment $M_0$** | $\frac{M_0}{L}, \frac{M_0}{L}$ (vertical reactions form a couple) | At the point of application of moment | Discontinuity in BMD | Linear (kinked) |

---

### 2.4 Point of Contraflexure (Point of Inflection)

A **point of contraflexure** (or **inflection point**) is a section in a beam where the **bending moment is zero** AND **changes sign** as we move along the beam. At such a point, the curvature of the beam changes direction — the beam switches from sagging to hogging (or vice versa).

**Engineering significance:**
- It marks the boundary between regions where the **top fibres are in tension** and regions where the **bottom fibres are in tension**.
- Critical for **reinforced concrete design** — main reinforcement placement depends on which fibres are in tension.
- In **continuous beams**, points of contraflexure appear near the intermediate supports.

**How to locate it:**
Set $M(x) = 0$ for $0 < x < L$ and confirm the sign changes on either side.

---

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application | Why Bending Moment Matters |
|:------------|:---------------------------|
| **Reinforced Concrete Beams** | BM dictates the area of steel reinforcement ($A_{st}$) in the tension zone |
| **Steel Girders in Bridges** | Maximum BM governs the selection of I-section (depth, flange width) |
| **Machine Shafts** | Fatigue failure from rotating bending moments is a primary design check |
| **Aircraft Wings** | Distributed aerodynamic loads produce BM that wing spars must resist |
| **Crane Jibs** | Cantilever bending moment governs counterweight design |
| **Pile Foundations** | Lateral BM from soil pressure dictates pile section and depth |

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of $\frac{dM}{dx} = V$ and $\frac{dV}{dx} = -w$ (First Principles)

Consider an infinitesimal element of a beam of length $dx$ cut out between sections $x$ and $x+dx$.

**Forces and moments acting on the element:**

- Shear force on the left face: $V$ (acts upward by convention on the left face)
- Shear force on the right face: $V + dV$ (acts downward on the right face by Newton's third law)
- Bending moment on the left face: $M$ (acts counter-clockwise on the left face)
- Bending moment on the right face: $M + dM$ (acts clockwise on the right face)
- Distributed load: $w \cdot dx$ acting downward (where $w$ is in kN/m)

**Step 1: Vertical equilibrium ($\sum F_y = 0$)**

$$V - (V + dV) - w \cdot dx = 0$$

$$-dV - w \cdot dx = 0$$

$$\frac{dV}{dx} = -w \quad \blacksquare$$

**Step 2: Moment equilibrium about the right face ($\sum M_{right} = 0$, counter-clockwise positive)**

$$M - (M + dM) + V \cdot dx - (w \cdot dx) \cdot \frac{dx}{2} = 0$$

The term $(w \cdot dx) \cdot \frac{dx}{2}$ is of the order $dx^2$ and is therefore **negligible** in the limit $dx \to 0$:

$$-dM + V \cdot dx = 0$$

$$\frac{dM}{dx} = V \quad \blacksquare$$

**Step 3: Combining both relations**

Differentiate $\frac{dM}{dx} = V$ with respect to $x$:

$$\frac{d^2 M}{dx^2} = \frac{dV}{dx}$$

Substitute $\frac{dV}{dx} = -w$:

$$\frac{d^2 M}{dx^2} = -w \quad \blacksquare$$

---

### 3.2 Derivation of BM for a Simply Supported Beam with UDL

**Setup:** Simply supported beam of span $L$ carrying a UDL of intensity $w$ (kN/m) over its entire length.

**Step 1: Find reactions (by symmetry)**

$$R_A = R_B = \frac{wL}{2}$$

**Step 2: Section the beam at distance $x$ from the left support A. Consider forces to the left.**

$$M_x = R_A \cdot x - w \cdot x \cdot \frac{x}{2}$$

The UDL $w \cdot x$ acts at the centroid of the distributed load, which is at $\frac{x}{2}$ from the section:

$$M_x = \frac{wL}{2} \cdot x - \frac{wx^2}{2}$$

$$\boxed{M_x = \frac{wx}{2}(L - x)}$$

**Step 3: Locate maximum BM**

Differentiate $M_x$ with respect to $x$ and equate to zero:

$$\frac{dM_x}{dx} = \frac{wL}{2} - wx = 0$$

$$x = \frac{L}{2}$$

**Step 4: Calculate maximum BM**

Substitute $x = L/2$:

$$M_{max} = \frac{w \cdot (L/2)}{2} \left(L - \frac{L}{2}\right) = \frac{wL}{4} \cdot \frac{L}{2} = \frac{wL^2}{8}$$

$$\boxed{M_{max} = \frac{wL^2}{8} \quad \text{(at midspan)}}$$

---

### 3.3 Derivation of BM for Simply Supported Beam with Point Load Off-Centre

**Setup:** Simply supported beam AB of span $L$, point load $P$ acting at distance $a$ from A and $b$ from B (where $a + b = L$).

**Step 1: Reactions**

Taking moments about B:

$$R_A \cdot L = P \cdot b \implies R_A = \frac{Pb}{L}$$

Taking moments about A:

$$R_B \cdot L = P \cdot a \implies R_B = \frac{Pa}{L}$$

**Step 2: BM at section between A and load (i.e., $0 \leq x \leq a$)**

Considering forces to the left of section at distance $x$:

$$M_x = R_A \cdot x = \frac{Pb \cdot x}{L}$$

$$\boxed{M_x = \frac{Pbx}{L} \quad (0 \leq x \leq a)}$$

**Step 3: BM at section between load and B (i.e., $a \leq x \leq L$)**

Considering forces to the **right** of section at distance $x$ (this is easier):

$$M_x = R_B \cdot (L - x) = \frac{Pa}{L}(L - x)$$

$$\boxed{M_x = \frac{Pa(L-x)}{L} \quad (a \leq x \leq L)}$$

**Step 4: BM at the load point ($x = a$)**

Substituting $x = a$ in the first expression:

$$M_C = \frac{Pb \cdot a}{L} = \frac{Pab}{L}$$

$$\boxed{M_{max} = \frac{Pab}{L} \quad \text{(at point of application of load)}}$$

**Step 5: Location of maximum BM**

Since BM is linear on both sides of the load and reaches its peak exactly at the load (kink in BMD), the maximum occurs at $x = a$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Special case:** When $a = b = L/2$ (load at centre), $M_{max} = \frac{P \cdot (L/2) \cdot (L/2)}{L} = \frac{PL}{4}$, which matches the standard result. This serves as a **sanity check**.

---

### 3.4 Derivation of BM for a Cantilever with UDL

**Setup:** Cantilever beam of span $L$ fixed at A, free at B, carrying a UDL of $w$ kN/m over the full length.

**Step 1: Reactions at fixed end A**

$$R_A = wL \quad (\text{upward})$$
$$M_A = \frac{wL^2}{2} \quad (\text{counter-clockwise, fixing moment})$$

**Step 2: BM at section $x$ from the free end B**

This is the most convenient choice. Considering forces to the **right** of the section (i.e., from the free end up to the section):

The UDL over length $x$ is $w \cdot x$, acting at $\frac{x}{2}$ from the section.

$$M_x = -w \cdot x \cdot \frac{x}{2} = -\frac{wx^2}{2}$$

$$\boxed{M_x = -\frac{wx^2}{2} \quad (\text{negative, hogging})}$$

**Step 3: Maximum BM (at fixed end, $x = L$)**

$$M_{max} = -\frac{wL^2}{2} \quad (\text{at fixed end A})$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The negative sign throughout indicates **hogging** (top fibres in tension) — characteristic of cantilever beams under downward loads. This is a key differentiator from simply supported beams.

---

### 3.5 Derivation of BM for Simply Supported Beam with UVL (Triangular Load, Zero at A, Maximum $w_0$ at B)

**Setup:** SSB AB of span $L$. Load varies linearly from $0$ at A to $w_0$ at B.

**Load function:** At distance $x$ from A, the load intensity is:

$$w(x) = \frac{w_0}{L} \cdot x$$

**Step 1: Reactions**

Total load $W = \frac{1}{2} w_0 L$, acting at centroid $\frac{2L}{3}$ from A.

Taking moments about A:

$$R_B \cdot L = \frac{w_0 L}{2} \cdot \frac{2L}{3} \implies R_B = \frac{w_0 L}{3}$$

$$R_A = \frac{w_0 L}{2} - \frac{w_0 L}{3} = \frac{w_0 L}{6}$$

**Step 2: BM at section $x$ from A**

Considering forces to the left of section at $x$:

The triangular load up to $x$ has intensity $w(x) = \frac{w_0 x}{L}$ at $x$, and $0$ at A. Its total magnitude is $\frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{w_0 x}{L} \cdot x = \frac{w_0 x^2}{2L}$, acting at centroid $\frac{2x}{3}$ from A (i.e., $\frac{x}{3}$ from the section).

$$M_x = R_A \cdot x - \frac{w_0 x^2}{2L} \cdot \frac{x}{3}$$

$$M_x = \frac{w_0 L}{6} \cdot x - \frac{w_0 x^3}{6L}$$

$$\boxed{M_x = \frac{w_0}{6L}(L^2 x - x^3) = \frac{w_0 x}{6L}(L^2 - x^2)}$$

**Step 3: Locate $M_{max}$**

Differentiate $M_x$ and equate to zero:

$$\frac{dM_x}{dx} = \frac{w_0}{6L}(L^2 - 3x^2) = 0$$

$$L^2 - 3x^2 = 0 \implies x = \frac{L}{\sqrt{3}}$$

**Step 4: Maximum BM**

Substitute $x = \frac{L}{\sqrt{3}}$:

$$M_{max} = \frac{w_0}{6L} \cdot \frac{L}{\sqrt{3}} \left(L^2 - \frac{L^2}{3}\right) = \frac{w_0}{6L} \cdot \frac{L}{\sqrt{3}} \cdot \frac{2L^2}{3}$$

$$M_{max} = \frac{w_0 L^2}{9\sqrt{3}} \approx 0.06415 \, w_0 L^2$$

$$\boxed{M_{max} = \frac{w_0 L^2}{9\sqrt{3}} \approx 0.1282 \cdot \frac{w_0 L^2}{2} \quad \text{at } x = \frac{L}{\sqrt{3}}}$$

---

### 3.6 Algebraic Verification of $\frac{d^2M}{dx^2} = -w$ for the UVL Case

For the UVL derivation above:

$$\frac{dM_x}{dx} = \frac{w_0}{6L}(L^2 - 3x^2)$$

$$\frac{d^2 M_x}{dx^2} = \frac{w_0}{6L}(-6x) = -\frac{w_0 x}{L} = -w(x) \quad \checkmark$$

The second derivative equals the negative of the load intensity, **confirming** the fundamental relationship.

---

### 3.7 Summary of Derived Expressions

| Beam Type | BM Equation $M(x)$ | $M_{max}$ | Location |
|:----------|:-------------------|:----------|:---------|
| SSB, point load $P$ at centre | $\frac{Px}{2}$ for $x \leq L/2$ | $\frac{PL}{4}$ | $x = L/2$ |
| SSB, UDL $w$ | $\frac{wx}{2}(L - x)$ | $\frac{wL^2}{8}$ | $x = L/2$ |
| SSB, point load $P$ at distance $a$ | $\frac{Pbx}{L}$ for $x \leq a$ | $\frac{Pab}{L}$ | $x = a$ |
| Cantilever, UDL $w$ (from free end) | $-\frac{wx^2}{2}$ | $-\frac{wL^2}{2}$ | Fixed end |
| SSB, UVL (0 at A, $w_0$ at B) | $\frac{w_0 x}{6L}(L^2 - x^2)$ | $\frac{w_0 L^2}{9\sqrt{3}}$ | $x = L/\sqrt{3}$ |

### 4.1 Sign Convention for Bending Moment (Mermaid Block Diagram)

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the sign convention flow for bending moment analysis.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Beam Section under Analysis"] --> B{"Nature of\nCurvature?"}
    B -- "Smile shape\nConcave up" --> C["Sagging BM\nPOSITIVE Sign"]
    B -- "Frown shape\nConcave down" --> D["Hogging BM\nNEGATIVE Sign"]
    C --> E["Bottom fibres in TENSION\nTop fibres in COMPRESSION"]
    D --> F["Top fibres in TENSION\nBottom fibres in COMPRESSION"]
    E --> G["Reinforcement placed at BOTTOM\nfor RC beams"]
    F --> H["Reinforcement placed at TOP\nfor RC beams"]
    G --> I["Example: Simply Supported\nBeam with downward load"]
    H --> J["Example: Cantilever beam\nfixed at left free at right"]
    I --> K["M is POSITIVE in SFD BMD plot"]
    J --> L["M is NEGATIVE in SFD BMD plot"]
```

This diagram outlines the **systematic procedure** for constructing the bending moment diagram for any determinate beam, following the KTU board exam methodology.

```mermaid
    S3 --> S4["Step 4: Apply dM by dx equals V rule\nto verify SF and BM consistency"]
    S4 --> S5["Step 5: Locate Mmax by setting dM by dx = 0\nor where V changes sign"]

    S6 --> S7["Step 7: Identify Points of Contraflexure\nwhere Mx = 0 and changes sign"]
    S7 --> S8["Step 8: Indicate sign convention explicitly\nSagging positive and Hogging negative"]
    S8 --> DONE["BMD Construction Complete\nReady for Design Verification"]
```

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**Define Bending Moment. State the sign convention used for bending moment in a beam. Also mention the SI unit.**

**Model Answer:**

The bending moment at any section of a beam is defined as the **algebraic sum of the moments of all forces** (loads and reactions) acting on either side of the section, taken about a point on the section itself.

**Sign Convention:**
- **Sagging bending moment** (concave upward, beam shape like a smile 😊) is taken as **positive**.
- **Hogging bending moment** (concave downward, beam shape like a frown ☹) is taken as **negative**.

**SI Unit:** The SI unit of bending moment is the **Newton-metre (N·m)**. In civil engineering practice, the more practical unit is the **kilonewton-metre (kN·m)**.

**[3 Marks Breakdown: 1 mark definition + 1.5 marks sign convention + 0.5 mark unit]**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**State and prove the relationship between bending moment, shear force, and load intensity for a beam.**

**Model Answer:**

Consider an infinitesimal element of beam of length $dx$. Let $V$ and $V + dV$ be the shear forces on the left and right faces respectively. Let $M$ and $M + dM$ be the bending moments on the left and right faces. Let $w$ be the intensity of the uniformly distributed load acting downward.

**Vertical equilibrium ($\sum F_y = 0$):**

$$V - (V + dV) - w \, dx = 0 \implies \dfrac{dV}{dx} = -w$$

**Moment equilibrium about the right face ($\sum M = 0$):**

$$M - (M + dM) + V \, dx = 0 \implies \dfrac{dM}{dx} = V$$

**Combining both:**

$$\dfrac{d^2 M}{dx^2} = -w$$

These are the three fundamental differential relationships of beam analysis.

**[3 Marks Breakdown: 1 mark setup + 1 mark derivation of each relationship + 1 mark combined form]**

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

---

**Question A (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam – May 2023, Modified]**

A simply supported beam AB of span **6 m** carries a uniformly distributed load of **20 kN/m** over the entire span and a concentrated load of **30 kN** at a distance of **2 m** from the left support A.

**(a)** Calculate the support reactions at A and B. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Draw the Shear Force Diagram (SFD) and Bending Moment Diagram (BMD), clearly indicating the values at all critical points. Also locate the point of contraflexure, if any. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Question A:

**Part (a): Support Reactions**

Let $R_A$ and $R_B$ be the vertical reactions at A and B respectively. Total UDL = $20 \times 6 = 120$ kN, acting at the centre (3 m from A).

**Vertical equilibrium:**

$$R_A + R_B = 120 + 30 = 150 \text{ kN}$$

**Moment equilibrium about A ($\sum M_A = 0$, counter-clockwise positive):**

$$R_B \times 6 - 30 \times 2 - 120 \times 3 = 0$$

$$6 R_B = 60 + 360 = 420$$

$$R_B = 70 \text{ kN}$$

**Therefore:**

$$R_A = 150 - 70 = 80 \text{ kN}$$

$$\boxed{R_A = 80 \text{ kN}, \quad R_B = 70 \text{ kN}}$$

---

**Part (b): SFD and BMD Construction**

The beam is divided into two regions:
- **Region I:** $0 \leq x \leq 2$ m (from A to load point C)
- **Region II:** $2 \leq x \leq 6$ m (from C to B)

**Region I (0 ≤ x ≤ 2):**

Shear force at section $x$:

$$V_x = R_A - w \cdot x = 80 - 20x$$

- At $x = 0^+$: $V = 80$ kN
- At $x = 2^-$: $V = 80 - 40 = 40$ kN

Bending moment at section $x$:

$$M_x = R_A \cdot x - w \cdot x \cdot \dfrac{x}{2} = 80x - 10x^2$$

- At $x = 0$: $M = 0$
- At $x = 2$: $M = 80(2) - 10(4) = 160 - 40 = 120$ kN·m

**Region II (2 ≤ x ≤ 6):**

Shear force at section $x$:

$$V_x = R_A - w \cdot x - 30 = 80 - 20x - 30 = 50 - 20x$$

- At $x = 2^+$: $V = 50 - 40 = 10$ kN
- At $x = 6^-$: $V = 50 - 120 = -70$ kN

**Point of zero shear in Region II:**

$$50 - 20x = 0 \implies x = 2.5 \text{ m}$$

Bending moment at section $x$:

$$M_x = 80x - 10x^2 - 30(x - 2)$$

- At $x = 2$: $M = 120$ kN·m (matches Region I ✓)
- At $x = 2.5$: $M = 80(2.5) - 10(6.25) - 30(0.5) = 200 - 62.5 - 15 = 122.5$ kN·m
- At $x = 6$: $M = 80(6) - 10(36) - 30(4) = 480 - 360 - 120 = 0$ ✓

**Maximum bending moment** in this beam:

$$M_{max} = 122.5 \text{ kN·m at } x = 2.5 \text{ m (from A)}$$

**Point of Contraflexure:**

Check if $M_x = 0$ at any interior point:
- Region I: $80x - 10x^2 = 0 \implies x(80 - 10x) = 0 \implies x = 0$ or $x = 8$ (outside range)
- Region II: $M_x = 80x - 10x^2 - 30(x - 2) = -10x^2 + 50x + 60$

Setting this to zero: $-10x^2 + 50x + 60 = 0 \implies x^2 - 5x - 6 = 0 \implies (x - 6)(x + 1) = 0$

Roots: $x = 6$ (at support B) and $x = -1$ (extraneous, outside beam).

**Therefore, no point of contraflexure exists in this beam** — the bending moment is positive (sagging) throughout the span. ✓

**SFD Critical Values:**
- Just right of A: $+80$ kN
- Just left of C: $+40$ kN
- Just right of C: $+10$ kN
- At zero shear point ($x = 2.5$ m): $0$ kN
- Just left of B: $-70$ kN

**BMD Critical Values:**
- At A: $0$ kN·m
- At C ($x = 2$ m): $+120$ kN·m
- At max point ($x = 2.5$ m): $+122.5$ kN·m
- At B: $0$ kN·m

$$\boxed{M_{max} = 122.5 \text{ kN·m at } x = 2.5 \text{ m from A}}$$

---

#### OR

---

**Question B (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Modified]**

A cantilever beam of span **4 m** is fixed at the left end A and free at the right end B. It carries a point load of **25 kN** at the free end B and a uniformly distributed load of **10 kN/m** over the entire span.

**(a)** Determine the reactions at the fixed end A. **(4 marks)**

**(b)** Draw the Shear Force Diagram (SFD) and Bending Moment Diagram (BMD) for the beam, labelling all critical values. Determine the magnitude and location of maximum bending moment. **(10 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Question B:

**Part (a): Reactions at Fixed End A**

Total downward load = Point load + UDL = $25 + (10 \times 4) = 25 + 40 = 65$ kN

Vertical reaction at A: $R_A = 65$ kN (upward)

Fixing moment at A (taking moments of all downward loads about A):

$$M_A = 25 \times 4 + 10 \times 4 \times \dfrac{4}{2} = 100 + 80 = 180 \text{ kN·m (counter-clockwise to resist)}$$

$$\boxed{R_A = 65 \text{ kN (upward)}, \quad M_A = 180 \text{ kN·m}}$$

---

**Part (b): SFD and BMD Construction**

Let $x$ be the distance measured from the **free end B** (this is the conventional choice for cantilevers, as it makes equations simpler).

**Shear Force at section $x$ from B** (consider forces to the right of the section, which includes the UDL over $x$ and the point load at B):

$$V_x = -[25 + 10x] = -(25 + 10x)$$

- At $x = 0$ (free end B): $V = -25$ kN
- At $x = 4$ (fixed end A): $V = -(25 + 40) = -65$ kN

**Bending Moment at section $x$ from B** (hogging, hence negative):

$$M_x = -\left[25 \cdot x + 10 \cdot x \cdot \dfrac{x}{2}\right] = -(25x + 5x^2)$$

- At $x = 0$ (free end B): $M = 0$
- At $x = 4$ (fixed end A): $M = -(100 + 80) = -180$ kN·m

**Maximum BM:** Since $|M_x|$ increases monotonically with $x$ (both terms are positive functions of $x$), the **maximum magnitude occurs at the fixed end A**:

$$|M_{max}| = 180 \text{ kN·m (hogging)} \text{ at } x = 4 \text{ m from B (i.e., at A)}$$

**SFD Shape:** Linear, varying from $-25$ kN at B to $-65$ kN at A (constant slope = $-w = -10$ kN/m ✓).

**BMD Shape:** Parabolic, varying from $0$ at B to $-180$ kN·m at A (concave down, second derivative = $-w = -10$ kN/m² ✓).

$$\boxed{M_{max} = 180 \text{ kN·m (hogging) at the fixed end A}}$$

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes that Cost Marks in KTU Board Exams:**
>
> 1. **Sign Error in BM Equations:** Forgetting the negative sign in cantilever BM (hogging is negative). If you write $M = +\frac{wx^2}{2}$ for a cantilever, expect **−2 to −3 marks** deduction.
> 2. **Units Omitted:** Writing "122.5" without units (kN·m) — minimum **−0.5 to −1 mark**.
> 3. **No Sign in BMD:** Plotting BM values without "+" or "−" labels — examiners interpret the **direction of plot** (above/below the datum line) as a substitute, but the safe practice is to label explicitly.
> 4. **Point of Contraflexure Not Checked:** If a question says "locate the point of contraflexure if any", many students skip checking whether $M_x$ actually changes sign. Always verify — if no such point exists, **state this clearly** to score the mark allocated to this step.
> 5. **Reaction Calculation Error Propagation:** A wrong reaction propagates through SFD and BMD, losing marks at every subsequent step. **Verify** reactions using $\sum F_y = 0$ **and** $\sum M = 0$ **both** before proceeding.
> 6. **Confusing Sagging with Hogging for SSB vs Cantilever:** SSB with downward load gives **sagging** (positive); Cantilever with downward load gives **hogging** (negative). Get this right or risk losing the entire 7-mark sub-part on diagram interpretation.
> 7. **Incomplete BMD in Stepped Loading Problems:** When a beam has multiple loads, the BMD may have **kinks (cusps)** at concentrated load points. Students often draw a smooth curve through these kinks — **don't**; preserve the angular discontinuity.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- [x] **Bending Moment** is the algebraic sum of moments of forces on either side of a section, about the section.
- [x] **Sign convention:** Sagging = positive (smile 😊), Hogging = negative (frown ☹).
- [x] **SI unit:** N·m (commonly kN·m in civil engineering).
- [x] **First fundamental relation:** $\frac{dV}{dx} = -w$ (slope of SFD = negative of load intensity).
- [x] **Second fundamental relation:** $\frac{dM}{dx} = V$ (slope of BMD = shear force value).
- [x] **Third fundamental relation:** $\frac{d^2 M}{dx^2} = -w$ (curvature of BMD relates to load).
- [x] **Maximum BM** occurs where **shear force = 0** (or changes sign).
- [x] **Point of contraflexure:** Section where $M = 0$ AND changes sign — locate by setting $M_x = 0$.
- [x] **SSB with central point load $P$:** $M_{max} = \frac{PL}{4}$.
- [x] **SSB with UDL $w$:** $M_{max} = \frac{wL^2}{8}$ at midspan.
- [x] **SSB with point load at distance $a$ from A, $b$ from B:** $M_{max} = \frac{Pab}{L}$.
- [x] **Cantilever with point load $P$ at free end:** $M_{max} = -PL$ at fixed end (hogging).
- [x] **Cantilever with UDL $w$:** $M_{max} = -\frac{wL^2}{2}$ at fixed end.
- [x] **SSB with UVL** (zero at one end, $w_0$ at other): $M_{max} = \frac{w_0 L^2}{9\sqrt{3}}$ at $x = L/\sqrt{3}$.
- [x] **At a concentrated load:** SFD has a vertical jump; BMD has a kink (slope discontinuity).
- [x] **At a concentrated moment:** BMD has a vertical jump; SFD is unaffected.
- [x] **Parabolic BMD** for UDL; **Triangular BMD** for point load; **Cubic BMD** for UVL.
- [x] **Engineering utility:** BM governs design of cross-section, reinforcement, and material selection in beams, columns, shafts, and structural members.
- [x] **Always verify** $\sum F_y = 0$ and $\sum M = 0$ for reactions before drawing SFD and BMD.

---

**End of Module 4 | Bending Moment Notes**

> [!NOTE]
> These notes are designed for **KTU 2024 Scheme** B.Tech Civil Engineering examinations. For comprehensive coverage, cross-reference with **PCCET303 - Structural Analysis - I** module-wise university question papers and the prescribed textbook (verify your course handout for the current edition, as per KTU anti-fabrication policy).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Normal Thrust and Radial Shear. Moving Loads and Influence Lines Introduction to Moving Loads - Concept of Influence Lines - Influence Lines for Reaction
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCET303 - STRUCTURAL ANALYSIS - I
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Normal Thrust and Radial Shear. Moving Loads and Influence Lines Introduction to Moving Loads - Concept of Influence Lines - Influence Lines for Reaction

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Normal Thrust and Radial Shear (in Three-Hinged Arches)

### Formal Definition
When a **three-hinged circular arch** is loaded, every cross-section experiences three internal force components: **Bending Moment (B.M.)**, **Normal Thrust (N)**, and **Radial Shear (V<sub>r</sub>)**. The **Normal Thrust (N)** acts *tangentially* to the arch axis (i.e., along the centerline) and represents the compressive axial force at the section. The **Radial Shear (V<sub>r</sub>)** acts *perpendicular* to the arch axis (i.e., along the radial direction) and represents the shearing force component resolved in the radial direction.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** These are the two **non-beam-type** internal forces that uniquely characterise curved members. They replace the conventional "axial force" and "vertical shear" of a straight beam.

### Conceptual Analogy
Imagine bending a flexible plastic ruler (straight beam) versus a curved plastic toy track (arch). On a **straight ruler**, the internal forces are easy: push-pull along the ruler (axial) and sliding along the cross-section (shear). But on a **curved track**, the "axial direction" itself keeps rotating as you walk along the arch! So engineers resolve the actual internal force vector into two pieces:
- One piece **along the tangent to the curve** → that's the **Normal Thrust N** (compressing the arch like a stack of curved blocks).
- One piece **perpendicular to the tangent** → that's the **Radial Shear V<sub>r</sub>** (trying to slide the blocks past each other radially).

> [!NOTE]
> **Sign Convention (KTU Standard):** Normal Thrust is taken as **positive when compressive** (arches are mostly in compression). Radial Shear is positive when it acts **inwards toward the center** of the arch on the **left half**, and **outwards on the right half** (or as per the convention stated in the problem).

---

## 1.2 Moving Loads and Influence Lines

### Formal Definition
A **Moving Load** is a load whose **point of application varies** along the span of a structure (e.g., a train crossing a bridge, a truck on a highway bridge, a crane on a gantry girder). Because the load position changes, the response of the structure (reaction, shear, moment, deflection) also changes.

An **Influence Line** is a graph (a line diagram) that shows the variation of a **particular response function** (such as a reaction, shear force, or bending moment at a specific section) as a **unit concentrated load (1 kN)** moves across the entire span of the structure.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Distinction from Bending Moment Diagram (BMD):**
> - A **B.M. Diagram (BMD)** shows how BM varies *along the span* for a **fixed** set of loads.
> - An **Influence Line Diagram (ILD)** shows how a *specific* response function varies as a **unit load moves** along the span.
> 
> BMD → "Where is the BM maximum?"
> ILD → "At what position of the unit load does the reaction/shear/BM at a particular section become maximum?"

### Conceptual Analogy
Imagine a **see-saw (simply supported beam)** with a single pivot. Place a 1 kg weight at different positions along the see-saw. The upward push required at the left support keeps changing. If you plot the left support reaction (y-axis) against the load position (x-axis), you get a **straight line going from 1 at the left end to 0 at the right end** — that straight line is the **Influence Line for Reaction R<sub>A</sub>**. It's like a "sensitivity graph" — it tells you **how sensitive** R<sub>A</sub> is to the position of the moving load.

> [!NOTE]
> **Use of Influence Lines (KTU Favourite Question):**
> 1. To find the **absolute maximum** value of a response function under a system of moving loads.
> 2. To find the **maximum response** at a particular section under a system of moving loads.
> 3. To determine the **most unfavourable (critical) position** of a live load.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Normal Thrust and Radial Shear — Theoretical Background

At any section XX of a three-hinged arch, inclined at angle **θ** to the horizontal:

1. The **Vertical Shear (V)** at the section is the algebraic sum of the vertical forces on one side of the section.
   - Taking the **left side** of the section: $V = R_A - \text{(sum of downward vertical loads on left)} - H \tan\theta$ for inclined loads, simplified as $V = R_A$ for vertical loads on left.

2. The **Horizontal Thrust (H)** at the crown is constant throughout (for vertical loads on a three-hinged arch).
   - $H = \dfrac{W \cdot a(L-a)}{2Lh}$ for a single point load W at distance a from the left support.

3. The total internal force **F** at the section can be resolved into two components:
   - **Along the tangent** (along the arch axis) → **Normal Thrust N**
   - **Along the normal (radial direction)** → **Radial Shear V<sub>r</sub>**

### Resolution Logic:
- The total force vector at section has a vertical component = V (vertical shear) and a horizontal component = H (horizontal thrust).
- The **tangent** to the arch axis at that section makes angle **θ** with the horizontal.
- Therefore, the component of (V, H) **along the tangent direction** gives N, and **along the radial direction** gives V<sub>r</sub>.

### KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Sign Convention / Unit |
|---|----------|---------|------------------------|
| 1 | Bending Moment at section XX in 3-hinged arch | $M = M_0 - H \cdot y$ | kN·m (positive sagging) |
| 2 | Vertical Shear at section XX | $V = R_A - (\text{loads on left})$ | kN (positive downward on left) |
| 3 | Horizontal Thrust at crown (single pt load W at distance a) | $H = \dfrac{W \cdot a(L-a)}{2Lh}$ | kN (compressive) |
| 4 | Normal Thrust (N) | $N = H\cos\theta + V\sin\theta$ | kN (positive = compressive) |
| 5 | Radial Shear (V<sub>r</sub>) | $V_r = V\cos\theta - H\sin\theta$ | kN |
| 6 | Angle θ for circular arch | $\sin\theta = \dfrac{x}{R},\ \cos\theta = \dfrac{y}{R}$ where x = horizontal distance from crown, y = vertical distance from crown | radians/degrees |
| 7 | Influence Line Ordinate for R<sub>A</sub> (SSB) at distance x from A | $\dfrac{L - x}{L}$ | dimensionless |
| 8 | Influence Line Ordinate for R<sub>B</sub> (SSB) at distance x from A | $\dfrac{x}{L}$ | dimensionless |
| 9 | Müller-Breslau Principle | "IL for a function = deflected shape of the beam when the corresponding constraint is removed and a unit displacement is applied in the direction of the function." | Conceptual |
| 10 | Maximum Reaction (SSB) for system of moving loads | $R_{A,\max} = \sum P_i \cdot \eta_i$ when the **heaviest load is at A and the resultant of loads + A is as close to A as possible** | kN |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:**
> - **Arches** (bridges, dams, auditoriums): Normal Thrust and Radial Shear dictate the **section sizing** and the **reinforcement pattern** in concrete arches.
> - **Influence Lines** are the **backbone of bridge design** — every railway/highway bridge is designed for the worst-case position of the moving vehicle using influence lines.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Normal Thrust and Radial Shear

### Setup
Consider a **three-hinged circular arch** ABC with supports A and B, and an internal hinge at C (the crown). Let a section XX be taken at a point on the arch at horizontal distance $x$ from the left support A, and at angle $\theta$ with the horizontal at that section. The arch has span $L$ and rise $h$. Radius of circular arch: $R = \dfrac{L^2 + 4h^2}{8h}$.

Let the load on the left of section XX produce:
- A vertical reaction at A: $R_A$ (upward)
- A horizontal thrust: $H$ (inward at the crown, i.e., the support A is pushed outward by the load; the arch pushes back inward with H).

The **resultant internal force** at section XX has:
- **Vertical component** = $V$ (the vertical shear at that section)
- **Horizontal component** = $H$ (the horizontal thrust — same everywhere for a 3-hinged arch under vertical loads)

### Step 1: Identify the Tangent Direction
At section XX, the arch axis makes angle $\theta$ with the horizontal. The **tangent** at XX is inclined at $\theta$. The **normal (radial)** direction is perpendicular to the tangent, i.e., at angle $\theta$ to the vertical.

### Step 2: Resolve Forces Along the Tangent (gives Normal Thrust N)
The resultant internal force F has components $H$ (horizontal) and $V$ (vertical). To get the component along the **tangent** (which itself is at angle $\theta$ above horizontal):

$$N = H \cos\theta + V \sin\theta$$

**Derivation Logic:** If the tangent makes angle $\theta$ with horizontal, then the horizontal force $H$ contributes $H \cos\theta$ along the tangent, and the vertical force $V$ contributes $V \sin\theta$ along the tangent. Both add because the resultant internal force vector lies in the first quadrant of the local tangent-normal coordinate.

### Step 3: Resolve Forces Along the Normal/Radial Direction (gives Radial Shear V<sub>r</sub>)
The component perpendicular to the tangent (along the radial inward direction):

$$V_r = V \cos\theta - H \sin\theta$$

**Derivation Logic:** The vertical component $V$ projects onto the radial direction as $V \cos\theta$. The horizontal component $H$ projects onto the radial direction as $-H \sin\theta$ (negative because $H$ acts outward-horizontal while the radial inward direction is opposite to the horizontal at this section angle).

### Step 4: Summary Equations (Master KTU Formulas)
The **two key equations** that KTU expects you to memorize and apply:

$$N = H\cos\theta + V\sin\theta$$

$$V_r = V\cos\theta - H\sin\theta$$

---

## 3.2 Influence Line for Reactions of a Simply Supported Beam

### Setup
A simply supported beam AB of span $L$. Reactions are $R_A$ (at A) and $R_B$ (at B). A unit load (1 kN) is placed at distance $x$ from A. We need to find $R_A$ and $R_B$ as functions of $x$.

### Step 1: Apply Equilibrium
Taking moments about B (considering the unit load at distance $x$ from A, i.e., distance $L-x$ from B):

$$R_A \cdot L - 1 \cdot (L - x) = 0$$

$$R_A = \frac{L - x}{L}$$

### Step 2: Sum of Vertical Forces
$$R_A + R_B = 1$$

$$R_B = 1 - R_A = 1 - \frac{L - x}{L} = \frac{x}{L}$$

### Step 3: Identify IL Ordinates
| Position of Unit Load ($x$) | $R_A$ ordinate | $R_B$ ordinate |
|---|---|---|
| $x = 0$ (at A) | $\dfrac{L-0}{L} = 1$ | $\dfrac{0}{L} = 0$ |
| $x = L/2$ (midspan) | $\dfrac{L/2}{L} = 0.5$ | $\dfrac{L/2}{L} = 0.5$ |
| $x = L$ (at B) | $\dfrac{L-L}{L} = 0$ | $\dfrac{L}{L} = 1$ |

### Step 4: Plot the Influence Lines
- **ILD for R<sub>A</sub>**: Straight line from ordinate **1 at A** to ordinate **0 at B** (linearly decreasing).
- **ILD for R<sub>B</sub>**: Straight line from ordinate **0 at A** to ordinate **1 at B** (linearly increasing).

### Step 5: Maximum Value of Reaction Under a System of Loads
For a system of moving point loads (e.g., a train of wheel loads $P_1, P_2, \ldots, P_n$ with fixed spacing), the **maximum value of a reaction** is obtained by:
1. Drawing the ILD for the reaction.
2. Placing the system of loads on the span.
3. Computing the reaction as: $R = P_1 \eta_1 + P_2 \eta_2 + \ldots$ where $\eta_i$ is the ILD ordinate under load $P_i$.

**Critical (most unfavourable) position for R<sub>A,max</sub>**: The **heaviest load is placed at A** (where $\eta_A = 1$, the maximum), and the **resultant of all loads on the span lies as close to A as possible** (so that all loads contribute positive ordinates).

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Three-Hinged Arch Internal Forces

For a **three-hinged arch** under a single point load $W$ at horizontal distance $a$ from A:

| Step | Quantity | Expression | Notes |
|------|----------|------------|-------|
| 1 | Vertical reaction at A | $R_A = \dfrac{W(L-a)}{L}$ | Standard SSB formula |
| 2 | Vertical reaction at B | $R_B = \dfrac{Wa}{L}$ | Standard SSB formula |
| 3 | Horizontal thrust (from M<sub>C</sub> = 0) | $H = \dfrac{Wa(L-a)}{2Lh}$ | Found by setting moment at crown C = 0 |
| 4 | Bending moment at section $x$ (left of load) | $M_x = R_A \cdot x - H \cdot y$ | Where $y$ is the height of the arch axis above the support at that $x$ |
| 5 | Vertical shear at section $x$ (left of load) | $V_x = R_A$ | For a single load on the right of section |
| 6 | Angle at section | $\tan\theta = \dfrac{dy}{dx}$ | For circular arch: $y = \sqrt{R^2 - (L/2 - x)^2} - (R - h)$ |
| 7 | Normal thrust | $N = H\cos\theta + V\sin\theta$ | Substitute values |
| 8 | Radial shear | $V_r = V\cos\theta - H\sin\theta$ | Substitute values |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Three-Hinged Arch — Internal Force Resolution Diagram (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Three-Hinged Arch ABC with Crown C] --> B[Take Section XX at angle theta]
    B --> C[Identify Force Components at Section]
    C --> D[Vertical Shear V from left side]
    C --> E[Horizontal Thrust H constant]
    D --> F[Resolve Along Tangent]
    E --> F
    F --> G[Normal Thrust N equals H cos theta + V sin theta]
    D --> H[Resolve Along Radial Direction]
    E --> H
    H --> I[Radial Shear Vr equals V cos theta - H sin theta]
    G --> J[Design Cross Section for Compression N]
    I --> K[Design Cross Section for Shear Vr]
    J --> L[Final Arch Design Ready]
    K --> L
```

## 4.2 Influence Line Construction Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Step 1: Place Unit Load 1 kN at distance x] --> P2[Step 2: Apply Equilibrium Equations]
    P2 --> P3[Step 3: Solve for Response Function]
    P3 --> P4[Step 4: Vary x from 0 to L]
    P4 --> P5[Step 5: Plot Function vs x]
    P5 --> P6[Influence Line Diagram ILD Complete]
    P6 --> P7[Apply Actual Moving Loads]
    P7 --> P8[Compute Response equals Sum Pi times eta i]
    P8 --> P9[Identify Critical Load Position]
    P9 --> P10[Maximum Response Obtained]
```

## 4.3 Influence Line for Simply Supported Beam Reactions (Schematic)

```
  Reaction RA  (ILD)
  1.0 |*
      |  *
  0.5 |    *
      |      *
  0.0 |________*___________ Distance x along beam
      A        x=L/2        B
      0        L/2          L

  Reaction RB  (ILD)
  1.0 |              *
      |          *
  0.5 |      *
      |  *
  0.0 |*___________________ Distance x along beam
      A        x=L/2        B
      0        L/2          L
```

**Reading the ILDs:**
- For R<sub>A</sub>: At any position of the unit load, the ordinate directly below the load gives the value of R<sub>A</sub>. Maximum R<sub>A</sub> = 1.0 occurs when unit load is at A.
- For R<sub>B</sub>: Maximum R<sub>B</sub> = 1.0 occurs when unit load is at B.

## 4.4 Block-Level Architecture: Why These Two Topics Are Linked in KTU Module 4

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Module 4: Arches and Moving Loads] --> B[Arches Three-Hinged]
    A --> C[Influence Lines for Static Response]
    B --> D[Internal Forces N and Vr]
    C --> E[IL for Reaction Shear Moment]
    D --> F[Design of Arch Bridge]
    E --> G[Design for Moving Train Vehicles]
    F --> H[Complete Bridge Engineering]
    G --> H
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**
**Define Normal Thrust and Radial Shear in a three-hinged arch. How are they determined at any section?**

**Model Answer:**
**Normal Thrust (N):** It is the component of the resultant internal force at a section of an arch, acting **tangentially (along the arch axis)**. It is essentially the axial compressive force at that section.

**Radial Shear (V<sub>r</sub>):** It is the component of the resultant internal force at a section of an arch, acting **perpendicular to the arch axis (along the radial direction)**. It represents the shearing force in the radial sense.

**Determination at any section XX:** Let the section be inclined at angle $\theta$ to the horizontal, and let $V$ be the vertical shear and $H$ the horizontal thrust at the section. Then:
- $N = H \cos\theta + V \sin\theta$
- $V_r = V \cos\theta - H \sin\theta$

These are obtained by resolving the internal force vector (components $H$ horizontal, $V$ vertical) along the tangent and normal directions at the section.

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
**Define an Influence Line. How is it different from a Bending Moment Diagram?**

**Model Answer:**
**Influence Line:** An influence line is a diagram showing the variation of a particular response function (such as a reaction, shear force, or bending moment at a specific section) of a structure as a **unit concentrated load (1 kN)** moves across the span.

**Difference from BMD:**

| Bending Moment Diagram (BMD) | Influence Line Diagram (ILD) |
|------------------------------|------------------------------|
| Shows variation of BM along the span for a **fixed** set of loads | Shows variation of a particular response function as a **unit load moves** |
| Load is fixed; section varies | Load position varies; section/function is fixed |
| Used to find maximum BM location under given loads | Used to find the position of moving loads for maximum response |

---

## Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question A]**

A three-hinged circular arch has a span of **30 m** and a rise of **6 m**. A point load of **120 kN** acts at the **crown (center)**. Find the normal thrust and radial shear at a section **8 m horizontally from the left support**.

**(a)** Determine the reactions at the supports and the horizontal thrust. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Calculate the bending moment, normal thrust, and radial shear at the section 8 m from the left support. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Given Data:**
- Span $L = 30$ m
- Rise $h = 6$ m
- Point load $W = 120$ kN at crown (center, i.e., at $x = 15$ m)
- Section: 8 m from left support A, so $x = 8$ m

**Radius of circular arch:**
$$R = \frac{L^2 + 4h^2}{8h} = \frac{30^2 + 4 \times 6^2}{8 \times 6} = \frac{900 + 144}{48} = \frac{1044}{48} = 21.75 \text{ m}$$

**Height of arch axis above supports at $x = 8$ m:**

Taking the left support A as origin, the equation of the circular arch (with center directly above the midspan at distance $R - h$ from supports) is:
$$y = \sqrt{R^2 - \left(\frac{L}{2} - x\right)^2} - (R - h)$$

At $x = 8$ m:
$$y = \sqrt{(21.75)^2 - (15 - 8)^2} - (21.75 - 6)$$
$$y = \sqrt{473.0625 - 49} - 15.75$$
$$y = \sqrt{424.0625} - 15.75$$
$$y = 20.5918 - 15.75 = 4.8418 \text{ m}$$

**Angle $\theta$ at the section:**
$$\cos\theta = \frac{x'}{R} = \frac{15 - 8}{21.75} = \frac{7}{21.75} = 0.3218$$
$$\theta = \cos^{-1}(0.3218) = 71.225°$$
$$\sin\theta = 0.9468, \quad \cos\theta = 0.3218$$

**(a) Reactions and Horizontal Thrust**

**Vertical reactions** (by symmetry of load position at center):
$$R_A = R_B = \frac{W}{2} = \frac{120}{2} = 60 \text{ kN}$$

**Horizontal thrust** (using $M_C = 0$ at crown, taking moments of left side forces about crown):
The crown is at midspan, height $h = 6$ m above supports. The left half of the arch is subjected to $R_A = 60$ kN upward at A, $H$ horizontal at A (inward), and the half of the load (60 kN) at the crown? No — for the formula approach, since the load is exactly at the crown, the load is shared.

For a single point load $W$ at distance $a$ from the left support, the horizontal thrust is:
$$H = \frac{W \cdot a (L - a)}{2 L h}$$

With $a = 15$ m (load at center):
$$H = \frac{120 \times 15 \times 15}{2 \times 30 \times 6} = \frac{27000}{360} = 75 \text{ kN}$$

**(b) Internal Forces at Section $x = 8$ m**

**Bending Moment at section** (left of load since $x = 8 < 15$):
$$M = R_A \cdot x - H \cdot y = 60 \times 8 - 75 \times 4.8418$$
$$M = 480 - 363.135 = 116.865 \text{ kN·m}$$

**Vertical Shear at section** (no loads between A and section since load is at center, which is to the right):
$$V = R_A = 60 \text{ kN}$$

**Normal Thrust:**
$$N = H \cos\theta + V \sin\theta = 75 \times 0.3218 + 60 \times 0.9468$$
$$N = 24.135 + 56.808 = 80.943 \text{ kN (compressive)}$$

**Radial Shear:**
$$V_r = V \cos\theta - H \sin\theta = 60 \times 0.3218 - 75 \times 0.9468$$
$$V_r = 19.308 - 71.010 = -51.702 \text{ kN}$$

The negative sign indicates the radial shear acts in the opposite sense (outward instead of inward) at this section. **Magnitude = 51.702 kN.**

---

### Question B (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question B]**

A simply supported beam has a span of **20 m**. Two point loads of **80 kN and 60 kN** spaced **3 m apart** (with 80 kN leading) move across the beam from left to right.

**(a)** Draw the influence lines for the reactions $R_A$ and $R_B$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Find the **maximum reaction at A** ($R_{A,\max}$) and the corresponding position of the loads. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Given Data:**
- Span $L = 20$ m
- Load system: $P_1 = 80$ kN, $P_2 = 60$ kN, spacing = 3 m (with 80 kN leading from the left)

**(a) Influence Lines for Reactions**

The ILD for $R_A$ is a straight line with ordinate **1 at A** and **0 at B**. Similarly, ILD for $R_B$ is a straight line with ordinate **0 at A** and **1 at B**.

**ILD for $R_A$ (linear equation):**
$$R_A = \frac{L - x}{L} = \frac{20 - x}{20}$$

| Position | Ordinate for $R_A$ |
|----------|---------------------|
| $x = 0$ (A) | $1.0$ |
| $x = 5$ | $0.75$ |
| $x = 10$ (midspan) | $0.5$ |
| $x = 15$ | $0.25$ |
| $x = 20$ (B) | $0.0$ |

**ILD for $R_B$:**
$$R_B = \frac{x}{20}$$

| Position | Ordinate for $R_B$ |
|----------|---------------------|
| $x = 0$ (A) | $0.0$ |
| $x = 10$ (midspan) | $0.5$ |
| $x = 20$ (B) | $1.0$ |

```
  RA ILD
  1.0 |*___________________
      |  *               
  0.5 |     *            
      |        *         
  0.0 |___________*______ 
      A         10m        B
      0         10         20
```

**(b) Maximum Reaction at A ($R_{A,\max}$)**

**Step 1: Apply the critical position rule.**
For $R_{A,\max}$, the **80 kN load (heavier) is placed at A** (where ordinate = 1). The 60 kN load will then be at $x = 3$ m from A.

**Step 2: Compute ordinates under each load.**
- Ordinate under 80 kN (at $x = 0$): $\eta_1 = 1.0$
- Ordinate under 60 kN (at $x = 3$): $\eta_2 = \dfrac{20 - 3}{20} = \dfrac{17}{20} = 0.85$

**Step 3: Calculate $R_{A,\max}$.**
$$R_{A,\max} = P_1 \eta_1 + P_2 \eta_2 = 80 \times 1.0 + 60 \times 0.85$$
$$R_{A,\max} = 80 + 51 = 131 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 4: Verify the critical position using the resultant criterion.**
- Sum of loads: $W = 80 + 60 = 140$ kN
- Position of resultant from 80 kN (which is at A in critical case): $x_R = \dfrac{60 \times 3}{140} = \dfrac{180}{140} = 1.286$ m
- The resultant is **1.286 m to the right of A**, which is **less than half the span** (10 m). 
- Also, the resultant and A are on the **same side of midspan** (both on the left half). 
- The condition for $R_{A,\max}$ is satisfied: the resultant should be as close to A as possible, which is what happens when the 80 kN load is at A.

**Final Answer: $R_{A,\max} = 131$ kN.**

---

### Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
> 1. **Sign of Normal Thrust:** Many students forget that $N$ is **compressive positive** in arch problems. A wrong sign here can flip the entire numerical answer.
> 2. **Angle $\theta$ identification:** For a circular arch, $\cos\theta = x'/R$ where $x'$ is the horizontal distance from the **center of the arch (i.e., from the crown projection)**, not from the support. Getting this wrong is the #1 mistake.
> 3. **Equation of circular arch ($y$):** Use $y = \sqrt{R^2 - (L/2 - x)^2} - (R - h)$, not $y = \sqrt{R^2 - x^2}$ — the origin is at the support A, not at the center of the circle.
> 4. **Influence Line ordinate placement:** When loads move from **left to right**, the **leading load is on the right** of the trailing load. Place the heavier load (or its position) such that the ILD area covered is maximum, then compute $R = \sum P_i \eta_i$.
> 5. **Forgetting to check the critical position:** The "heaviest load at A" rule works for $R_{A,\max}$, but the position must be checked using the **resultant criterion** to get full marks.
> 6. **Units:** Always carry units (kN, kN·m) in the final answer and intermediate steps.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 4 (Part 1):**
> 
> **Normal Thrust and Radial Shear:**
> - [ ] Three-hinged arch is statically determinate (3 equilibrium equations + 1 condition $M_C = 0$).
> - [ ] For vertical loads, horizontal thrust $H$ is **constant throughout** the arch.
> - [ ] $H$ formula for a single point load: $H = \dfrac{W a (L-a)}{2 L h}$.
> - [ ] $M$ at any section: $M = M_0 - H y$ (where $M_0$ is BM of simply supported beam).
> - [ ] Master equations: $N = H \cos\theta + V \sin\theta$ and $V_r = V \cos\theta - H \sin\theta$.
> - [ ] $\theta$ is the angle the arch tangent makes with the horizontal.
> - [ ] For circular arch: $R = \dfrac{L^2 + 4h^2}{8h}$, $y = \sqrt{R^2 - (L/2 - x)^2} - (R - h)$.
> - [ ] $N$ is the design-driving force (arches are mainly in compression).
> 
> **Influence Lines — Introduction:**
> - [ ] ILD = plot of a response function vs position of a **unit** (1 kN) load.
> - [ ] ILD ≠ BMD: ILD has a **moving unit load**, BMD has **fixed loads**.
> - [ ] ILD for $R_A$ in SSB: straight line from 1 at A to 0 at B.
> - [ ] ILD for $R_B$ in SSB: straight line from 0 at A to 1 at B.
> - [ ] Müller-Breslau Principle: "ILD for a function = deflected shape when the corresponding constraint is released and a unit displacement is applied."
> - [ ] Maximum response under a system of loads: $R = \sum P_i \eta_i$.
> - [ ] Critical load position rule for $R_{A,\max}$: heaviest load at A, resultant as close to A as possible.
